questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: What is Company Law? The Nature of a Company
questions.text: Explain the essential characteristics of a registered company under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, discuss the concept of separate legal personality, limited liability, perpetual succession, and the company's capacity to hold property and sue in its own name, and illustrate each characteristic with reference to the relevant statutory provisions and case law.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: What is Company Law? The Nature of a Company
questions.text: Critically assess the significance of the Companies Act 2014 as a consolidating statute in Irish company law. In your answer, explain the main structural changes introduced by the Act — including the introduction of the LTD as the default private company type — and assess whether the 2014 Act succeeded in simplifying and modernising Irish company law.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: What is Company Law? The Nature of a Company
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of separate legal personality and its foundational importance in company law. In your answer, discuss the decision in Salomon v A Salomon & Co Ltd [1897] AC 22, explain the principle that a company is distinct from its members, and consider the consequences of this principle for shareholders, creditors, and third parties.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Identify and explain the principal advantages of incorporation as a vehicle for conducting business in Ireland. In your answer, consider limited liability, separate legal personality, ease of raising capital, transferability of shares, and tax efficiency, and explain how each advantage benefits investors and business operators.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Identify and explain the principal disadvantages of incorporation. In your answer, discuss the compliance burden under the Companies Act 2014, the disclosure requirements, the costs of formation and maintenance, and the obligations imposed on directors — and assess whether the advantages of incorporation typically outweigh these disadvantages.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Compare and contrast the company as a vehicle for business with a partnership and a sole trader arrangement under Irish law. In your answer, consider liability, tax treatment, governance, cost of formation, and the ability to raise capital, and assess which structure is most appropriate for a start-up with three equal founders who wish to limit their personal liability.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the effect of s.31 of the Companies Act 2014, which provides that the constitution of a company binds the company and its members to the same extent as if it were a contract signed and sealed by each member. In your answer, explain the contractual rights created by the constitution, identify who may sue upon them, and discuss the limitations of the s.31 contract.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Discuss the extent to which a member can enforce the terms of a company's constitution against the company under s.31 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the principle that a member can only enforce the constitution in their capacity as a member (not in any other capacity such as solicitor or employee), and critically assess this limitation with reference to Eley v Positive Government Security Life Assurance Co Ltd (1876) and subsequent Irish case law.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain how the constitution of a company may be altered under the Companies Act 2014 and the limits on the power of a majority to alter the constitution. In your answer, discuss the special resolution requirement, the rule against fraud on the minority, and the ability of a court to restrain an alteration that is oppressive to minority shareholders.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the key features of a private company limited by shares (LTD) under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the single-document constitution, the absence of an objects clause, the minimum director requirement, and how an LTD differs from the old-style private company limited by shares under the Companies Acts 1963–2013.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the key features of a Designated Activity Company (DAC) under Part 16 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain why a DAC requires a two-document constitution (memorandum and articles), the requirement for an objects clause, and the circumstances in which a company would choose to incorporate as a DAC rather than an LTD.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Compare and contrast the LTD and the DAC under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, identify the key differences in constitution, capacity, governance, and practical usage, and advise a client who is establishing a subsidiary company for a specific purpose (asset holding) which vehicle is more appropriate and why.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the Salomon principle as established in Salomon v A Salomon & Co Ltd [1897] AC 22 and its continued importance in Irish company law. In your answer, explain the facts and decision in Salomon, explain the key aspects of the principle, and discuss how it has been applied and affirmed in subsequent Irish case law.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Discuss the significance of the separate legal personality of a company for creditors in an insolvency. In your answer, explain how the Salomon principle limits the ability of creditors to recover from shareholders personally, and consider how the law has developed mechanisms (e.g., restriction, disqualification, fraudulent trading, and reckless trading) to protect creditors despite the veil of incorporation.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Critically assess whether the rule in Salomon v Salomon remains appropriate in the modern commercial context. In your answer, consider arguments in favour of maintaining strict separate legal personality (certainty, investment incentive), arguments for greater accountability (abuse of corporate form, undercapitalisation), and whether the existing statutory and equitable exceptions provide adequate protection for creditors.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Disregarding or Lifting the Corporate Veil
questions.text: Explain the circumstances in which the courts will disregard the corporate veil (lift the veil of incorporation) in Irish law. In your answer, identify and explain the principal statutory grounds (including fraudulent trading under s.722 and reckless trading under s.610 of the Companies Act 2014) and the main common law grounds (including agency, sham/façade, and the single economic unit argument), illustrating each with Irish case law.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Disregarding or Lifting the Corporate Veil
questions.text: Critically examine the common law grounds for lifting the corporate veil in Irish company law. In your answer, consider the 'sham or façade' ground developed in Jones v Lipman [1962], the agency ground, and the 'group as a single economic unit' approach, and assess whether the Irish courts have been consistent in their application of these doctrines.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Disregarding or Lifting the Corporate Veil
questions.text: Discuss the statutory grounds for imposing personal liability on directors under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the offences of fraudulent trading (s.722) and reckless trading (s.610), explain the civil liability that may arise from each, and assess whether these provisions adequately deter directors from engaging in these behaviours.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Section 158 — Delegation of Management Powers to Directors
questions.text: Explain s.158 of the Companies Act 2014, which vests management powers of an LTD in the board of directors. In your answer, explain the scope of these powers, the extent to which directors may delegate to individual directors or officers, and the residual role of the general meeting in an LTD under the 2014 Act.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Section 158 — Delegation of Management Powers to Directors
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between the board of directors and the general meeting in an LTD under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain how s.158 differs from the position under the old Table A (where a general meeting could give binding directions to the board in some circumstances), and assess the extent to which shareholders retain ultimate control over management decisions.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Section 158 — Delegation of Management Powers to Directors
questions.text: Explain the role of the chairperson and the chief executive officer (CEO) in the governance of an Irish private company. In your answer, discuss how powers are delegated by the board to executive officers, the legal basis for such delegation under the Companies Act 2014, and the extent to which such delegation affects the board's collective responsibility.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Corporate Capacity and Authority — The Indoor Management Rule
questions.text: Explain the concept of corporate capacity and actual authority in Irish company law. In your answer, explain how an LTD under the Companies Act 2014 has full and unlimited capacity (s.38), explain the concept of actual authority (express and implied), and discuss how a third party can rely on the actual authority of an officer of the company.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Corporate Capacity and Authority — The Indoor Management Rule
questions.text: Explain the Indoor Management Rule (the rule in Royal British Bank v Turquand (1856)) and its application under Irish law, including the modifications introduced by s.40 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, set out the original rule, the statutory formulation, and the limits on a third party's ability to rely on apparent authority.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Corporate Capacity and Authority — The Indoor Management Rule
questions.text: Discuss the protection of third parties dealing with companies under ss.38–40 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the extent to which third parties are bound by the constitutional objects of a DAC, explain the 'good faith' requirement under s.40, and assess whether the 2014 Act strikes the right balance between protecting third parties and holding companies to their constitutional limits.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fixed and Floating Charges — Definitions, Registration, and Priority
questions.text: Explain the distinction between a fixed charge and a floating charge over company assets in Irish law. In your answer, define each type of charge, explain the hallmarks of a floating charge as established in Re Yorkshire Woolcombers Association Ltd [1903], and discuss the practical significance of the distinction for a lender.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fixed and Floating Charges — Definitions, Registration, and Priority
questions.text: Explain the registration requirements for charges over company assets under Part 7 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the obligation to register a charge within 21 days of creation, the consequences of late or non-registration, and the effect of registration on priority as between competing security holders.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fixed and Floating Charges — Definitions, Registration, and Priority
questions.text: Discuss the rules on priority between fixed and floating charges and between competing fixed charges in Irish company law. In your answer, explain the general rule that the first to register takes priority, the exception for the prior equitable interest (notice), and the special rule that a fixed charge ranks ahead of a floating charge over the same assets regardless of the order of creation.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fixed versus Floating Charges and Crystallisation
questions.text: Explain the process of crystallisation of a floating charge. In your answer, define crystallisation, identify the events that cause automatic crystallisation (including winding up, appointment of receiver, and cessation of business), explain automatic crystallisation clauses, and explain the effect of crystallisation on the priority of the charge.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fixed versus Floating Charges and Crystallisation
questions.text: Critically examine the significance of the distinction between a fixed and floating charge following the House of Lords' decision in Re Spectrum Plus Ltd [2005]. In your answer, explain why the characterisation of a charge as fixed or floating is important, particularly in relation to the priority of preferential creditors on insolvency, and discuss how the Irish courts have applied the Spectrum Plus principles.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fixed versus Floating Charges and Crystallisation
questions.text: Discuss the concept of a 'negative pledge clause' in the context of floating charges. In your answer, explain what a negative pledge clause does, how it is used by lenders to prevent the company from granting further security over the charged assets, and consider the extent to which a negative pledge clause binds a third party who has actual or constructive notice of it.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain what is meant by a retention of title (Romalpa) clause and the legal basis on which such clauses operate. In your answer, explain the decision in Aluminium Industrie Vaassen BV v Romalpa Aluminium Ltd [1976], and discuss the types of retention of title clause (simple reservation of title, all moneys clause, and proceeds clause) and how each operates.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Discuss the effectiveness of retention of title clauses in Irish law, including the distinction between simple reservation of title and mixed or proceeds clauses. In your answer, explain the conditions for a simple ROT clause to be effective, the difficulties encountered with mixed goods and proceeds clauses, and the extent to which such clauses must be registered as charges under the Companies Act 2014.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Critically assess the practical limitations of retention of title clauses as a form of security for an unsecured trade creditor. In your answer, consider the difficulties in tracing goods once they have been processed or mixed with other goods, the burden of identifying and recovering goods, and whether ROT clauses provide effective protection against the risk of a buyer's insolvency.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Types of Directors and The Duty to Act in Good Faith
questions.text: Explain the different types of directors recognised under Irish company law — executive, non-executive, shadow, and de facto directors — and the legal significance of each classification. In your answer, explain how the Companies Act 2014 defines a director, explain the concept of a shadow director under s.221, and discuss the implications of being classified as a de facto or shadow director for the purposes of directors' duties.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Types of Directors and The Duty to Act in Good Faith
questions.text: Explain the fiduciary duty of directors to act in good faith in the best interests of the company under s.228(1)(a) of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, define 'bona fide in the interests of the company', discuss the subjective nature of this test, explain the duty to have regard to the interests of employees and creditors (especially on the verge of insolvency), and illustrate with relevant Irish case law.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Types of Directors and The Duty to Act in Good Faith
questions.text: Discuss the duty of directors not to act for an improper purpose under s.228(1)(b) of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the distinction between the proper purpose doctrine and the good faith duty, discuss how courts have applied this doctrine in the context of the allotment of shares (Howard Smith Ltd v Ampol Petroleum Ltd [1974]), and assess whether a director who genuinely believes they are acting in the company's interests may still breach this duty.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Duty to Avoid Conflicts of Interest and the Duty of Skill, Care, and Diligence
questions.text: Explain the duty of directors to avoid conflicts of interest under s.228(1)(e) and s.228(1)(f) of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the no-conflict rule, the no-profit rule (exploitation of corporate opportunity), the disclosure requirements, and the extent to which shareholder approval may authorise a conflict of interest.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Duty to Avoid Conflicts of Interest and the Duty of Skill, Care, and Diligence
questions.text: Explain the duty of directors to exercise reasonable care, skill, and diligence under s.228(1)(g) of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the objective minimum standard (the reasonable director test), the subjective element (the particular director's knowledge and experience), and discuss how the Irish courts have applied this dual standard in the context of directors' negligence.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Duty to Avoid Conflicts of Interest and the Duty of Skill, Care, and Diligence
questions.text: Critically examine the corporate opportunity doctrine in Irish company law. In your answer, explain the principle that a director must not divert a corporate opportunity to themselves, discuss the leading Irish authority (Industrial Development Consultants Ltd v Cooley [1972] and equivalent Irish cases), and assess whether the doctrine is clear and workable in the context of modern business relationships.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Remedies for Breach of Directors' Duties
questions.text: Explain the civil remedies available where a director has breached their fiduciary duties under Irish company law. In your answer, discuss account of profits, equitable compensation, rescission of the transaction, and injunction, and explain when each remedy is appropriate and how the courts have applied them in practice.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Remedies for Breach of Directors' Duties
questions.text: Discuss the ratification of directors' duties in Irish company law. In your answer, explain the extent to which shareholders can ratify a breach of duty (including a breach of the no-profit and no-conflict rules), explain the limitations on ratification (including fraud on the minority and transactions requiring shareholder approval by special resolution), and assess whether the current law provides an appropriate balance between director accountability and commercial flexibility.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Remedies for Breach of Directors' Duties
questions.text: Explain the liability of directors for fraudulent trading under s.722 and reckless trading under s.610 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the elements of each offence, the standard of conduct required, the persons who may be made liable, and the remedies available to a liquidator who successfully establishes that directors engaged in fraudulent or reckless trading.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Section 238 and Substantial Non-Cash Asset Transactions
questions.text: Explain the requirements of s.238 of the Companies Act 2014 governing substantial non-cash asset transactions between a company and its directors. In your answer, explain the threshold for a 'substantial' transaction, the requirement of prior member approval, and the consequences of entering into a prohibited transaction without approval.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Section 238 and Substantial Non-Cash Asset Transactions
questions.text: Discuss the civil consequences of a contravention of s.238 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the voidability of the transaction, the circumstances in which the court will affirm the transaction notwithstanding the absence of member approval, and who may challenge the transaction and within what timeframe.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Section 238 and Substantial Non-Cash Asset Transactions
questions.text: Explain the rationale for imposing restrictions on substantial non-cash asset transactions between companies and their directors under s.238 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, consider the conflict of interest concern, the risk of self-dealing, and assess whether the requirement of prior shareholder approval is an effective safeguard.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the restrictions on loans and quasi-loans to directors and connected persons under s.239 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain what constitutes a 'loan', a 'quasi-loan', and a 'credit transaction', identify the persons to whom the restrictions apply, and explain the permitted exceptions under the Act.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the civil and criminal consequences of making a loan to a director in contravention of s.239 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the voidability of the loan, the criminal penalties applicable to the company and the directors, and the defences available where the loan was made in ignorance of the contravention.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Critically assess the adequacy of s.239 of the Companies Act 2014 as a safeguard against misappropriation of company funds by directors. In your answer, consider the permitted exceptions, the civil consequences of breach, and assess whether the current regime provides sufficient deterrence against the misuse of company funds by directors who are also shareholders.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: S212 — Oppression and Disregard of Members' Interests
questions.text: Explain the remedy available to a member under s.212 of the Companies Act 2014 where the affairs of a company are being conducted in a manner oppressive to that member or in disregard of their interests as a member. In your answer, explain the meaning of 'oppressive' and 'disregard', discuss who may apply for relief, and identify the range of orders a court may make under s.212.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: S212 — Oppression and Disregard of Members' Interests
questions.text: Discuss how the Irish courts have interpreted the term 'oppression' in the context of s.212 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, consider key decisions including Re Irish Visiting Motorists' Bureau Ltd [1973] and Re Williams Group Tullamore Ltd [1985], and explain what conduct has been found to constitute oppression in practice.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: S212 — Oppression and Disregard of Members' Interests
questions.text: Critically examine the buy-out order as a remedy for oppression under s.212 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain when a court will order a majority shareholder to purchase a minority shareholder's shares, the basis on which shares are valued for this purpose, and assess whether the buy-out order provides an adequate remedy for minority shareholders in a quasi-partnership company.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Rule in Foss v Harbottle and the Derivative Action
questions.text: Explain the rule in Foss v Harbottle (1843) and the two principles it establishes: the proper plaintiff rule and the internal management rule. In your answer, explain the rationale for the rule, its effect on minority shareholders who seek to bring an action on behalf of the company, and discuss the justifications for the rule in the context of the company as a separate legal person.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Rule in Foss v Harbottle and the Derivative Action
questions.text: Explain the exceptions to the rule in Foss v Harbottle that have been recognised in Irish company law, including the fraud on the minority exception. In your answer, set out the conditions that must be satisfied for a shareholder to bring a derivative action on the company's behalf and discuss the relationship between the statutory derivative action under s.236 of the Companies Act 2014 and the common law exceptions.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Rule in Foss v Harbottle and the Derivative Action
questions.text: Explain the statutory derivative action under s.236 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain who may bring such an action, the requirement of court leave, the factors the court must consider in deciding whether to grant leave, and the extent to which the statutory action represents an improvement on the pre-existing common law framework.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Restriction of Directors — Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014
questions.text: Explain the restriction regime under s.819 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the circumstances in which a court must make a restriction order against a director of an insolvent company, the standard of conduct assessed, the effect of a restriction order, and the role of the Office of the Director of Corporate Enforcement (ODCE/CEA) in enforcement.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Restriction of Directors — Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014
questions.text: Discuss the relief from restriction available under s.820 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the grounds on which a director may seek relief, the matters the court must be satisfied of before granting relief, and assess whether the relief provisions undermine the deterrent effect of the restriction regime.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Restriction of Directors — Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014
questions.text: Critically assess the effectiveness of the restriction regime under s.819 as a mechanism for deterring poor directorial behaviour in Ireland. In your answer, consider the mandatory nature of restriction orders, the limited practical consequences of restriction (since a restricted director may still act as a director of a sufficiently capitalised company), and whether restriction serves as an adequate incentive to compliance.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Disqualification of Directors — Section 839 of the Companies Act 2014
questions.text: Explain the disqualification regime under s.839 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the grounds for automatic disqualification (s.839(1)) and discretionary disqualification (s.839(2)), the effect of a disqualification order on a person's ability to act as a director or company secretary, and the criminal consequences of acting in contravention of a disqualification order.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Disqualification of Directors — Section 839 of the Companies Act 2014
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between the restriction regime (s.819) and the disqualification regime (s.839) of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the key differences between restriction and disqualification in terms of grounds, duration, effect, and procedure, and assess which regime provides a more effective sanction against delinquent directors.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Disqualification of Directors — Section 839 of the Companies Act 2014
questions.text: Explain the role of the Corporate Enforcement Authority (formerly the ODCE) in the disqualification of directors in Ireland. In your answer, explain how the CEA may apply for a disqualification order, the grounds on which such an order may be made, and critically assess whether the disqualification regime has been effectively used in practice to deter director misconduct.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the rules governing the payment of dividends by a company under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the requirement that dividends may only be paid out of distributable profits, define 'distributable profits', explain the directors' obligations when recommending a dividend, and discuss the consequences of an unlawful distribution.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Discuss the concept of a 'distribution' within the meaning of Part 7 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain what constitutes a distribution (going beyond the obvious cash dividend), explain the requirement that distributions are made only from distributable profits, and consider how in-kind and non-cash distributions are treated.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the liability of directors and members for an unlawful distribution under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain when a distribution is unlawful, the liability of directors who authorised the unlawful distribution, the liability of members who received distributions knowing them to be unlawful, and the available remedies.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the rules governing the transfer of shares in a private company limited by shares (LTD) under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the general right to transfer shares, the typical pre-emption rights and board approval requirements found in the constitution of a private company, and the procedure for registering a transfer.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Discuss the circumstances in which the board of directors of a private company may refuse to register a transfer of shares. In your answer, explain the general duty of directors to consider a transfer request in good faith and in the interests of the company, discuss the rights of the transferor and transferee if a transfer is refused, and consider the relevance of the unfair prejudice remedy under s.212 to a refusal to register.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the effect of pre-emption provisions (rights of first refusal) in the constitution of an Irish private company on the transfer of shares. In your answer, explain how pre-emption rights operate, the procedure for offering shares to existing members before an outside transfer, and consider the consequences of a transfer in breach of pre-emption rights.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the different types of resolutions under the Companies Act 2014 — ordinary resolutions, special resolutions, and resolutions requiring unanimity — and the circumstances in which each is required. In your answer, explain the majority required for each, the notice requirements, and give examples of decisions that require each type of resolution.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the written resolution procedure available to private companies under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain when this procedure may be used, the requirements for the resolution to be signed by the required majority, the time limits within which signatures must be obtained, and the advantages of the procedure over holding a general meeting.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Discuss the circumstances in which a special resolution is required under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, identify at least five matters that require a special resolution (including alteration of the constitution, reduction of share capital, and voluntary winding up), explain the majority and notice requirements, and assess whether the requirement for a special resolution provides adequate protection for minority shareholders.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the rules governing annual general meetings (AGMs) of companies under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain which companies are required to hold AGMs, the business transacted at an AGM, the notice requirements, and the consequences of failing to hold an AGM.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the rules governing extraordinary general meetings (EGMs) of companies under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain who may convene an EGM, the requisition right of members under s.178, the notice period, and what happens where the directors fail to convene an EGM following a valid requisition.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Discuss the quorum and notice requirements for general meetings under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the default minimum quorum for a general meeting, the requirements for a valid notice of meeting (including the minimum period, contents, and persons entitled to receive notice), and consider the consequences where a meeting is held without proper notice.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the rules governing meetings of the board of directors under the Companies Act 2014 and any applicable provisions of the company's constitution. In your answer, explain the quorum requirements, the chairperson's casting vote, the keeping of minutes, and the power of directors to act by majority decision.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Discuss the concept of corporate governance in the context of an Irish private company. In your answer, explain the key principles of good board governance — including the separation of the roles of chairperson and CEO, board composition, and the management of conflicts of interest — and assess the extent to which these principles are legally enforced under the Companies Act 2014.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the role and responsibilities of the company secretary in an Irish private company under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the secretary's statutory duties (filing obligations, minute-keeping, and maintaining the register of members), explain the personal liability that may arise from a failure to perform these duties, and assess whether the role of the company secretary is adequately regulated.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the examinership procedure under Part 10 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain who may petition for the appointment of an examiner, the test of 'reasonable prospect of survival', the powers of the examiner, the protection from creditors while under court protection, and the role of the scheme of arrangement in effecting a rescue.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Discuss the conditions that must be satisfied before a court will confirm a scheme of arrangement under s.541 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the 'no worse off' test (the comparator test), the requirement of class approval, and discuss the circumstances in which the court will override the opposition of a dissenting class of creditors.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Critically assess examinership as a corporate rescue mechanism in Ireland. In your answer, compare examinership with receivership and liquidation, explain the advantages of examinership for employees, creditors, and shareholders, and consider whether the examinership process is accessible to small and medium enterprises (SMEs) given the cost and procedural requirements.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the nature and function of receivership in Irish company law. In your answer, explain how a receiver is appointed (by the court and out of court), the powers and duties of a receiver, the receiver's primary obligation to the appointing creditor, and the extent of any duty owed to the company, its directors, and unsecured creditors.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Discuss the distinction between a receiver and a receiver and manager (administrative receiver) in Irish company law. In your answer, explain the additional powers of a receiver and manager to manage the company's business as a going concern, explain the circumstances in which such powers will be exercised, and consider the impact on directors who lose control of the company's assets.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Critically assess the position of unsecured creditors in a receivership under Irish company law. In your answer, explain the order of priority of payments in a receivership, the limited protection available to unsecured creditors, and consider whether the law should impose greater obligations on receivers to protect the interests of unsecured creditors.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the different modes of winding up under the Companies Act 2014, distinguishing between members' voluntary winding up, creditors' voluntary winding up, and compulsory (court) winding up. In your answer, explain the procedure for each, the circumstances in which each is appropriate, and the role of the liquidator in each type of winding up.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the grounds on which the court may order the compulsory winding up of a company under s.569 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, discuss the principal grounds — including inability to pay debts, just and equitable, and deadlock — explain the consequences of a winding-up order, and discuss the effect of the order on ongoing legal proceedings.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.text: Explain the priority of claims in a company liquidation under the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, set out the order of priority — including the costs of winding up, preferential creditors, fixed charge holders, floating charge holders (net of the prescribed part), and unsecured creditors — and critically assess whether this order of priority achieves a just outcome.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Realisation of Corporate Assets — Voidable Transactions
questions.text: Explain the law on fraudulent preferences under s.604 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain the conditions for a payment or transaction to constitute a fraudulent preference (including the dominant intention requirement, the connection to a connected person, and the time limits), and explain how a liquidator may have such transactions set aside.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Realisation of Corporate Assets — Voidable Transactions
questions.text: Explain the law on voidable floating charges under s.597 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain why a floating charge created in favour of a connected person within two years of winding up (or a year in favour of an unconnected person) may be set aside, explain the exceptions, and assess whether this provision adequately protects creditors from the preferential creation of security.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Realisation of Corporate Assets — Voidable Transactions
questions.text: Explain the transactions at an undervalue provisions under s.608 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, explain when a liquidator may set aside a transaction entered into at an undervalue, the time limits applicable, the defences available, and the remedies available to the liquidator on successfully challenging such a transaction.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Establishment and Functions of the Corporate Enforcement Authority
questions.text: Explain the establishment and principal functions of the Corporate Enforcement Authority (CEA) under the Companies Act 2014 as amended by the Companies (Corporate Enforcement Authority) Act 2021. In your answer, explain the CEA's powers of investigation, its role in enforcing company law, and how it replaced the Office of the Director of Corporate Enforcement (ODCE).

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Establishment and Functions of the Corporate Enforcement Authority
questions.text: Explain the investigative powers of the Corporate Enforcement Authority. In your answer, discuss the CEA's power to require the production of documents, its power to enter and search premises, the power to arrest and question suspects, and the constitutional constraints that apply to the exercise of these investigative powers.

questions.subject: Company Law
questions.lessonTitle: Establishment and Functions of the Corporate Enforcement Authority
questions.text: Critically assess the effectiveness of the Corporate Enforcement Authority in combating corporate crime in Ireland. In your answer, consider the resources and powers available to the CEA, its track record in prosecuting significant cases, and whether the establishment of the CEA as a dedicated authority (replacing the ODCE) has improved the enforcement of company law in Ireland.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: History and Origins of Bunreacht na hÉireann
questions.text: Critically examine the historical and political factors that led to the enactment of Bunreacht na hÉireann in 1937. In your answer, consider the extent to which the 1937 Constitution departed from the Irish Free State Constitution of 1922 and assess the significance of those departures for Irish constitutionalism.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: History and Origins of Bunreacht na hÉireann
questions.text: Explain the concept of popular sovereignty as enshrined in Bunreacht na hÉireann. How does the Preamble to the Constitution reflect this principle, and how have the courts given effect to it in constitutional jurisprudence?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: History and Origins of Bunreacht na hÉireann
questions.text: To what extent can the 1937 Constitution be described as reflecting the social and religious values of its era? In your answer, refer to relevant provisions of the original text and consider the impact of subsequent constitutional amendments on its character.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the distinction between the 'literal rule' and the 'broad, purposive approach' to constitutional interpretation. With reference to decided cases, which approach has the Irish Supreme Court favoured, and with what consequences for constitutional adjudication?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the tension between the 'historical approach' and the 'living document' approach to constitutional interpretation. How have Irish courts navigated this tension between original intent and evolving social values? Use relevant case law to support your answer.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically assess the role of natural law in the interpretation of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the judicial authorities that have relied on natural law and evaluate whether this interpretive approach remains appropriate in contemporary Irish constitutional law.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Amendment of the Constitution: Articles 46 and 47
questions.text: Explain the procedure for amending Bunreacht na hÉireann under Article 46. What restrictions, if any, exist on what can be amended, and how have the courts approached the question of whether the Constitution contains unamendable 'core values'?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Amendment of the Constitution: Articles 46 and 47
questions.text: Critically examine the role of the referendum under Article 47 as a mechanism for constitutional change. In your answer, consider cases involving the validity of referendum processes and assess the scope of judicial oversight of the amendment procedure.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Amendment of the Constitution: Articles 46 and 47
questions.text: Discuss whether there are implicit limits on the power to amend the Constitution. With reference to the concept of the 'basic structure doctrine' and Irish judicial authority, assess whether any provisions of Bunreacht na hÉireann are effectively unamendable.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Doctrine of Separation of Powers and Constitutional Framework
questions.text: Critically examine the doctrine of separation of powers as enshrined in Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the extent to which the Constitution achieves a genuine separation of the legislative, executive, and judicial functions and assess the practical limitations of this separation.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Doctrine of Separation of Powers and Constitutional Framework
questions.text: Discuss the principle of the rule of law and its relationship with the separation of powers under the 1937 Constitution. How have the Irish courts given effect to this principle in constitutional adjudication?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Doctrine of Separation of Powers and Constitutional Framework
questions.text: To what extent does the Irish constitutional framework permit one organ of government to exercise functions ordinarily belonging to another? Illustrate your answer with reference to relevant constitutional provisions and decided cases.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Oireachtas and Legislative Power: Article 15
questions.text: Article 15.2.1° vests the 'sole and exclusive power of making laws for the State' in the Oireachtas. Critically examine the scope of this exclusive legislative power and the constitutional limitations on its exercise.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Oireachtas and Legislative Power: Article 15
questions.text: Discuss the constitutional principle that the Oireachtas cannot abdicate its legislative function. With reference to the decision in Cityview Press v An Comhairle Oiliúna [1980] and subsequent case law, explain the test used to distinguish permissible delegation of legislative power from unconstitutional abdication.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Oireachtas and Legislative Power: Article 15
questions.text: Explain the role and functions of the Seanad under Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider whether the Seanad provides effective constitutional checks on the legislative power of the Dáil and assess the constitutional implications of proposals for Seanad reform.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Government and Executive Power: Article 28
questions.text: Explain the constitutional position of the Government under Article 28 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the principle of collective responsibility, the role of the Taoiseach, and the accountability mechanisms provided by the Constitution.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Government and Executive Power: Article 28
questions.text: Critically assess the scope of executive power under Article 28.2. Does the Constitution provide adequate safeguards against executive overreach, and what role do the courts play in reviewing the exercise of executive power?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Government and Executive Power: Article 28
questions.text: Discuss the constitutional framework governing emergency legislation under Article 28.3.3°. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which such legislation may be enacted and the extent of judicial review available in such circumstances.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Courts and Judicial Power: Articles 34–37
questions.text: Article 34.1 provides that justice shall be administered in courts established by law. Explain the constitutional requirements for the administration of justice and discuss the significance of relevant Irish case law in defining the scope of the judicial power.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Courts and Judicial Power: Articles 34–37
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional guarantee of judicial independence under Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, refer to Articles 35 and 36 and discuss the mechanisms that protect judges from interference by the executive and legislature.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Courts and Judicial Power: Articles 34–37
questions.text: Discuss the distinction between 'limited' and 'unlimited' jurisdiction in the Irish court system as established by Articles 34 and 36. With reference to relevant case law, explain how the courts have interpreted the requirement that the High Court shall have 'full original jurisdiction'.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional role of the President of Ireland under Articles 12 to 14 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the extent to which the President exercises discretionary powers and assess the constitutional safeguards governing the exercise of those powers.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the President's power to refer a Bill to the Supreme Court under Article 26. What are the legal consequences of a ruling of unconstitutionality, and how does this procedure interact with the doctrine of precedent and the general law of constitutional challenge?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the constitutional provisions governing the removal of a President from office under Article 12.10. In your answer, consider the procedural safeguards applicable to impeachment and assess whether these provisions are adequate to ensure presidential accountability.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Delegated Legislation and Henry VIII Clauses
questions.text: Explain the concept of delegated legislation and distinguish it from primary legislation. In your answer, consider the constitutional limitations on the power of the Oireachtas to delegate legislative authority to ministers or other bodies, with particular reference to the Cityview Press test.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Delegated Legislation and Henry VIII Clauses
questions.text: What is a 'Henry VIII clause'? Critically assess the constitutional validity of such clauses under Bunreacht na hÉireann, having regard to the principles established in cases such as Meagher v Minister for Agriculture [1994] and assess the implications of such clauses for the separation of powers.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Delegated Legislation and Henry VIII Clauses
questions.text: Discuss the judicial mechanisms available to challenge the validity of delegated legislation in Ireland. In your answer, consider the grounds on which secondary legislation may be declared ultra vires and assess the effectiveness of these mechanisms as a check on executive and ministerial power.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Grounds and Procedure for Judicial Review
questions.text: Explain the procedural requirements for initiating judicial review proceedings in Ireland under Order 84 of the Rules of the Superior Courts. In your answer, consider the requirement for leave, applicable time limits, and the principal constitutional and statutory grounds on which review may be sought.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Grounds and Procedure for Judicial Review
questions.text: Critically examine the grounds of judicial review available in Irish constitutional law to challenge the exercise of public power. In your answer, consider the grounds of illegality, irrationality, and procedural impropriety and explain how these grounds have been applied in Irish jurisprudence.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Grounds and Procedure for Judicial Review
questions.text: Discuss the concept of 'constitutional justice' in the context of judicial review of administrative action. How have the Irish courts developed the principles of natural and constitutional justice, and what is the practical significance of the audi alteram partem and nemo iudex in sua causa rules?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional basis for the right to seek a remedy in the Irish courts. In your answer, consider the range of remedies available in constitutional litigation — including declarations, injunctions, and damages — and the principles governing the court's discretion to grant or refuse relief.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically assess the remedy of a declaration of incompatibility under Section 5 of the European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003. How does this remedy differ from the declaration of unconstitutionality available in constitutional litigation, and what are the practical consequences of each form of relief?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the doctrine of constitutional standing (locus standi) in Irish law. In your answer, consider the development of standing rules from Cahill v Sutton [1980] onwards and assess the extent to which modern standing rules facilitate or restrict effective access to constitutional justice.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the Article 26 reference procedure under Bunreacht na hÉireann. Who may invoke this procedure, in what circumstances, and what is the legal effect of a decision by the Supreme Court on a referred Bill?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional consequences of the Supreme Court upholding the constitutionality of a Bill under Article 26. With reference to the immunity from further challenge, assess whether the Article 26 procedure adequately safeguards constitutional rights.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the extent to which the Article 26 procedure has been an effective mechanism for constitutional adjudication. In your answer, consider the legislative measures that have been referred and evaluate the significance of this procedure within the broader framework of constitutional review.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional guarantee of equality before the law under Article 40.1 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the scope of this guarantee and discuss the extent to which it permits or prohibits differential treatment of persons under the law.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the concept of 'human personality' in Article 40.1. With reference to decided cases, explain how the courts have interpreted the requirement that the State shall hold every citizen equal before the law while acknowledging human differences of capacity, physical and moral.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between the equality guarantee in Article 40.1 and the prohibitions on discrimination in EU law and the ECHR. In your answer, consider whether the constitutional equality guarantee provides broader or narrower protection than these international instruments.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of unenumerated rights under Article 40.3 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. How did the Supreme Court in Ryan v AG [1965] justify the recognition of rights not expressly stated in the Constitution, and what rights have subsequently been recognised under this doctrine?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically assess the judicial methodology for identifying and vindicating unenumerated rights under Article 40.3. In your answer, consider the tension between natural law reasoning and positivist approaches and evaluate which methodology is more consistent with the constitutional text.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the extent to which the State's obligation to protect personal rights under Article 40.3 imposes positive duties on the legislature and executive. In your answer, refer to relevant case law and consider whether the Irish courts have been willing to order the State to take positive steps to vindicate constitutional rights.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional guarantee of personal liberty under Article 40.4 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the scope of the guarantee, the procedure for inquiring into the legality of detention, and the circumstances in which deprivation of liberty may be constitutionally justified.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the remedy of an inquiry under Article 40.4.2° as a mechanism for protecting personal liberty in Ireland. In your answer, consider the procedural requirements, the grounds on which release may be ordered, and the constitutional significance of this remedy.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the constitutional restrictions on preventive detention in Ireland. With reference to relevant constitutional provisions and decided cases, assess the extent to which the Irish legal system permits the detention of persons who have not been convicted of a criminal offence.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional protection of the dwelling under Article 40.5 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the scope of the term 'dwelling', the nature of the protection afforded, and the circumstances in which entry by State agents may be constitutionally justified.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the interaction between the constitutional protection of the dwelling under Article 40.5 and the powers of search and entry conferred on Gardaí by statute. How have the courts resolved conflicts between statutory search powers and the constitutional guarantee?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the exclusionary rule in Irish constitutional law with particular reference to Article 40.5. With reference to The People (DPP) v Kenny [1990] and subsequent case law, assess the circumstances in which evidence obtained in breach of the inviolability of the dwelling will be excluded at trial.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional guarantee of freedom of expression under Article 40.6.1°(i) of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the scope of the right, the express limitations contained in the constitutional text, and the additional restrictions permitted by legislation.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional framework governing defamation law in Ireland. In your answer, consider how the Defamation Act 2009 balances the right to free expression under Article 40.6 against the protection of reputation and assess whether this balance is constitutionally appropriate.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the constitutional protection of press freedom under Article 40.6.2°. In your answer, consider the extent to which the media enjoy special constitutional status and assess how Irish courts have balanced press freedom against competing rights such as the right to privacy and the right to a fair trial.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Freedom of Assembly and Association: Article 40.6
questions.text: Explain the constitutional guarantees of freedom of peaceful assembly and freedom of association under Article 40.6.1°(ii) and (iii) of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the scope of each right and the constitutional limitations that may be imposed on their exercise.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Freedom of Assembly and Association: Article 40.6
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional right to form and join trade unions under Article 40.6.1°(iii). With reference to Educational Company of Ireland v Fitzpatrick [1961] and subsequent case law, assess whether the constitutional protection extends to a right to have the union recognised for collective bargaining.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Freedom of Assembly and Association: Article 40.6
questions.text: Discuss the constitutional status of political parties in Ireland. In your answer, consider whether freedom of association includes the right to form and join political parties, and assess the constitutional constraints on legislation restricting or regulating party activities.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional protection of the family under Article 41 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the definition of 'family' adopted by the courts, the rights of the family as a unit, and the extent to which non-marital families attract constitutional protection.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional status of marriage under Article 41.3. With reference to the Thirty-Fourth Amendment of the Constitution (Marriage Equality) Act 2015, assess how the constitutional concept of 'marriage' has evolved and consider the significance of this amendment for constitutional interpretation.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the State's obligation under Article 41 to protect and support family life. In your answer, consider whether Article 41 imposes positive obligations on the State to legislate in support of families and assess the extent to which the courts have enforced such obligations.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional framework governing education under Article 42 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the constitutional recognition of parental rights in education, the role of the State as provider and regulator, and the rights of religious denominations to manage schools in receipt of State funding.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional tension between parental rights in education under Article 42 and the State's obligation to provide free primary education. How have the courts resolved conflicts between these provisions?

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the constitutional right to education in the context of children with special educational needs. In your answer, consider cases such as O'Donoghue v Minister for Health [1996] and assess the extent to which the Constitution obliges the State to provide appropriate educational provision for children with disabilities.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional rights of children as set out in Article 42A of Bunreacht na hÉireann, inserted by the Thirty-First Amendment. In your answer, consider what rights are recognised and how these differ from the protection afforded to children under the pre-amendment constitutional framework.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically assess the significance of Article 42A.1, which provides that the State 'acknowledges and affirms the natural and imprescriptible rights of all children'. In your answer, consider how this provision interacts with parental rights under Article 42 and family rights under Article 41.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the constitutional framework for State intervention in family life to protect children under Article 42A.2. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which the State may intervene in parental custody and assess whether the constitutional balance between family autonomy and child protection is appropriately calibrated.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional protection of private property under Article 43 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the scope of the right, the distinction between an 'unjust attack' on property rights and a legitimate regulation in accordance with the exigencies of the common good, and the test applied by the courts to distinguish between them.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the principles applied in Irish case law regarding the constitutional limits on State interference with private property. In your answer, assess what criteria the courts apply when evaluating whether legislation constitutes an unjust attack on property rights under Article 43.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between the constitutional protection of property under Article 43 and the doctrine of proportionality. In your answer, consider how the courts balance individual property rights against the demands of the 'exigencies of the common good'.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional guarantee of freedom of religion under Article 44 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the scope of the right, the distinction between freedom of belief and freedom to manifest religion, and the constitutional provisions regarding State support for religion.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional principle of State neutrality in religious matters following the removal of the 'special position' of the Catholic Church by the Fifth Amendment in 1972. Assess whether the current text of Article 44 adequately reflects the principle of State religious neutrality.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the interaction between freedom of religion under Article 44 and other constitutional rights, including the right to equality under Article 40.1 and freedom of expression under Article 40.6. In your answer, consider how the courts have resolved conflicts between these competing constitutional interests.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Directive Principles of Social Policy: Article 45
questions.text: Explain the nature and constitutional status of the Directive Principles of Social Policy under Article 45 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the express non-justiciability of these provisions and assess their significance within the broader constitutional order.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Directive Principles of Social Policy: Article 45
questions.text: Critically assess the practical significance of Article 45 in constitutional adjudication. In your answer, consider whether and how the courts have made use of the Directive Principles to inform the interpretation of other constitutional provisions or to evaluate the validity of legislative choices.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Directive Principles of Social Policy: Article 45
questions.text: Discuss the extent to which Article 45 has influenced social legislation in Ireland. In your answer, consider whether the non-justiciable nature of these provisions renders them practically irrelevant, or whether they serve an important expressive and guidance function in Irish constitutional law.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Due Course of Law: The Core Guarantee
questions.text: Explain the constitutional guarantee of trial 'in due course of law' under Article 38.1 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the scope of this guarantee, its relationship with the common law fair trial right, and how Irish courts have developed its content.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Due Course of Law: The Core Guarantee
questions.text: Critically examine the concept of 'constitutional justice' as developed by the Irish courts under Article 38.1. In your answer, consider the key procedural guarantees the courts have derived from this provision and assess their significance for persons charged with criminal offences.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Due Course of Law: The Core Guarantee
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between Article 38.1 (trial in due course of law) and Article 40.4 (personal liberty). In your answer, consider the interaction between these two provisions in criminal proceedings and assess the composite constitutional protections available to a person facing trial.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional principle that criminal offences must be defined with sufficient certainty to satisfy Article 38.1 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, refer to relevant case law including King v AG [1981] and assess the test applied by the courts to determine whether a penal statute is unconstitutionally vague.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the decision in King v AG [1981] in the context of the constitutional requirement of legal certainty in criminal law. In your answer, consider the grounds on which the court struck down the offence of loitering with intent and assess the broader constitutional implications of that decision.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the interaction between the constitutional prohibition on vague offences and the principle of legality in criminal law (nullum crimen sine lege). In your answer, consider how the principle of legal certainty is given constitutional expression in Ireland and assess its practical importance for the legislative definition of criminal conduct.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Presumption of Innocence and Reverse Onus
questions.text: Explain the constitutional status of the presumption of innocence in Irish law. In your answer, consider how the presumption is derived from Article 38.1 and the procedural consequences it has for the prosecution and defence in criminal proceedings.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Presumption of Innocence and Reverse Onus
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional validity of reverse onus clauses in criminal legislation. In your answer, consider the distinction between an evidential burden and a legal burden and discuss the constitutional test applied to such provisions with reference to relevant Irish case law.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Presumption of Innocence and Reverse Onus
questions.text: Discuss the extent to which the principle of proportionality applies to the imposition of reverse onus provisions in criminal statutes. In your answer, consider whether the Irish courts have adopted a consistent approach and assess the circumstances in which such provisions may be constitutionally justified.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional right to a trial without undue delay in Irish law. In your answer, consider the constitutional basis for this right, the factors relevant to assessing whether delay has been 'undue', and the remedies available where such delay is established.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the decision in PM v Malone [2002] and its significance for the constitutional protection against delay in criminal proceedings. In your answer, consider the competing interests identified by the court and assess how the Irish courts balance these interests in practice.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the approach of the Irish courts to applications for prohibition of trial on grounds of undue delay. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which prohibition will be granted or refused and assess whether the constitutional framework adequately protects defendants against prejudice caused by prosecutorial delay.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Duty to Seek and Preserve Evidence
questions.text: Explain the constitutional duty on the prosecution to seek and preserve evidence in criminal proceedings in Ireland. In your answer, consider the constitutional basis for this duty, the circumstances in which it arises, and the consequences of a breach for the fairness of a criminal trial.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Duty to Seek and Preserve Evidence
questions.text: Critically examine the principles established in Braddish v DPP [2001] regarding the obligation of the Gardaí to seek and preserve potentially relevant evidence. In your answer, assess the scope of this obligation and consider whether it has been applied consistently by the Irish courts.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Duty to Seek and Preserve Evidence
questions.text: Discuss the remedies available to a criminal defendant where the prosecution has failed to seek or preserve relevant evidence. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which the courts will stay criminal proceedings or order prohibition of a trial on the basis of evidential loss or destruction.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional framework governing pre-trial publicity and its impact on the right to a fair trial under Article 38.1. In your answer, consider the principles the courts apply when assessing whether adverse pre-trial publicity has created a real and serious risk of an unfair trial.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the principles applicable to applications to prohibit criminal trials on grounds of prejudicial pre-trial publicity. In your answer, consider the test established by the courts and assess how those principles have been applied in subsequent Irish case law.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the mechanisms available to trial judges to safeguard the fairness of criminal proceedings in cases of significant pre-trial publicity. In your answer, consider directions to juries, stays of proceedings, changes of venue, and other measures, and assess which mechanism best protects Article 38.1 rights in practice.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the principles of natural and constitutional justice as developed by the Irish courts. In your answer, consider the two core principles — audi alteram partem and nemo iudex in sua causa — and assess their application in both judicial and administrative decision-making contexts.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional basis for the right to fair procedures in Ireland. In your answer, consider how the courts have derived procedural rights from Articles 38 and 40 and assess whether the constitutional framework provides adequate protection for persons subject to decisions affecting their rights.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the right to be heard before an adverse decision is made. In your answer, consider the minimum procedural requirements imposed by constitutional justice — including notice of the case against a person, an opportunity to respond, and the giving of reasons — and assess the consequences of non-compliance.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional basis for the Special Criminal Court under Article 38.3 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which the ordinary courts are deemed 'inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice', the procedure for establishing such a court, and the constitutional validity of trial without jury before it.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional challenges that have been brought against trial before the Special Criminal Court. In your answer, consider the grounds on which the courts have upheld the constitutionality of such trial arrangements and assess whether the current framework adequately protects the right to jury trial.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the constitutional and human rights implications of the operation of the Special Criminal Court. In your answer, consider the compatibility of trial without jury with the right to a fair trial under Article 38.1 and with Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional right to legal representation in Irish criminal proceedings. In your answer, consider the constitutional basis for this right, the circumstances in which the State is obliged to provide legal aid, and the scope of the right to be represented by a lawyer of one's choice.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the decision in State (Healy) v Donoghue [1976] in the context of the constitutional right to legal representation. In your answer, consider the constitutional basis identified by the court for this right and assess its subsequent development in case law.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the right to legal representation in the context of Garda interrogation in Ireland. In your answer, consider the constitutional implications of the decision in People (DPP) v Gormley and White [2014] and assess how this decision has changed the constitutional rights of suspects detained for questioning.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional framework governing Ireland's relationship with international law under Article 29 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider the doctrines of monism and dualism and assess which approach Ireland has adopted in its constitutional framework for incorporating international obligations.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional status of international treaties in Irish domestic law. In your answer, consider how Article 29.6 limits the domestic legal effect of international agreements and assess the consequences of this provision for the enforceability of Ireland's treaty obligations in domestic courts.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss Ireland's constitutional commitments to the pacific settlement of international disputes and the general principles of international law under Article 29.1 and 29.2. In your answer, consider whether these provisions impose justiciable obligations on the Government and assess their practical significance.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain the constitutional basis for Ireland's membership of the European Union under Article 29.4. In your answer, consider the constitutional amendments required for the ratification of successive EU Treaties and assess the significance of the requirement for a referendum before further sovereignty transfers.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the constitutional implications of Crotty v An Taoiseach [1987] for the ratification of EU treaties in Ireland. In your answer, consider why the Supreme Court required a referendum for ratification of the Single European Act and assess the constitutional framework this established for future EU treaty changes.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
