questions.text: Discuss the constitutional framework for ratifying EU treaties in Ireland. In your answer, consider the criteria used to determine whether a new EU treaty requires a referendum under Article 29.4 and assess how this framework has operated in the context of successive treaty amendments.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Primacy of EU Law: The Irish Constitutional Position
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of the primacy of EU law as established in Costa v ENEL [1964] and subsequent EU case law. In your answer, consider how Article 29.4.6° of Bunreacht na hÉireann gives constitutional expression to EU primacy and assess the implications of this doctrine for the Irish constitutional order.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Primacy of EU Law: The Irish Constitutional Position
questions.text: Critically examine the extent to which the primacy of EU law is subject to constitutional limits in Ireland. In your answer, consider whether the Irish courts have recognised any 'constitutional identity' limits on EU supremacy, and compare the Irish constitutional position with that of other Member States.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.lessonTitle: Primacy of EU Law: The Irish Constitutional Position
questions.text: Discuss the interaction between the supremacy of EU law and the protection of fundamental rights under Bunreacht na hÉireann. In your answer, consider how conflicts between EU law and constitutionally protected rights are resolved and assess whether the Irish constitutional framework adequately protects fundamental rights in EU law contexts.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Explain how the European Convention on Human Rights was incorporated into Irish domestic law by the European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003. In your answer, consider the interpretive obligations imposed on the courts and public bodies by the Act and assess the nature and legal effect of the incorporation achieved.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Critically examine the remedy of a declaration of incompatibility under Section 5 of the European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003. In your answer, consider how this remedy differs from a declaration of unconstitutionality, its legal consequences, and whether it provides effective protection for Convention rights in Irish law.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between the European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003 and the Irish Constitution. In your answer, consider what happens when a provision of Irish law is both incompatible with the ECHR and arguably constitutional, and assess whether the current framework for incorporating the Convention is adequate for the protection of fundamental rights.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the rules on offer and acceptance in Irish contract law. In your answer, distinguish between an offer and an invitation to treat, explain the rules on communication and revocation of offers, and consider when acceptance is effective — including the postal rule and its application to electronic communications.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Critically assess the concept of the 'battle of the forms' in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain what is meant by this problem, discuss the 'last shot' rule and the 'first shot' rule as competing solutions, and consider which approach is most consistent with objective theory of contract formation.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the circumstances in which silence may constitute acceptance in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain the general rule that silence does not amount to acceptance, identify any exceptions, and assess whether the current position adequately reflects commercial practice.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of consideration in Irish contract law. In your answer, define consideration, explain the rules that consideration must be sufficient but need not be adequate, that consideration must move from the promisee, and that past consideration is no consideration, illustrating each rule with relevant case law.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the rule that performance of an existing duty cannot constitute good consideration in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain the public duty rule and the existing contractual duty rule, discuss the modification of this rule following Williams v Roffey Bros [1991] and consider whether Irish law has adopted this approach.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Critically assess the doctrine of consideration as a requirement for the enforceability of agreements. In your answer, consider the relationship between consideration and intention to create legal relations, evaluate the arguments for abolishing or reforming the consideration requirement, and assess how promissory estoppel addresses gaps left by the doctrine.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of promissory estoppel in Irish contract law. In your answer, set out the elements of the doctrine as established in Central London Property Trust Ltd v High Trees House Ltd [1947], explain when it operates as a 'shield' rather than a 'sword', and discuss the Irish courts' approach to the doctrine.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between promissory estoppel and the consideration requirement in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain how estoppel supplements rather than replaces consideration, identify the limits of the doctrine (including the requirement that the promise be clear and unambiguous), and assess whether Irish law should allow promissory estoppel to be used as a cause of action.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the requirements for promissory estoppel to operate in Irish law and critically examine the element of 'detrimental reliance'. In your answer, consider whether detriment must be suffered, whether it is sufficient that the promisee altered their position in reliance on the promise, and assess the Irish case law on this point.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of intention to create legal relations in Irish contract law. In your answer, distinguish between domestic or social agreements (where the presumption is against legal enforceability) and commercial agreements (where the presumption is in favour), and explain how the courts determine whether a binding contract was intended.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the application of the presumption against legal relations in the domestic and social context in Irish contract law. In your answer, refer to cases such as Balfour v Balfour [1919], explain the circumstances in which the presumption may be rebutted, and assess whether the current approach is satisfactory.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Critically examine the enforceability of so-called 'honour clauses' and 'subject to contract' agreements in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain how these devices negative intention to create legal relations, discuss how the courts approach pre-contractual negotiations, and consider whether the current rules adequately protect parties in commercial negotiations.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the rules on contractual capacity as they apply to minors in Irish law. In your answer, distinguish between contracts that are binding on a minor (contracts for necessaries), contracts that are voidable at the minor's option (contracts of continuing obligation), and contracts that are void, referring to the relevant statutory provisions and case law.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the rules on contractual capacity as they apply to persons of unsound mind and persons under the influence of alcohol or drugs in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain when a contract may be void or voidable on the ground of incapacity, and consider the requirement that the other party knew of the incapacity.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the rules on corporate capacity in Irish contract law. In your answer, discuss the ultra vires doctrine, explain how it has been modified by statute (including the Companies Act 2014), and assess the extent to which the doctrine continues to operate as a constraint on corporate contracting.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the formal requirements for the validity of contracts in Irish law. In your answer, discuss which contracts must be in writing (including contracts for the sale or other disposition of land under s.51 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009) and which must be evidenced in writing, and explain the consequences of non-compliance.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of part performance and its role in Irish contract law as an equitable means of enforcing contracts for the disposition of land that lack the required written memorandum or note. In your answer, set out the elements of the doctrine, discuss the Irish case law, and explain whether the LCLRA 2009 has altered the position.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the requirements for a valid guarantee under s.2 of the Statute of Frauds (Ireland) 1695. In your answer, explain what constitutes a guarantee, explain the writing requirement, and distinguish a guarantee from an indemnity — including the significance of that distinction for the Statute of Frauds.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the distinction between a contractual term and a mere representation in Irish law. In your answer, identify the tests used by the courts to determine whether a pre-contractual statement was intended to be a term or a representation — including the relative knowledge test, the timing of the statement, and whether the statement was reduced to writing — and discuss the consequences of that classification.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the rules on incorporation of express terms into a contract in Irish law. In your answer, explain the three methods of incorporation (by signature, by notice, and by course of dealing), apply the rule in L'Estrange v Graucob [1934], and consider when a party may be bound by a term they did not read or understand.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the parol evidence rule in Irish contract law and critically assess its scope and limitations. In your answer, explain what the rule prohibits, identify the main exceptions (including collateral contracts, implied terms, and ambiguity), and assess whether the rule serves any useful function in modern commercial contracts.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Distinguish between conditions, warranties, and innominate (intermediate) terms in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain the significance of this classification for the remedies available on breach, discuss how the courts classify contractual terms, and refer to relevant Irish and English case law including The Mihalis Angelos [1971] and Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co Ltd v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha Ltd [1962].

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the concept of an innominate term as developed in Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co Ltd v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha Ltd [1962] and assess its reception in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain how the courts determine whether a breach of an innominate term goes to the root of the contract, and consider whether the innominate term approach is satisfactory in practice.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the rule that a party who accepts a repudiatory breach affirms the contract and loses the right to terminate. In your answer, explain the concept of affirmation, the requirement that a party act promptly on discovering a breach, and discuss the Irish case law on the loss of the right to terminate for breach of condition.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the different categories of implied terms in Irish contract law. In your answer, distinguish between terms implied in fact (the business efficacy test and the officious bystander test), terms implied by law (as a matter of legal policy in a class of contract), and terms implied by custom, referring to relevant Irish and English case law.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the principles of contractual interpretation in Irish law as set out in cases such as Analog Devices BV v Zurich Insurance Co [2005] and the Supreme Court in Persona Digital Telephony Ltd v Minister for Public Enterprise [2017]. In your answer, explain the objective test of construction, the approach to ambiguous language, and the role of 'business common sense'.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Critically assess whether a court can imply a term into a contract to fill a gap left by the parties. In your answer, discuss the distinction between interpretation and implication, explain the role of the Moorcock test and the Privy Council's clarification in Attorney General of Belize v Belize Telecom Ltd [2009], and consider how Irish courts have approached this question.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the rules on the incorporation and interpretation of exemption clauses in Irish contract law. In your answer, discuss the requirement for reasonable notice, the rule that ambiguous exemption clauses are construed against the party relying on them (contra proferentem), and the rule that clear words are required to exclude liability for negligence.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the statutory control of exemption clauses in Irish law under the Sale of Goods Act 1893 and the Sale of Goods and Supply of Services Act 1980 (as amended). In your answer, explain which implied terms cannot be excluded in consumer contracts, which may be excluded in commercial contracts subject to a reasonableness test, and how these provisions have been modified by the Consumer Rights Act 2022.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Critically assess the judicial and statutory techniques for controlling exemption clauses in Irish consumer contracts. In your answer, consider the interplay between the common law rules on incorporation, interpretation, and the statutory controls under the Consumer Rights Act 2022 and the EU Unfair Contract Terms Directive, and evaluate whether the current framework adequately protects consumers.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the requirement of certainty of terms in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain the distinction between terms that are void for uncertainty and terms that are incomplete (and therefore no contract has been formed), illustrate with case law, and discuss how the courts use implication of terms to save apparently incomplete or uncertain agreements.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the legal effect of 'agreements to agree' and 'agreements to negotiate' in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain the general rule established in cases such as May and Butcher Ltd v R [1934] that an agreement to agree is not an enforceable contract, consider the exceptions developed in case law (particularly where a mechanism for determining the term is provided), and assess the position under Irish law.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain how the courts deal with contracts that appear to be uncertain on their face. In your answer, discuss the maxim ut res magis valeat quam pereat (the presumption in favour of validity), the use of objective criteria to resolve uncertainty, and the principle that courts will not lightly strike down commercial agreements — referring to relevant Irish and English authorities.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.lessonTitle: Consumer Rights Act 2022: Sale of Goods
questions.text: Explain the key provisions of the Consumer Rights Act 2022 as they apply to contracts for the sale of goods. In your answer, explain the conformity requirements (including satisfactory quality, fitness for purpose, and conformity with description and sample), the hierarchy of remedies available to a consumer on non-conformity, and how the 2022 Act changed the previous law under the Sale of Goods Acts.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.lessonTitle: Consumer Rights Act 2022: Sale of Goods
questions.text: Explain the consumer's right to repair or replacement under the Consumer Rights Act 2022 and the circumstances in which the consumer may seek a price reduction or rescission. In your answer, discuss the conditions that must be satisfied for each remedy, the time limits applicable, and the hierarchy in which remedies must be pursued.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.lessonTitle: Consumer Rights Act 2022: Sale of Goods
questions.text: Discuss the rules on the passing of property and risk in consumer contracts for the sale of goods under the Consumer Rights Act 2022. In your answer, explain when property passes under a consumer sale, how risk is allocated between seller and consumer, and how these rules interact with the remedies framework on non-conformity.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.lessonTitle: Consumer Rights Act 2022: Digital Content and Services
questions.text: Explain the new framework introduced by the Consumer Rights Act 2022 for contracts for digital content and digital services. In your answer, explain what constitutes 'digital content' and a 'digital service', set out the conformity requirements, and explain the remedies available to a consumer where digital content or services do not conform.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.lessonTitle: Consumer Rights Act 2022: Digital Content and Services
questions.text: Discuss how the Consumer Rights Act 2022 treats contracts for 'goods with digital elements'. In your answer, explain how such contracts are distinguished from pure digital content contracts, explain the conformity obligations that apply to both the physical and digital components, and discuss the update obligations imposed on the trader.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.lessonTitle: Consumer Rights Act 2022: Digital Content and Services
questions.text: Critically assess the adequacy of the Consumer Rights Act 2022 in protecting consumers who enter contracts for subscription-based digital services. In your answer, consider the modification of ongoing digital service contracts, the consumer's right to terminate a modified contract, and whether the 2022 Act adequately addresses issues of algorithmic pricing, personal data as payment, and hidden charges.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the law on unfair contract terms in consumer contracts under the Consumer Rights Act 2022 (implementing EU Directive 93/13/EEC as amended). In your answer, explain the 'core terms' exemption (price and subject matter), set out the test for unfairness, explain the effect of an unfair term, and discuss the role of enforcement bodies.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the 'grey list' (indicative list) of potentially unfair terms in consumer contracts under the Consumer Rights Act 2022. In your answer, identify several categories from the list, explain why these terms are presumptively unfair, and consider the circumstances in which a listed term might nonetheless be considered fair.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Critically assess how Irish and European courts have approached the unfairness test in consumer contract terms, with reference to the CJEU's jurisprudence. In your answer, discuss the requirement of 'significant imbalance', the relevance of 'good faith', and assess whether the current framework strikes the right balance between contractual freedom and consumer protection.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the law on misrepresentation in Irish contract law. In your answer, define misrepresentation, explain the distinction between fraudulent, negligent, and innocent misrepresentation, set out the remedies available for each, and discuss how the Misrepresentation Act 1893 (as applied in Ireland) modified the common law position.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the remedy of rescission for misrepresentation in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain the right of rescission, the circumstances in which it may be lost (affirmation, lapse of time, impossibility of restitution, and third party rights), and consider whether rescission remains the most appropriate remedy for misrepresentation.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the duty of disclosure in Irish contract law. In your answer, discuss the general rule that mere silence does not constitute misrepresentation, identify the exceptions including contracts uberrimae fidei, the duty of disclosure arising from a half-truth, the duty to correct a statement that has become false, and assess whether Irish law should impose a broader duty of pre-contractual disclosure.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the law on common mistake in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain what is meant by common mistake, distinguish between mistakes as to the existence of the subject matter (res extincta) and mistakes as to quality, and critically assess the limits of common mistake as recognised by the courts following Bell v Lever Brothers Ltd [1932].

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the rules on mutual and unilateral mistake in Irish contract law. In your answer, distinguish between mutual mistake (where both parties are at cross-purposes) and unilateral mistake (where only one party is mistaken), explain when such mistakes will render a contract void, and discuss the relevance of the parties' conduct and the objective test of agreement.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the equitable jurisdiction to grant relief for mistake in Irish contract law, including the power to set aside a contract or to rectify a written instrument. In your answer, explain the conditions for rectification, discuss how the equitable jurisdiction interacts with the strict common law rules on mistake, and assess whether the equitable remedies provide adequate relief.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of duress in Irish contract law. In your answer, distinguish between duress to the person, duress of goods, and economic duress, explain the requirements for each form, and discuss how the courts determine whether the claimant had no 'practical choice' but to enter the contract.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Critically examine the development of the doctrine of economic duress in Irish and English contract law. In your answer, explain the requirements for economic duress as established in cases such as Universe Tankships Inc of Monrovia v ITWF [1983] and Pao On v Lau Yiu Long [1980], and assess whether the current doctrine adequately distinguishes illegitimate commercial pressure from hard bargaining.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between duress, undue influence, and unconscionable bargains in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain how these doctrines overlap, identify the key distinctions between them, and assess whether the law provides adequate and coherent protection against unfair contracts.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of undue influence in Irish contract law. In your answer, distinguish between actual undue influence (Class 1) and presumed undue influence (Class 2), explain when the presumption arises and how it may be rebutted, and discuss the relevance of independent advice in this context.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the special position of a bank seeking to enforce a surety agreement against a spouse or cohabitant who gave security under the influence of the principal debtor. In your answer, explain the approach of the Irish courts following decisions such as AIB v O'Brien [1995] IESC and the English decision in Royal Bank of Scotland plc v Etridge (No.2) [2001], and assess the extent to which Irish law has adopted the Etridge framework.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the concept of an 'unconscionable bargain' in Irish equity. In your answer, distinguish this doctrine from undue influence, explain the conditions under which equity will set aside an unconscionable bargain (including inequality of bargaining power, exploitation of weakness, and lack of independent advice), and assess whether Irish law recognises a general doctrine of unconscionability.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the rules on discharge of a contract by performance in Irish contract law. In your answer, discuss the general rule that exact performance is required (the rule in Cutter v Powell [1795]), explain the substantial performance doctrine, the divisibility exception, and the prevention principle, illustrating with relevant case law.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain how a contract may be discharged by agreement in Irish law. In your answer, distinguish between bilateral rescission (accord and satisfaction), unilateral waiver, variation, and novation, explain the consideration requirements applicable to each, and discuss when a waiver may be revoked.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the doctrine of entire contracts in Irish law and its potential harsh consequences. In your answer, explain when a contract will be treated as 'entire' (requiring complete performance before any payment is due), consider how the courts mitigate harshness through doctrines of substantial performance, quantum meruit, and unjust enrichment, and refer to relevant case law.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the rules on discharge of a contract by breach in Irish contract law. In your answer, distinguish between actual breach and anticipatory repudiation, explain when a breach entitles the innocent party to treat the contract as discharged (breach of condition, repudiatory breach, or breach of innominate term going to the root), and discuss the requirement to 'accept' the repudiation.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the doctrine of anticipatory repudiation in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain what constitutes an anticipatory repudiation, explain the innocent party's options (immediate acceptance of repudiation or affirmation of the contract), and consider the risks associated with electing to affirm, including the decision in White and Carter (Councils) Ltd v McGregor [1962].

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the concept of a 'fundamental breach' in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain the historical development of the doctrine, its rejection as a rule of law by the House of Lords in Photo Production Ltd v Securicor Transport Ltd [1980], and assess whether the concept of a fundamental breach retains any relevance in modern Irish contract law.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of frustration in Irish contract law. In your answer, set out the tests for frustration (including the 'radical change in the obligation' test from Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham UDC [1956]), identify the categories of event that may frustrate a contract, and explain the legal consequences of frustration.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the limits of the doctrine of frustration in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain when frustration will not apply — including self-induced frustration, foreseen events, and where the risk was expressly or impliedly allocated by the contract — and assess whether these limitations are justifiable.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the consequences of frustration in Irish law, including the operation of the Civil Law (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2011 (which gives statutory effect to the Law Reform Commission recommendations on frustrated contracts). In your answer, explain the position at common law (the Chandler v Webster [1904] rule), the reform introduced, and the current position on the recovery of money paid and the payment for partial performance.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the law on damages for breach of contract in Ireland. In your answer, explain the two limbs of the rule in Hadley v Baxendale (1854), the distinction between the expectation measure and the reliance measure, and the claimant's duty to mitigate loss.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the assessment of damages in contract law, with particular reference to the distinction between the expectation interest, the reliance interest, and the restitutionary interest. In your answer, explain when a claimant may recover for loss of a chance, and critically assess the circumstances in which the courts will award reliance or restitutionary damages in preference to the expectation measure.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the rules on liquidated damages clauses and penalty clauses in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain the distinction between a genuine pre-estimate of loss (enforceable liquidated damages) and a clause that is in terrorem (unenforceable as a penalty), and assess how the rule has evolved following the UK Supreme Court decision in Cavendish Square Holding BV v Makdessi [2015] and consider its application in Irish law.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the equitable remedy of specific performance in Irish contract law. In your answer, set out the circumstances in which specific performance will be granted (including the requirement of inadequacy of damages and mutuality), identify the categories of contract where specific performance is refused (including contracts for personal services), and discuss any recent developments in the Irish case law.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the equitable remedy of injunction in the context of contracts in Irish law. In your answer, distinguish between prohibitory and mandatory injunctions, explain the American Cyanamid test for interlocutory injunctions, and discuss the circumstances in which an injunction may be granted to restrain breach of a negative stipulation in a contract (de facto specific performance).

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the remedy of rescission in Irish equity and how it differs from termination at common law. In your answer, explain the grounds for rescission (misrepresentation, undue influence, duress, and mistake), the bars to rescission (affirmation, lapse of time, impossibility of restitution, and third party rights), and assess whether the restitutionary consequences of rescission are handled satisfactorily under Irish law.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of privity of contract in Irish law. In your answer, explain the rule that a contract cannot confer rights or impose obligations on a third party, discuss the House of Lords' decision in Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co v Selfridge & Co Ltd [1915], and identify the main common law exceptions including agency, assignment, and the trust of a promise.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the equitable and statutory inroads into the doctrine of privity of contract in Irish law. In your answer, consider the impact of collateral contracts, the assignment of contractual rights, the trust analysis, and any relevant Irish statutory provisions (including the effect of the Married Women's Status Act 1957 and relevant insurance legislation), and assess whether these exceptions undermine the doctrine's coherence.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Critically assess whether Irish law should introduce a Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act modelled on the English Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. In your answer, explain the English reform, consider the arguments for and against reform of the privity rule in Ireland, and assess the extent to which the existing exceptions adequately protect third party interests.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the distinction between contracts that are illegal at formation (void for illegality) and contracts that are void (but not illegal) as contrary to public policy in Irish contract law. In your answer, identify the principal categories of each, explain the consequences of illegality and public policy invalidity, and discuss the in pari delicto rule.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Discuss the law on contracts in restraint of trade in Irish contract law. In your answer, explain the categories of restraint of trade (employment, sale of business, and exclusive dealing), set out the test of reasonableness as between the parties and as regards the public interest, and assess how the Irish courts balance freedom of contract against the right to work.

questions.subject: Contract Law
questions.text: Explain the effect of illegality on the right to recover money paid under an illegal contract in Irish law. In your answer, discuss the in pari delicto rule and its exceptions (including the locus poenitentiae doctrine), explain how the law has evolved to allow recovery in some cases, and critically assess whether the current rules produce just outcomes.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Characteristics and Indicia of a Crime
questions.text: Identify and explain the essential characteristics that distinguish a criminal offence from a civil wrong. In your answer, discuss the public interest dimension of criminal law, the role of the State as prosecutor, and the significance of the standard of proof beyond reasonable doubt. Refer to relevant Irish authorities where appropriate.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Characteristics and Indicia of a Crime
questions.text: Explain what is meant by the 'indicia of a crime' and discuss how the courts determine whether a statutory provision creates a criminal offence or merely a regulatory one. In your answer, consider the approach of the Irish courts and any relevant case law.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Characteristics and Indicia of a Crime
questions.text: Critically assess the principal functions of criminal law — punishment, deterrence, rehabilitation, and public protection — and consider to what extent these objectives are in tension with one another in the Irish criminal justice system.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the classification of criminal offences in Ireland as arrestable and non-arrestable offences under the Criminal Law Act 1997. In your answer, explain the significance of this classification for police powers of arrest, the mode of trial, and the sentencing jurisdiction of the courts.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Distinguish between indictable offences triable on indictment only, indictable offences triable summarily, and purely summary offences. Explain the constitutional and statutory framework governing the trial of criminal offences in Ireland, including the right to jury trial under Article 38.5 of the Constitution.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Discuss the concept of strict liability in Irish criminal law. In your answer, explain what is meant by a strict liability offence, identify the categories of offence where strict liability is typically imposed, and critically assess the justifications for and objections to strict liability as a matter of criminal law principle.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Actus Reus — Core Principles, Voluntariness, and Omissions
questions.text: Explain the principle that the actus reus of a criminal offence must be a voluntary act. In your answer, define what is meant by an involuntary act, discuss the defence of automatism (both sane and insane), and refer to relevant Irish and comparative case law where appropriate.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Actus Reus — Core Principles, Voluntariness, and Omissions
questions.text: Discuss the general rule that a person cannot be criminally liable for an omission in Irish law. In your answer, identify the recognised exceptions to this rule — including duty arising from statute, special relationship, assumption of responsibility, and creation of a dangerous situation — and illustrate each with relevant case law.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Actus Reus — Core Principles, Voluntariness, and Omissions
questions.text: Explain the concept of a 'state of affairs' offence and how it differs from conventional act-based liability. Critically assess whether state of affairs liability is consistent with the principle of voluntariness that underpins the actus reus requirement.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the rules of causation in Irish criminal law. In your answer, distinguish between factual causation (the 'but for' test) and legal causation, and discuss the circumstances in which an intervening act (novus actus interveniens) will break the chain of causation, with reference to relevant Irish and English case law.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Critically examine the 'thin skull' (eggshell skull) rule and its application in Irish criminal law. Discuss how this rule interacts with the principles of factual and legal causation, and assess whether its application is consistent with principles of criminal responsibility.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Discuss the problems of causation that arise when a victim refuses medical treatment or takes their own life following an assault. In your answer, consider how the Irish courts approach these situations with reference to the relevant case law and the principle that the defendant must take their victim as they find them.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Mens Rea — Intention, Recklessness, and Negligence
questions.text: Explain the concept of mens rea and its central role in Irish criminal law. In your answer, distinguish between direct intention and oblique intention, discuss the approach of the Irish courts to oblique intent, and explain how foresight of consequences is treated as evidence (but not proof) of intention.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Mens Rea — Intention, Recklessness, and Negligence
questions.text: Compare and contrast subjective recklessness and objective recklessness in Irish criminal law. In your answer, explain the decision in People (DPP) v Murray [1977] IR 360, identify the offences that require subjective recklessness under Irish law, and consider whether the objective standard is appropriate in any category of criminal offence.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Mens Rea — Intention, Recklessness, and Negligence
questions.text: Discuss the doctrine of transferred malice and its application in Irish criminal law. In your answer, explain the principle that intention is transferred from the intended victim to the actual victim, identify the limits of the doctrine, and assess whether the doctrine is defensible as a matter of criminal law theory.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Murder — Definition, Mens Rea, and Capital Murder
questions.text: Define murder under Irish law. In your answer, explain the mental element required for murder following People (DPP) v Murray [1977] IR 360, discuss the concept of capital murder under s.3 of the Criminal Justice Act 1990, and explain the constitutional dimension introduced by Article 13.6 of the Constitution.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Murder — Definition, Mens Rea, and Capital Murder
questions.text: Explain the mens rea for murder in Ireland as established in People (DPP) v Murray. In your answer, distinguish between the intent to kill and the intent to cause serious injury, and critically assess whether the Irish formulation of the mental element for murder is satisfactory having regard to the principle of correspondence.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Murder — Definition, Mens Rea, and Capital Murder
questions.text: Critically assess the abolition of the death penalty in Ireland and its replacement by the mandatory life sentence for murder under the Criminal Justice Act 1990. In your answer, consider the constitutional framework governing capital punishment under Article 13.6, the mandatory minimum sentence regime, and arguments for and against judicial discretion in sentencing for murder.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the defence of provocation in Irish criminal law as a partial defence to a charge of murder. In your answer, set out the elements of the defence as established in People (DPP) v MacEoin [1978] IR 27, discuss the 'reasonable man' standard, and explain the effect of the defence on the charge.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Critically examine the limitations of the provocation defence as currently recognised in Irish law. In your answer, consider the requirement that the accused must have suffered a sudden loss of self-control, the relevance of past history, and whether the defence adequately caters to cumulative provocation and 'slow-burn' cases such as domestic violence scenarios.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Compare the Irish approach to provocation with that adopted in other common law jurisdictions such as England and Wales (Loss of Control under the Coroners and Justice Act 2009). In your answer, identify the key differences and assess whether Irish law should be reformed to align with the English approach.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Involuntary Manslaughter and Dangerous Driving Causing Death
questions.text: Explain the law of involuntary manslaughter in Ireland. In your answer, distinguish between constructive manslaughter (unlawful act manslaughter) and gross negligence manslaughter, set out the elements of each, and illustrate with relevant case law.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Involuntary Manslaughter and Dangerous Driving Causing Death
questions.text: Discuss the offence of dangerous driving causing death under the Road Traffic Acts. In your answer, explain the mental element required, distinguish this offence from manslaughter by dangerous driving, and discuss the sentencing framework applicable to this offence.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Involuntary Manslaughter and Dangerous Driving Causing Death
questions.text: Critically assess the adequacy of the law of corporate manslaughter in Ireland. In your answer, explain the difficulties in attributing criminal liability to a corporate body for causing death through gross negligence, consider the 'identification doctrine', and assess whether the current law adequately addresses corporate accountability for workplace deaths.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Assisted Suicide and the Right to Die
questions.text: Explain the current legal position on assisted suicide in Ireland under s.2(2) of the Criminal Law (Suicide) Act 1993. In your answer, discuss the offence of aiding, abetting, counselling, or procuring the suicide of another, and consider the constitutional implications raised in cases such as Fleming v Ireland [2013] IESC 19.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Assisted Suicide and the Right to Die
questions.text: Critically examine whether the prohibition on assisted suicide in Ireland is compatible with rights under the European Convention on Human Rights, with particular reference to Article 8 (right to private life) and the jurisprudence of the European Court of Human Rights, including Pretty v United Kingdom (2002).

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Assisted Suicide and the Right to Die
questions.text: Discuss the ethical and legal arguments for and against legalising assisted dying in Ireland. In your answer, consider the competing interests of personal autonomy, protection of vulnerable persons, and the role of the courts versus the legislature in determining this question.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Assault, Assault Causing Harm, and Serious Harm
questions.text: Explain the offences of assault, assault causing harm, and causing serious harm under the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997. In your answer, set out the actus reus and mens rea of each offence, explain how 'harm' and 'serious harm' are defined, and distinguish between the three offences.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Assault, Assault Causing Harm, and Serious Harm
questions.text: Define 'assault' under s.2 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 and explain the role of consent in relation to this offence. In your answer, consider the limits of the defence of consent, including the rule in Attorney General's Reference (No. 6 of 1980) as applied in Irish law.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Assault, Assault Causing Harm, and Serious Harm
questions.text: Discuss the offence of causing serious harm under s.4 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997. In your answer, explain the mental element required, consider the distinction between 'harm' and 'serious harm', and assess the adequacy of this provision in addressing cases of serious violence.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Harassment and Stalking — S.10, S.10A NFOATP 1997
questions.text: Explain the offences of harassment under s.10 and stalking under s.10A of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 (as amended by the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023). In your answer, set out the elements of each offence and explain how the law has evolved to address modern forms of online and offline stalking.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Harassment and Stalking — S.10, S.10A NFOATP 1997
questions.text: Critically assess the adequacy of the Irish law on harassment and stalking in protecting victims. In your answer, discuss the practical difficulties in proving the mental element of these offences, the relevance of 'course of conduct', and whether the 2023 reforms represent a sufficient response to the problem.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Harassment and Stalking — S.10, S.10A NFOATP 1997
questions.text: Discuss the interaction between criminal law and civil law remedies in Irish harassment and stalking cases. In your answer, consider the role of safety orders and barring orders under domestic violence legislation alongside the criminal law provisions and assess whether the combined framework adequately protects victims.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Rape — Definition, Honest Belief ABOLISHED, and Consent
questions.text: Define rape under Irish law with reference to the Criminal Law (Rape) Act 1981 and the Criminal Law (Rape) (Amendment) Act 1990. In your answer, explain the actus reus and the mental element, explain how the concept of consent is defined and applied, and discuss the abolition of the honest belief defence under the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Rape — Definition, Honest Belief ABOLISHED, and Consent
questions.text: Explain how consent is defined and vitiated under the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017. In your answer, discuss the statutory circumstances in which consent cannot be given (e.g., incapacity, force, fraud, sleep), explain what is meant by 'affirmative consent', and critically assess whether the 2017 Act adequately protects complainants.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Rape — Definition, Honest Belief ABOLISHED, and Consent
questions.text: Critically examine the abolition of the 'honest belief' defence in rape cases under the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017. In your answer, explain the old law as established in People (DPP) v C [2001], discuss the legislative change, and consider whether the new objective standard for belief in consent strikes the right balance between the rights of accused persons and complainants.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Sexual Assault and Rape Under Section 4
questions.text: Explain the offence of sexual assault under s.2 of the Criminal Law (Rape) (Amendment) Act 1990. In your answer, define what constitutes a 'sexual assault', explain the mental element, and discuss how the courts determine whether an assault is 'sexual' in character with reference to relevant case law.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Sexual Assault and Rape Under Section 4
questions.text: Explain the offence of rape under section 4 (aggravated sexual assault) as created by the Criminal Law (Rape) (Amendment) Act 1990. In your answer, set out the elements of the offence, explain how it differs from rape at common law and from sexual assault, and discuss the sentencing framework.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Sexual Assault and Rape Under Section 4
questions.text: Discuss the procedural and evidential protections available to complainants in sexual offence trials in Ireland. In your answer, consider the restrictions on cross-examination concerning prior sexual history under s.3 of the Criminal Law (Rape) Act 1981, the anonymity provisions, and any recent legislative or judicial developments.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Sexual Offences Against Children and Protected Persons
questions.text: Explain the principal sexual offences against children under the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006 and the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017. In your answer, explain the offence of defilement under ss.3 and 4 of the 2006 Act, discuss the strict liability nature of the age element, and consider whether this is justified.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Sexual Offences Against Children and Protected Persons
questions.text: Explain the concept of a 'protected person' under the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 1993 and the offences created to protect such persons. In your answer, define who qualifies as a protected person, set out the offences, and discuss the rationale for the additional protections provided.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Sexual Offences Against Children and Protected Persons
questions.text: Critically assess the adequacy of Irish law in combating child sexual exploitation online. In your answer, discuss the offences of child grooming and solicitation under the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017, the offences relating to child pornography under the Child Trafficking and Pornography Act 1998, and whether the current legislative framework keeps pace with technological developments.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Define theft under s.4 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. In your answer, explain each element of the offence — appropriation, property belonging to another, dishonesty, and intention to permanently deprive — and illustrate each with relevant case law where available.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the offence of obtaining by deception under s.6 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. In your answer, define what constitutes a 'deception', explain the mental element, and discuss how this offence differs from theft.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Discuss the offences of fraud and false accounting under the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. In your answer, explain the elements of each offence, consider the concept of dishonesty, and assess how the 2001 Act addressed gaps in the earlier law on deception-based offences.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Define burglary under s.12 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. In your answer, explain the two forms of burglary, the concept of 'building or part of a building', the mental element, and how burglary differs from mere trespass or theft.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the offence of aggravated burglary under s.13 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. In your answer, set out the elements of the offence, explain what constitutes a 'weapon of offence', and discuss how this offence differs from burglary in terms of both the actus reus and the sentencing regime.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Critically assess the law on robbery under s.14 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. In your answer, explain the elements of robbery, the requirement of force or threat of force, and how robbery is distinguished from theft and extortion.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Handling Stolen Goods, Criminal Damage, and Cybercrime
questions.text: Explain the offence of handling stolen property under s.17 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. In your answer, explain what constitutes 'stolen property', set out the forms of handling, and discuss the mental element including the significance of 'knowing or believing' the property to be stolen.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Handling Stolen Goods, Criminal Damage, and Cybercrime
questions.text: Explain the offences created by the Criminal Damage Act 1991. In your answer, define 'damage', explain the principal offence under s.2, discuss the aggravated offence under s.2(2) (arson and endangerment), and explain the relevance of lawful excuse.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Handling Stolen Goods, Criminal Damage, and Cybercrime
questions.text: Discuss the principal cybercrime offences under Irish law, including offences under the Criminal Damage Act 1991, the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001, and any relevant EU-derived legislation. In your answer, explain how the existing framework applies to hacking, unauthorised access, and online fraud, and assess whether Irish law is adequate to address modern cybercrime.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Aid, Abet, Counsel, Procure — and Accessories
questions.text: Explain the law on secondary participation in crime in Ireland under s.7 of the Criminal Law Act 1997. In your answer, define 'aiding', 'abetting', 'counselling', and 'procuring', explain the mental element required for each, and discuss how secondary participation differs from principal offending.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Aid, Abet, Counsel, Procure — and Accessories
questions.text: Explain the offence of being an accessory after the fact at common law as preserved by s.7(2) of the Criminal Law Act 1997. In your answer, set out the elements of the offence, the mental element, and how this differs from being a principal in the second degree.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Aid, Abet, Counsel, Procure — and Accessories
questions.text: Discuss the mental element required for secondary liability in Irish criminal law. In your answer, consider whether knowledge of the principal's intention is sufficient, assess the decision in DPP v Cawley [2010] IECCA, and critically consider the tension between the 'procures' limb and the requirement for a causal connection between the secondary party's conduct and the commission of the offence.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Common Design, Joint Enterprise, and Withdrawal
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of common design (or joint enterprise) in Irish criminal law. In your answer, explain how liability may be imposed on a party to a common design for acts committed by a co-participant, set out the conditions under which the co-participant's acts will be within the scope of the agreement, and illustrate with relevant Irish case law.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Common Design, Joint Enterprise, and Withdrawal
questions.text: Critically assess the scope of joint enterprise liability in Ireland in light of the Supreme Court of the United Kingdom's decision in R v Jogee [2016] UKSC 8. In your answer, explain the pre-Jogee 'parasitic accessory liability' doctrine, the change effected by Jogee, and discuss whether Irish law should adopt or has adopted a similar approach.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Common Design, Joint Enterprise, and Withdrawal
questions.text: Explain the law on withdrawal from a common design or joint enterprise in Irish criminal law. In your answer, explain what constitutes an effective withdrawal, the steps required to communicate and neutralise one's prior assistance, and assess whether the current law on withdrawal strikes the right balance.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the offence of conspiracy in Irish criminal law. In your answer, define conspiracy, explain the mental element required, discuss the rule that an agreement between two or more persons is sufficient (whether or not the substantive offence is committed), and consider how the law treats the 'impossibility' argument.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the offence of incitement in Irish criminal law. In your answer, define incitement, explain the mental element, and discuss how the law deals with unsuccessful incitement where the incitee does not carry out the incited offence.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the law of criminal attempt under s.25 of the Criminal Justice Act 1994 and at common law. In your answer, explain the actus reus of attempt (the proximity test), the mental element, and critically assess the rule on impossibility as it applies to attempted crimes, with reference to the relevant Irish and English case law.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the defence of duress in Irish criminal law. In your answer, set out the elements of the defence — an immediate threat of death or serious harm, directed at the accused or a person for whom the accused is responsible, which a sober person of reasonable firmness could not resist — and discuss the limits of the defence, including whether it is available to a charge of murder.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the law on self-defence and defence of others in Ireland under s.18 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997. In your answer, explain the conditions under which the use of force is justified, the requirement of proportionality, and the position of a person who uses excessive force or makes a mistake as to the need for self-defence.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Critically compare the defences of duress and necessity in Irish criminal law. In your answer, explain how necessity operates at common law and assess whether Irish law should recognise a broader defence of necessity. Consider the decision in R v Dudley & Stephens (1884) and assess how Irish courts might approach a similar scenario.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the defence of the dwelling under s.2 of the Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011. In your answer, explain the statutory presumption introduced by the Act, define what constitutes a 'dwelling', explain when the use of force is permitted, and discuss the limits of the defence.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Critically assess the Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011. In your answer, consider the policy justifications for the Act, the conditions under which force (including lethal force) may be used against an intruder, and whether the Act strikes an appropriate balance between the rights of homeowners and the rights of intruders.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between the defence of the dwelling under the 2011 Act and the defence of self-defence under s.18 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997. In your answer, explain how these defences interact, identify any overlaps, and consider which provides the stronger protection to a homeowner who uses force against an intruder.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: DVA 2018, Coercive Control, and the Coco Act
questions.text: Explain the offence of coercive control under s.39 of the Domestic Violence Act 2018. In your answer, define 'coercive control', explain what conduct falls within the offence, discuss the mental element, and critically assess whether this offence adequately addresses the lived experience of victims in controlling or coercive relationships.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: DVA 2018, Coercive Control, and the Coco Act
questions.text: Explain the principal civil remedies available to victims of domestic violence under the Domestic Violence Act 2018, including safety orders, barring orders, and emergency barring orders. In your answer, set out the conditions for making each order, the persons who may apply, and the legal consequences of breach.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: DVA 2018, Coercive Control, and the Coco Act
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between the criminal law provisions of the Domestic Violence Act 2018 and the Harassment, Harmful Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 (Coco Act) in addressing harmful and controlling behaviour. In your answer, explain the key offences under the Coco Act — including sharing intimate images without consent — and assess whether the combined framework adequately protects victims.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the principal public order offences under the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994. In your answer, set out the elements of the offences of threatening, abusive, or insulting behaviour (s.6), violent disorder (s.15), riot (s.14), and affray (s.16), and explain how these offences differ from each other.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Discuss the offence of intoxication in a public place under s.4 of the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 and the related Garda powers under ss.8 and 9. In your answer, explain the powers of arrest, direction, and exclusion available to Gardaí, and assess whether these powers strike an appropriate balance between public order and civil liberties.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Critically assess the adequacy of Irish public order law in dealing with online harassment and harmful communications. In your answer, consider the extent to which the traditional public order offences under the 1994 Act apply to online conduct and the extent to which the Harassment, Harmful Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 fills any gaps.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Offences Against Justice and State Offences
questions.text: Explain the offences of perjury, perverting the course of justice, and contempt of court in Irish criminal law. In your answer, set out the elements of each offence, explain the mental element required, and discuss how these offences protect the integrity of the criminal justice system.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Offences Against Justice and State Offences
questions.text: Explain the principal offences against the State under the Offences Against the State Act 1939 and the Offences Against the State (Amendment) Act 1998. In your answer, discuss the offences of membership of an unlawful organisation and the special evidentiary rules applicable in the Special Criminal Court.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Offences Against Justice and State Offences
questions.text: Critically assess the constitutionality and compatibility with the ECHR of the Special Criminal Court in Ireland. In your answer, discuss the rationale for its establishment, the departure from the ordinary jury trial requirement under Article 38.5 of the Constitution, and the leading case law on this issue including Kavanagh v Ireland (2001).

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the courts structure for the trial of criminal offences in Ireland, including the District Court, Circuit Court, Central Criminal Court, and Special Criminal Court. In your answer, explain the jurisdiction of each court, the constitutional basis for their establishment, and the significance of Article 38 of the Constitution.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the law on arrest in Ireland. In your answer, distinguish between arrest with and without warrant, explain the power of arrest without warrant under s.4 of the Criminal Law Act 1997, discuss the requirement that an arrestee be informed of the reason for their arrest, and explain the consequences of an unlawful arrest.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.text: Explain the powers of detention and questioning of suspects under s.4 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984. In your answer, set out the periods of detention permitted, the rights of the detained person (including access to a solicitor and notification of a third party), and the consequences of a breach of the Custody Regulations.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Bail and the Right to Silence
questions.text: Explain the law on bail in Ireland following the Twenty-Fourth Amendment of the Constitution (Article 40.4.6°) and the Bail Act 1997. In your answer, explain the grounds on which bail may be refused (including the risk of committing a serious offence), discuss the constitutional right to bail, and explain the procedure for applying for bail.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Bail and the Right to Silence
questions.text: Explain the right to silence in Irish criminal law and assess the extent to which this right has been modified by s.2 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984, s.5 of the Criminal Justice Act 2007, and related provisions. In your answer, consider the effect of adverse inferences from silence on the accused's right to a fair trial under Article 38.1 of the Constitution and Article 6 ECHR.

questions.subject: Criminal Law
questions.lessonTitle: Bail and the Right to Silence
questions.text: Discuss the constitutional right to a fair trial under Article 38.1 of the Constitution. In your answer, explain the principal components of the right (including the right to disclosure, the right to cross-examine witnesses, and the right to legal representation), discuss the circumstances in which a trial may be stayed as an abuse of process, and identify any recent judicial developments.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: Equity acts in personam, not in rem. Critically examine this maxim and three others of your choice, explaining their historical origins, their doctrinal significance, and illustrating with Irish and English case law how courts have applied them in modern equity. In particular, assess whether the maxims remain meaningful practical guides or have become mere rhetorical devices.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: 'He who comes to equity must come with clean hands.' Critically discuss this maxim with reference to the case law, examining its scope, the test for 'unclean hands' in Irish equity, and whether the defence can be invoked by defendants who are themselves in breach of equitable obligations. Refer to Gonthier v Orange Contract Scaffolding and any relevant Irish authorities.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: Thomas has been in a long-standing dispute with his neighbour Patrick over a right of way. Thomas now seeks an injunction to prevent Patrick from obstructing the right of way, but Patrick argues that Thomas himself blocked the same right of way for several months two years ago. Advise on: (i) the maxim 'he who comes to equity must come with clean hands'; (ii) whether Thomas's prior conduct disentitles him to equitable relief; (iii) the maxim 'delay defeats equity' (laches); and (iv) whether Thomas should instead rely on a common law remedy.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Three Certainties: Certainty of Intention and Subject Matter
questions.text: Knight v Knight (1840) established that a valid express trust requires certainty of intention, certainty of subject matter, and certainty of objects. Critically examine certainty of intention and certainty of subject matter, explaining the distinction between a trust and a moral obligation, the significance of precatory words in Irish and English case law, and the 'tangible segregation' requirement for certainty of subject matter illustrated in Hunter v Moss and Re London Wine Co.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Three Certainties: Certainty of Intention and Subject Matter
questions.text: A testator leaves a will stating: 'I leave my investments to my daughter in the hope and confidence that she will use them to benefit other family members.' The daughter keeps all assets for herself. Advise on: (i) whether the words 'hope and confidence' create a trust (certainty of intention); (ii) the contrast between precatory words and imperative trust language; (iii) the relevance of Re Adams and the Kensington Vestry and Paul v Constance; and (iv) whether a constructive trust could arise on the facts.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Three Certainties: Certainty of Intention and Subject Matter
questions.text: Alan transfers €100,000 to his solicitor saying 'hold this on trust for the benefit of my children and such other persons as I may decide later.' Advise on: (i) whether there is sufficient certainty of intention; (ii) whether 'such other persons as I may decide later' satisfies certainty of subject matter or objects; (iii) the distinction between a trust and a power; and (iv) whether the trust fails and what would happen to the money if it does.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Three Certainties: Certainty of Objects
questions.text: The test for certainty of objects differs depending on whether the trust is a fixed trust or a discretionary trust. Critically examine both tests — the 'complete list' test for fixed trusts and the 'is or is not' test for discretionary trusts established in McPhail v Doulton — explaining the rationale for the distinction and assessing how subsequent case law (Re Baden No. 2) has addressed conceptual and evidential uncertainty.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Three Certainties: Certainty of Objects
questions.text: A testator leaves property on trust 'for the benefit of my old friends and former work colleagues.' Advise on: (i) whether the class of beneficiaries satisfies the McPhail v Doulton test for discretionary trust certainty of objects; (ii) the distinction between conceptual uncertainty (uncertain meaning of class description) and evidential uncertainty (difficulty in identifying members); (iii) whether Re Baden No. 2's 'semantic or linguistic certainty' approach assists the trustee; and (iv) what happens to the trust property if the trust fails for uncertainty.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Three Certainties: Certainty of Objects
questions.text: SoundWorks Ltd establishes a fund for 'the benefit of employees, ex-employees and their dependants' to be distributed at the trustee's discretion. An ex-employee, Brigid, who retired fifteen years ago argues she should be included. The current trustees doubt whether she falls within the class. Advise on: (i) the applicable test for certainty of objects in a discretionary trust following McPhail v Doulton; (ii) whether 'ex-employees' and 'dependants' are sufficiently certain; (iii) the administrative workability test from Re Gulbenkian; and (iv) whether the trustee has the power to seek directions from the court.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Constitution of Trusts: The Rule in Milroy v Lord
questions.text: The rule in Milroy v Lord (1862) establishes that equity will not assist a volunteer and will not perfect an imperfect gift. Critically examine this rule, explaining Turner LJ's dictum, the requirements for a complete constitution of a trust, and the different modes of constituting a trust by transfer to trustees. In your answer, examine how the rule applies to the three modes of disposition identified in Milroy v Lord.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Constitution of Trusts: The Rule in Milroy v Lord
questions.text: A settlor purports to transfer shares in a private company to trustees for the benefit of her nephew by instructing her stockbroker verbally, without completing the requisite stock transfer form. The nephew seeks to enforce the trust. Advise on: (i) whether the trust has been validly constituted under Milroy v Lord; (ii) the requirement that a settler must do everything in their power to transfer legal title; (iii) the rule in Re Rose (1952) and whether anything done before the form was completed could constitute; and (iv) whether Pennington v Waine could assist the nephew on the facts.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Constitution of Trusts: The Rule in Milroy v Lord
questions.text: Discuss the extent to which the courts have developed exceptions to or mitigations of the rule in Milroy v Lord, with particular reference to: (i) the rule in Re Rose and its extension in Mascall v Mascall; (ii) the donatio mortis causa exception; (iii) the rule in Strong v Bird; and (iv) the unconscionability principle from Pennington v Waine. Critically assess whether these exceptions have significantly undermined the coherence of Milroy v Lord.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Constitution of Trusts: Exceptions and 'Equity Will Perfect'
questions.text: The rule in Strong v Bird (1874) provides that where a testator has appointed the intended donee as executor and previously manifested an intention to give property to that donee, the appointment as executor perfects the imperfect gift. Critically examine this rule, the requirement of continuing intention, and the Irish application of this rule, and assess whether Re Stewart's extension of the rule to administrators as well as executors is doctrinally sound.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Constitution of Trusts: Exceptions and 'Equity Will Perfect'
questions.text: Donatio mortis causa is a gift made in contemplation of death that is conditional on the donor dying. Critically examine the three requirements for a valid donatio mortis causa as identified in Cain v Moon — contemplation of impending death, a gift conditional on death, and delivery or dominion over the subject matter — and assess how the courts have approached the delivery requirement, with reference to Sen v Headley and the Irish authorities.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Constitution of Trusts: Exceptions and 'Equity Will Perfect'
questions.text: Brian, a terminally ill man, hands his car keys to his sister Máire and says: 'If I don't make it through this operation, the car is yours.' He also gives her a sealed envelope containing his bank account details and a note saying 'all my savings too.' Brian dies following the operation. His executor challenges both transactions. Advise on: (i) whether either transaction constitutes a valid donatio mortis causa; (ii) whether delivery of a car by handing over the keys satisfies the delivery requirement; (iii) whether bank account details constitute sufficient 'delivery' following Sen v Headley; and (iv) whether the rule in Strong v Bird could assist Máire if she is appointed executor.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Secret Trusts: Fully Secret and Half-Secret Trusts
questions.text: Secret trusts are obligations imposed on a legatee or devisee who, having agreed to hold property for the benefit of a third party, would otherwise keep it beneficially. Critically examine the distinction between fully secret trusts and half-secret trusts, explaining the requirements for each type, the fraud theory and the dehors the will theory as competing bases for their enforcement, and the significance of communication and acceptance in each case.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Secret Trusts: Fully Secret and Half-Secret Trusts
questions.text: A testatrix leaves €200,000 by will 'to my solicitor, Brian, absolutely.' Before making the will, she had telephoned Brian and told him she intended to leave money to him in her will to be held for her estranged daughter, Orla. Brian said nothing in reply. She died without having any further conversation with him. Advise on: (i) whether a fully secret trust has been validly created; (ii) the requirements for communication and acceptance; (iii) whether Brian's silence constitutes acceptance; and (iv) whether it makes any difference that the communication occurred before the will was made.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Secret Trusts: Fully Secret and Half-Secret Trusts
questions.text: A testator leaves €500,000 'to my trustees on trust, the terms of which have been communicated to them.' Unknown to the beneficiaries, the trustees were given a letter the day before the testator died setting out that the money was to go to the testator's illegitimate child. The trustees dispute the validity of the half-secret trust. Advise on: (i) the requirements for a valid half-secret trust; (ii) the requirement that communication must occur before or at the time of the will; (iii) the significance of Re Keen (1937) on consistency between will and communication; and (iv) whether the half-secret trust fails and what happens to the money.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: Mutual wills are wills made by two testators in pursuance of an agreement to dispose of their property in a particular way. Critically examine the equitable doctrine of mutual wills, explaining the requirements for the doctrine to apply, the nature of the constructive trust that arises on the death of the first testator, and the extent to which the surviving testator is bound by the agreement. Refer to Re Goodchild and Olins v Walters.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: Margaret and James are a married couple who each make wills leaving all property to the other and then, on the second death, to their children equally. James dies first. Margaret subsequently cohabitates with a new partner and makes a new will leaving everything to her partner, excluding the children. The children bring an action in equity. Advise on: (i) whether the wills constitute mutual wills; (ii) what evidence is required to establish an agreement to make mutual wills; (iii) whether Margaret is bound by the agreement after James's death; and (iv) the nature and extent of the constructive trust imposed on Margaret's estate.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: Critically examine how the courts determine whether two wills constitute mutual wills rather than mirror wills.
