In your answer, assess: (i) the significance of an express agreement compared with an implied agreement; (ii) whether it is the agreement or the death of the first testator that gives rise to the constructive trust; (iii) the effect on third parties who receive property from the surviving testator's estate without notice of the mutual wills agreement; and (iv) whether the doctrine of mutual wills is well suited to modern family structures.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trustees' Duties: Investment and the Prudent Trustee Standard
questions.text: The primary duty of a trustee is to invest trust property. Critically examine the duty to invest under Irish trust law, explaining the historical contrast between the traditional restrictive list of authorised investments and the modern prudent person/prudent investor standard. Assess how the Trustee Act 1893 (as amended) and the Trustee Act 2000 (England) have modified the standard, and evaluate whether the 'prudent person investing for another' test adequately protects beneficiaries.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trustees' Duties: Investment and the Prudent Trustee Standard
questions.text: A trustee invests the entire trust fund in a single technology company, arguing it appeared a sound investment at the time. The company subsequently goes insolvent. The beneficiaries sue the trustee for breach of the investment duty. Advise on: (i) the duty to diversify; (ii) whether the concentration of the fund in a single asset constitutes a breach of the prudent trustee standard; (iii) the standard of care expected — is it objective or does it take account of the trustee's specific expertise?; and (iv) the measure of compensation available to the beneficiaries.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trustees' Duties: Investment and the Prudent Trustee Standard
questions.text: The trustees of a charitable trust for the advancement of education are considering whether to invest in fossil fuel companies that offer high returns, despite pressure from beneficiaries to adopt an ethical investment policy. Advise on: (i) the primary duty to maximise return for the benefit of current and future beneficiaries; (ii) whether trustees may adopt an ethical investment policy that may reduce returns; (iii) the English guidance in Cowan v Scargill and the Charity Commission's ethical investment guidance; and (iv) whether the position would differ if the charity's own objects are undermined by the proposed investments.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trustees' Duties: Duty Not to Profit and the Rule Against Self-Dealing
questions.text: A trustee must not profit from the trust. Critically examine the no-profit rule and the rule against self-dealing in Irish and English equity, explaining the strict liability nature of both rules, the basis for relief (authorisation, disclosure, and beneficiary consent), and the leading cases including Keech v Sandford, Boardman v Phipps, and Bray v Ford. Assess whether the strict application of the no-profit rule remains justified in modern commercial trust contexts.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trustees' Duties: Duty Not to Profit and the Rule Against Self-Dealing
questions.text: One of three trustees, Claire, is also a solicitor. She acts as solicitor for the trust, charging professional fees, without the prior approval of her co-trustees or the beneficiaries. She also negotiates the renewal of a lease on a trust property and, believing the trust cannot afford to renew, obtains the lease in her own name at a competitive rent. Advise on: (i) whether Claire's professional fees constitute a breach of the no-profit rule; (ii) whether any exception for professional trustees applies; (iii) whether her taking of the lease opportunity constitutes a breach of the rule against self-dealing; and (iv) whether the beneficiaries can trace the trust's interest into the lease.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trustees' Duties: Duty Not to Profit and the Rule Against Self-Dealing
questions.text: Boardman v Phipps [1967] established that a trustee (or even a fiduciary agent) who makes a profit using information obtained in a fiduciary capacity holds that profit on constructive trust. Critically assess the scope and limits of this principle, examining in particular: (i) whether the rule extends to all fiduciaries or only express trustees; (ii) the principle of unauthorised profit versus unauthorised use of trust information; (iii) the allowance for work done (quantum meruit in equity); and (iv) whether the result in Boardman was just given that the trust itself could not have exploited the information.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trustees' Powers: Delegation, Advancement and Maintenance
questions.text: Trustees have traditionally been required to act personally (delegatus non potest delegare). Critically examine the evolution of the duty not to delegate in trust law, explaining the circumstances in which delegation is now permitted under modern trust legislation, the obligations imposed on trustees when delegating investment management to agents, and the liability consequences when an agent exceeds their mandate.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trustees' Powers: Delegation, Advancement and Maintenance
questions.text: The power of advancement under section 32 of the Trustee Act 1925 (Ireland equivalent) allows trustees to apply trust capital for the benefit of a beneficiary with a vested or contingent interest. Critically examine this power, explaining the 'benefit' requirement (Re Pauling's Settlement Trusts), the extent to which advancement can be used for tax planning purposes (Pilkington v IRC), and the need to account for advancements when the contingency is satisfied.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trustees' Powers: Delegation, Advancement and Maintenance
questions.text: Maria, aged 17, is a beneficiary of a discretionary trust established for her benefit until she reaches 25. The trust fund is worth €500,000. Her guardian asks the trustees to: (i) pay her school fees of €15,000 per year from income (statutory power of maintenance); and (ii) advance €50,000 from capital to fund a business training course that Maria wishes to attend. Advise the trustees on: (a) the scope of the statutory power of maintenance and whether income payments for school fees fall within it; (b) the power of advancement, including whether a business training course constitutes 'benefit'; (c) any conditions the trustees should attach to the advancement; and (d) the accounting requirement if Maria later takes capital at age 25.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: Resulting trusts arise in two categories: presumed resulting trusts (where the presumption of resulting trust arises from voluntary conveyance or purchase money contribution) and automatic resulting trusts (where a beneficial interest is not exhausted). Critically examine both categories, explaining the basis for each, the distinction between them as analysed by Lord Browne-Wilkinson in Westdeutsche Landesbank v Islington, and their application in Irish law.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: A husband purchases a house in the sole name of his wife using his own money. He claims the house is held on resulting trust for him. The wife argues the purchase was a gift to her. Advise on: (i) the presumption of resulting trust; (ii) the presumption of advancement in the marital context, and whether it survives following Stack v Dowden and Pettitt v Pettitt; (iii) the rebuttal of either presumption by evidence of intention; and (iv) whether the position under section 44 of the Family Law Act 1995 (Ireland) affects the resulting trust analysis.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: A trust is established for charitable purposes. After five years, all the objects of the trust are achieved and a surplus of €300,000 remains. The trustees apply to court for directions. Advise on: (i) whether an automatic resulting trust arises in favour of the settlor; (ii) the cy-près doctrine and whether it is applicable before considering resulting trust; (iii) the distinction between initial failure and subsequent failure of charitable purposes; and (iv) whether a resulting trust could arise in favour of the settlor's estate if the settlor has died.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Constructive Trusts: Categories and Common Intention
questions.text: Constructive trusts arise in a wide variety of circumstances in equity — from the vendor/purchaser trust, to the trustee's unauthorised profit, to the common intention constructive trust in family property disputes. Critically examine at least three categories of constructive trust, explaining the factual triggers for each, and assess whether there is any unifying principle linking these categories or whether the constructive trust is simply an equitable remedy imposed whenever unconscionability demands it.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Constructive Trusts: Categories and Common Intention
questions.text: In disputes over the beneficial ownership of property held in one partner's name, the courts have distinguished between express common intention constructive trusts (evidenced by express agreement) and inferred common intention constructive trusts (inferred from conduct). Critically examine the development of the common intention constructive trust in English law through Lloyds Bank v Rosset, Stack v Dowden, and Jones v Kernott, and assess whether the Irish courts have followed a similar approach in the context of co-habiting couples.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Constructive Trusts: Categories and Common Intention
questions.text: John and Mary cohabited for eight years. The house they lived in was purchased in John's name alone. Mary contributed €30,000 to the purchase price, paid the household bills for the full eight years, and made structural improvements to the property. When they separate, Mary claims a beneficial interest in the house. Advise on: (i) whether a common intention constructive trust arises; (ii) the significance of direct financial contribution versus indirect contribution to mortgage payments; (iii) whether the Irish courts would follow Stack v Dowden or maintain a more rigid approach; and (iv) whether Mary alternatively has a claim based on proprietary estoppel.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: Proprietary estoppel arises where an owner of land encourages or acquiesces in another's belief that they have or will have an interest in the land, and that other person acts to their detriment in reliance on that belief. Critically examine the elements of proprietary estoppel as stated in Taylor Fashions v Liverpool Victoria Trustees and as applied in Inwards v Baker and Gillett v Holt, and assess the Irish authorities on the doctrine.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: A father tells his adult son: 'Work on the farm for the rest of my life and I'll leave you the farm in my will.' The son works on the farm for twenty years for minimal wages, turning down other career opportunities. The father dies without making a will and the farm passes to the son's sister under intestacy rules. Advise on: (i) whether all three elements of proprietary estoppel — assurance, reliance, and detriment — are satisfied; (ii) whether the assurance must be sufficiently specific; (iii) how the courts exercise their discretion in fashioning a remedy, from conferring the full fee simple to monetary compensation (Jennings v Rice); and (iv) the Irish position on discretionary remediation.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.text: Compare and contrast proprietary estoppel with: (i) the common intention constructive trust; (ii) the doctrine of part performance in the context of land contracts; and (iii) a contractual licence. In each comparison, identify the key doctrinal differences, including the availability of remedies and the consequences for third party purchasers of the land.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Breach of Trust: Personal Liability and Defences
questions.text: A trustee who commits a breach of trust is personally liable to restore the trust fund or compensate for the loss. Critically examine the nature of personal liability for breach of trust under Irish equity, explaining how the measure of compensation differs from a common law damages award, the joint and several liability of co-trustees, and the circumstances in which a trustee can be indemnified by a co-trustee or by a beneficiary who consented to or requested the breach.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Breach of Trust: Personal Liability and Defences
questions.text: The trustees of a family trust invest trust funds in a private company owned by one of the trustees without obtaining independent advice or the prior approval of the beneficiaries. The company subsequently fails, and the trust fund loses €400,000. Advise on: (i) the nature of the breach of trust; (ii) the personal liability of the investing trustee and the liability of any co-trustee who failed to object; (iii) the 'but for' causation test applied in Target Holdings v Redferns; (iv) any defences available including section 61 of the Trustee Act 1925 (exoneration by court) and the consent defence.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Breach of Trust: Personal Liability and Defences
questions.text: Critically examine the defences available to a trustee facing a breach of trust claim, with particular reference to: (i) the statutory defence of honest and reasonable conduct under section 61 of the Trustee Act 1925 (Ireland equivalent); (ii) the beneficiary's consent or acquiescence; (iii) the limitation period for breach of trust claims; (iv) the Limitation Act 1957 (Ireland) and its application to fraudulent breach of trust; and (v) the exclusion of liability clauses in trust instruments and whether they can be relied upon to exclude liability for fraud.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Third-Party Liability: Knowing Receipt and Dishonest Assistance
questions.text: A third party who receives trust property in breach of trust may be personally liable in equity under the doctrine of knowing receipt. Critically examine the elements of knowing receipt, explaining the knowledge requirement from Baden v Société Générale, the controversy over whether subjective or objective dishonesty should be the standard (Bank of Credit and Commerce International (Overseas) Ltd v Akindele), and whether the better approach is a test based on unconscionability.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Third-Party Liability: Knowing Receipt and Dishonest Assistance
questions.text: A solicitor, acting on behalf of a client, assists in transferring trust assets to an offshore company, having received advice from a financial consultant that the transaction was 'probably legitimate' without making further enquiry. The solicitor then receives a significant payment for his services. Advise on: (i) whether the solicitor is liable for dishonest assistance under Royal Brunei Airlines v Tan; (ii) the objective dishonesty standard; (iii) whether the solicitor's reliance on advice he did not verify is sufficient to negate dishonesty; and (iv) whether he is also liable under knowing receipt.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Third-Party Liability: Knowing Receipt and Dishonest Assistance
questions.text: Compare and contrast dishonest assistance and knowing receipt as forms of accessory/recipient liability in equity. In your answer, address: (i) the different elements of each claim; (ii) whether a trust must be shown to have been breached in both cases; (iii) the different remedies available — personal liability versus constructive trust; (iv) the current controversy over the standard of fault required for knowing receipt; and (v) whether the English and Irish courts have adopted a consistent approach.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Injunctions: Types and the Campus Oil Test
questions.text: Campus Oil Ltd v Minister for Industry and Energy [1983] established the test for granting interlocutory injunctions in Ireland. Critically examine the three-stage Campus Oil test — fair question to be tried, balance of convenience, and status quo preservation — and compare it with the English American Cyanamid test from which it departs. In your answer, explain why the Irish Supreme Court in Campus Oil modified the Cyanamid approach and assess whether the Irish test adequately balances the parties' competing interests pending trial.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Injunctions: Types and the Campus Oil Test
questions.text:
MediaCo successfully obtains a judgment for €800,000 against AdFraud Ltd after a fraud. MediaCo discovers that AdFraud has begun transferring assets to accounts in Gibraltar and has listed its Dublin office building for sale. MediaCo seeks a Mareva injunction to freeze AdFraud's assets pending enforcement.
AdFraud argues:
- There is no real risk of dissipation
- A Mareva would damage its legitimate business operations
- MediaCo has delayed in bringing the application
Discuss:
1. The requirements for a Mareva injunction
2. Whether the facts disclose a sufficient risk of dissipation
3. The balance of convenience
4. The impact of any delay by MediaCo
Reference Mareva Compania Naviera SA v International Bulkcarriers, Babanaft International v Bassatne, and O'Mahony v Horgan.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Injunctions: Types and the Campus Oil Test
questions.text: TechStart Ltd has developed a valuable software product over four years, incorporating trade secrets. A former employee, Dan, has left to establish a competing business and TechStart believes Dan is using its proprietary source code. TechStart seeks an Anton Piller (search) order to enter Dan's business premises to search for and seize the code before it can be destroyed. Advise on: (i) the three requirements for an Anton Piller order — strong prima facie case, serious potential damage, clear evidence of possession and risk of destruction; (ii) the procedural requirements for ex parte applications; (iii) the undertaking in damages; and (iv) whether the mandatory interlocutory injunction threshold (strong prima facie case) applies rather than the Campus Oil fair question standard.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Specific Performance and Other Equitable Remedies
questions.text: Specific performance is a discretionary equitable remedy compelling a party to perform their contractual obligations. Critically examine the circumstances in which specific performance will be granted, the requirement that damages must be inadequate, the categories of contract specifically enforceable as of course (land, unique goods) and those that will not be enforced (personal service contracts, contracts requiring constant supervision), and the principles governing the court's discretion.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Specific Performance and Other Equitable Remedies
questions.text: GreenField Ltd contracts to sell a 50-acre development site to HomeBuild Ltd for €5 million. At the date for completion, GreenField refuses to complete, claiming it received a higher offer. HomeBuild sues for specific performance. GreenField argues: (i) that HomeBuild has failed to act promptly in bringing proceedings; (ii) that HomeBuild itself was in breach of a minor term of the contract; and (iii) that HomeBuild has 'unclean hands' because it used confidential information about the site's development potential obtained from GreenField's former employee. Advise HomeBuild on: the availability of specific performance for a contract for the sale of land; the adequacy of damages defence; and each of GreenField's three arguments.

questions.subject: Equity Law
questions.lessonTitle: Specific Performance and Other Equitable Remedies
questions.text: Critically compare specific performance with rectification, rescission, and account of profits as equitable remedies, explaining: (i) the circumstances in which each remedy is available; (ii) the discretionary nature of each remedy and the bars to relief; (iii) whether equitable remedies can be claimed alongside common law damages; and (iv) the relationship between equitable compensation and common law damages following Target Holdings v Redferns and AIB Group v Mark Redler.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: In the aftermath of the Second World War, six founding states signed the Treaty of Paris 1951 and the Treaty of Rome 1957. Critically examine the political and economic motivations that drove European integration in this period and assess the extent to which the Schuman Declaration of 1950 can be regarded as the founding constitutional moment of the European project.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Jean Monnet described European integration as a process and not a finished product. Analyse the key institutional and legal developments from the ECSC to the EEC and assess how the concept of 'ever closer union' has evolved from a political aspiration into a justiciable legal principle in the case law of the Court of Justice.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Declan, an Irish law student, argues that the European Communities Act 1972 was sufficient to give EU law its present authority in Ireland and that no constitutional amendment was ever necessary. His tutor disagrees. Advise on the constitutional basis for Ireland's membership of the EU, the role of the Third and Tenth Amendments to the Constitution, and whether Declan's analysis is correct.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The Single European Act 1986, the Maastricht Treaty 1992, and the Lisbon Treaty 2007 each represented a qualitative shift in the constitutional architecture of the EU. Critically compare any two of these treaties, analysing what each added in terms of institutional structure, competence, and the legal rights of citizens.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The Treaty on European Union (TEU) and the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU) now form the constitutional bedrock of the Union. Assess the significance of the Lisbon Treaty in consolidating and clarifying the EU's legal framework, with particular reference to the conferral principle in Article 5 TEU, the distinction between exclusive and shared competences, and the introduction of a formal exit clause.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Siobhán, a constitutional law academic, argues that the Lisbon Treaty completed the constitutionalisation of the EU without openly declaring it a constitution, primarily because the Constitutional Treaty was rejected in 2005. Critically examine this argument, assessing the extent to which the current Treaties perform a constitutional function in the legal order of the Union.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Article 50 TEU, introduced by the Lisbon Treaty, created the first formal mechanism for a member state to withdraw from the European Union. Critically examine the procedural requirements of Article 50 and assess the extent to which its interpretation by the Court of Justice and the UK Supreme Court in Miller clarified the conditions under which the right of withdrawal can be exercised.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The UK's withdrawal from the EU raised novel questions about the legal position of EU citizens in the UK and UK nationals in EU member states. Discuss the mechanism under which pre-Brexit rights of residence and work were protected, and assess the adequacy of the Withdrawal Agreement as a legal instrument for the protection of acquired rights under EU law.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Following Brexit, Patrick, an Irish solicitor practising in Dublin, advises a Northern Irish client who held dual Irish and UK citizenship and who had been relying on EU free movement rights to work in Germany. Advise Patrick on: (i) whether his client retains EU citizenship rights following Brexit; (ii) the significance of the Protocol on Ireland/Northern Ireland (the Windsor Framework); and (iii) how EU citizenship attaches to nationality of a member state under Article 20 TFEU.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The European Parliament has been progressively empowered from a purely consultative assembly to a co-equal co-legislator under the ordinary legislative procedure. Critically trace this development from the Treaty of Rome to the Lisbon Treaty and assess whether the Parliament can now be regarded as a genuinely democratic legislature within the EU's institutional framework.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Following the 2024 European Parliament elections, several political groups sought to form a majority coalition to elect the President of the European Commission. Explain the constitutional basis under the TEU for the Parliament's role in electing the Commission President, assess the significance of the 'Spitzenkandidat' process, and discuss whether Article 17(7) TEU genuinely constrains the European Council's nomination power.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: An NGO challenges the validity of an EU Regulation adopted under the ordinary legislative procedure, arguing that the Parliament's committee stage was procedurally defective because insufficient time was given for scrutiny of amendments. Advise on: (i) the stages of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 294 TFEU; (ii) the grounds on which acts may be annulled under Article 263 TFEU; and (iii) the standing of the NGO to bring a direct action.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Distinguish carefully between the Council of the European Union and the European Council. Examine the respective roles of each institution in the EU's legislative and executive processes, and assess whether the increased prominence of the European Council since Lisbon has disturbed the constitutional balance between the EU's institutions.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Qualified majority voting (QMV) under Article 16 TEU and Protocol No. 36 has become the default decision-making rule in the Council for most legislative business. Critically assess the significance of this shift away from unanimity, examining both the democratic gains in terms of legislative efficiency and the risks of smaller member states being consistently outvoted on matters affecting core national interests.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Ireland is a member of a blocking minority in the Council opposing a proposed Regulation on digital services taxation. Advise the Irish government on: (i) the conditions for a blocking minority under Article 16 TEU; (ii) the Ioannina Compromise mechanism; (iii) whether any subject matter falling under unanimous voting could be used to veto the proposal; and (iv) the distinction between legislative and non-legislative acts.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The European Commission is frequently described as the 'guardian of the Treaties' and the 'engine of integration'. Critically examine the institutional role of the Commission in the EU's constitutional order, addressing its monopoly on legislative initiative, its executive functions, and its accountability to the European Parliament under Article 17 TEU.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The Commission initiated infringement proceedings under Article 258 TFEU against Ireland for failing to implement an EU Directive on environmental standards within the transposition period. Explain the stages of the Article 258 procedure, examine the circumstances in which the Court of Justice may impose financial penalties under Article 260 TFEU, and assess Ireland's options to contest the proceedings.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: A newly appointed Commissioner for Internal Market faces pressure from her member state's government to favour national industries in the internal market regulatory agenda. Advise on: (i) the independence obligations of Commissioners under Article 17(3) TEU; (ii) the procedure for removing individual Commissioners; (iii) the collective responsibility of the Commission; and (iv) the circumstances in which a motion of censure can be brought by the European Parliament.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The Court of Justice of the European Union consists of the Court of Justice and the General Court. Critically examine the jurisdiction of each court and analyse how the preliminary reference procedure under Article 267 TFEU has allowed the Court of Justice to develop the foundational principles of EU law — including supremacy, direct effect, and state liability — into a coherent constitutional doctrine.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The Court of Justice is frequently criticised for its teleological approach to Treaty interpretation, which critics argue prioritises integration objectives over member state sovereignty. Examine this criticism with reference to at least three landmark judgments of the Court, and assess whether the doctrine of institutional balance adequately constrains the Court's interpretative power.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: A company, BioTech Ireland Ltd, has been found by the General Court to have been denied access to documents held by the Commission contrary to Regulation 1049/2001. The Commission wishes to appeal. Advise on: (i) the appellate jurisdiction of the Court of Justice over General Court judgments; (ii) the grounds on which an appeal lies; (iii) whether BioTech can bring a separate action for damages under Article 340 TFEU; and (iv) what the Court may award.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The concept of the 'democratic deficit' has been central to political and academic debate about the legitimacy of EU governance since the 1970s. Drawing on specific institutional features of the EU, critically assess whether successive treaty reforms — in particular the empowerment of the European Parliament, the citizens' initiative, and the national parliaments' subsidiarity check — have adequately addressed the structural sources of the democratic deficit.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Andrew Moravcsik has argued that the EU does not suffer from a democratic deficit because it exercises authority over predominantly technical regulatory areas that democratic theory itself assigns to expert insulation. Critically evaluate this counter-argument, with particular reference to the EU's economic governance role following the eurozone crisis and the implications for the democratic legitimacy of austerity-related conditionality imposed on member states.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: A political science student argues that Brexit was a direct consequence of the EU's democratic deficit, and that it could have been prevented had the EU's institutions been more democratically responsive. Advise on: (i) the structural features of the EU that contributed to democratic disengagement in the UK; (ii) the institutional responses introduced by the Lisbon Treaty to enhance democratic accountability; and (iii) whether the Article 50 TEU process itself was sufficiently transparent and democratically legitimated.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Primary Law: Treaties and the Charter
questions.text: The TEU, the TFEU, and the Charter of Fundamental Rights together constitute the primary law of the European Union. Critically compare the legal status and function of each instrument, addressing in particular how the Court of Justice has used the Charter to develop the fundamental rights dimension of EU law and whether giving the Charter the same legal value as the Treaties was a genuine constitutional innovation.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Primary Law: Treaties and the Charter
questions.text: Critically examine the hierarchy of norms in EU law. Explain the distinction between primary law and secondary law, analyse how the Court of Justice polices this hierarchy through judicial review under Article 263 TFEU and through the invalidity reference procedure under Article 267 TFEU, and assess the implications of the EU legal order having no formal constitutional amendment procedure beyond intergovernmental treaty revision.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Primary Law: Treaties and the Charter
questions.text: Following the Lisbon Treaty, the Charter of Fundamental Rights became legally binding. Advise on: (i) the legal basis for the Charter's binding status under Article 6 TEU; (ii) the significance of Articles 51 and 52 of the Charter, which limit its scope to EU institutions and member states 'implementing' EU law; (iii) the relationship between the Charter, the ECHR, and general principles of EU law; and (iv) Ireland's Protocol on the Charter.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Secondary Law: Regulations, Directives and Decisions
questions.text: Regulations, Directives and Decisions are the principal instruments of EU secondary legislation under Article 288 TFEU. Compare and contrast the legal character and domestic effect of each instrument, explaining why the Court of Justice has developed the doctrine of direct effect of Directives despite their formally indirect nature and the textual distinction between Directives and Regulations in Article 288 TFEU.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Secondary Law: Regulations, Directives and Decisions
questions.text: Member states frequently argue that a Commission Decision addressed to them encroaches on their policy discretion in a way that a Regulation would not. Critically examine whether the legal form of an EU act — regulation, directive or decision — has any remaining practical significance following the development of direct effect, and assess the argument that the distinction matters principally in relation to standing and judicial review under Article 263 TFEU.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Secondary Law: Regulations, Directives and Decisions
questions.text: A Directive requiring member states to establish a minimum wage mechanism was adopted in 2023. Ireland argues that the Directive has been wrongly characterised as an internal market measure under Article 114 TFEU and should have been adopted under Article 153 TFEU, which requires unanimity in the Council. Advise on: (i) the choice of legal basis in EU law; (ii) the consequences of adopting secondary legislation on an incorrect legal basis; (iii) Ireland's standing to challenge the Directive under Article 263 TFEU.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Delegated Acts, Implementing Acts and Comitology
questions.text: The Lisbon Treaty introduced a formal distinction between delegated acts under Article 290 TFEU and implementing acts under Article 291 TFEU, replacing the pre-Lisbon comitology system with a more constitutionally transparent structure. Critically examine this distinction, explaining the conditions under which each type of act may be adopted and assessing whether the new framework has genuinely strengthened Parliamentary oversight of Commission executive action.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Delegated Acts, Implementing Acts and Comitology
questions.text: The European Parliament argues that the Commission has exceeded the terms of a legislative delegation under Article 290 TFEU by adopting a delegated regulation that goes beyond what the parent regulation authorised. Advise on: (i) the constitutional requirements for delegated acts, including the explicit conditions of Article 290; (ii) the mechanisms available to the Parliament and Council to revoke a delegation or object to a delegated act; and (iii) the availability of judicial review under Article 263 TFEU.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Delegated Acts, Implementing Acts and Comitology
questions.text: Critically assess the constitutional significance of the shift from comitology to the current delegated act/implementing act framework. In your answer, address: (i) the democratic concerns raised by the original comitology system; (ii) the extent to which the Lisbon reforms enhanced Parliamentary control; (iii) whether the distinction between policy choice (Article 290) and purely technical implementation (Article 291) is workable in practice.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: General principles of EU law — including proportionality, legal certainty, legitimate expectations, equality, and the right to a fair hearing — occupy a constitutional rank in the EU legal order. Critically examine how the Court of Justice has developed and applied these principles, addressing both their role as grounds for reviewing the validity of EU acts and their function as interpretative principles in cases involving member state measures.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The principle of proportionality requires that EU measures must be suitable, necessary and not disproportionate in their effects. Analyse how the Court of Justice applies proportionality review to EU legislative acts, with reference to at least three significant cases, and critically assess whether the Court's proportionality review of legislative choices is sufficiently deferential to the political judgment of the EU legislator.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Muireann is an Irish pharmacist who relied on published Commission guidelines indicating that a new licensing process would not apply to existing products. The guidelines were subsequently withdrawn without notice and new rules were imposed, causing her significant financial loss. Advise Muireann on: (i) the principle of legitimate expectations as a general principle of EU law; (ii) the conditions for a legitimate expectation to be recognised; (iii) whether the Commission's conduct may give rise to an EU law damages claim under Article 340 TFEU.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fundamental Rights: Development and the Charter
questions.text: Fundamental rights entered the EU legal order not through Treaty text but through judge-made law. Trace the development of fundamental rights protection in EU law from Stauder v City of Ulm through Nold and Hauer to the formal constitutionalisation of the Charter under the Lisbon Treaty. Critically assess whether the Court of Justice's approach to fundamental rights before the Charter was constitutionally sound or pragmatically driven.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fundamental Rights: Development and the Charter
questions.text: The Court of Justice has developed a rich jurisprudence on the right to effective judicial protection as a general principle of EU law and, since Lisbon, as guaranteed by Articles 47 and 48 of the Charter. Critically examine the relationship between the right to an effective remedy under Article 47 of the Charter, the principle of effectiveness under EU law, and the right to a fair trial under Article 6 ECHR, with reference to at least three relevant judgments.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fundamental Rights: Development and the Charter
questions.text: An EU Regulation requires that asylum-seekers in Ireland be returned to the first EU member state they entered under the Dublin III Regulation. Ciarán, a refugee advocate, argues that return to Bulgaria in this case violates the Charter's prohibition on inhuman or degrading treatment under Article 4. Advise on: (i) the application of the Charter to Ireland as an EU member state implementing the Dublin Regulation; (ii) the extent to which Article 4 of the Charter is absolute; (iii) the relevance of the judgment in N.S. v Secretary of State for the Home Department.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Scope of the Charter: When Does It Apply to Member States?
questions.text: Article 51(1) of the Charter limits its application to member states only 'when they are implementing Union law'. The Court of Justice in Åkerberg Fransson interpreted this broadly to include member states 'acting within the scope of EU law'. Critically assess whether this interpretation is consistent with the text of Article 51(1) and with the Explanations to the Charter, and examine the implications of this broad approach for the division of competences between the EU and member states.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Scope of the Charter: When Does It Apply to Member States?
questions.text: The Åkerberg Fransson judgment (C-617/10) provoked significant controversy, particularly the German Federal Constitutional Court's Solange warning. Compare the approaches of the Court of Justice and national constitutional courts to determining when EU fundamental rights standards apply to member state action, and assess whether the current dialogue between these courts is constitutionally stable.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Scope of the Charter: When Does It Apply to Member States?
questions.text: A company, MediaNet Ltd, challenges an Irish statutory provision on defamation grounds, arguing that it violates freedom of expression guaranteed by Article 11 of the Charter. The Irish government argues that the provision has nothing to do with EU law and that the Charter does not apply. Advise on: (i) the 'implementing EU law' threshold under Article 51(1) of the Charter; (ii) whether the existence of a relevant EU Directive on audiovisual media services is sufficient to bring the Irish measure within the scope of EU law; (iii) the relevance of the Digital Services Act.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The supremacy of EU law over conflicting national law was established by the Court of Justice in Costa v ENEL (1964). Critically trace the development of this doctrine through Internationale Handelsgesellschaft, Simmenthal, and Factortame, examining the constitutional basis claimed by the Court for this doctrine and assessing the objections raised by national constitutional courts, including the German Federal Constitutional Court's Solange I and II jurisprudence.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The supremacy of EU law has never been expressly stated in the Treaties — it remains a judge-made doctrine. Critically examine whether this is constitutionally defensible, assessing the competing bases for supremacy advanced by the Court of Justice (the autonomous nature of the EU legal order) versus those accepted by member states (Treaty-based conferral and domestic incorporation). In your answer, refer to the Lisbon Treaty's Declaration 17 on primacy.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The Oireachtas has enacted a statute that requires all pharmaceutical companies to obtain an Irish licence before marketing medicinal products in Ireland, even where the product holds a European Medicines Agency authorisation. Advise on: (i) whether the Irish statute conflicts with EU law; (ii) the obligation of national courts and public authorities under Simmenthal (C-106/77) to disapply conflicting national law; (iii) whether an Irish court must seek a preliminary reference before disapplying the statute; and (iv) the constitutional basis for disapplying an Act of the Oireachtas in light of Crotty v An Taoiseach and the European Communities Act 1972.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Direct Effect of Treaty Articles and Regulations
questions.text: Van Gend en Loos (1963) established that EU law creates rights which individuals can rely on in national courts — the doctrine of direct effect. Critically examine the conditions for direct effect laid down in Van Gend en Loos and Defrenne v Sabena, explaining how the Court of Justice developed both vertical and horizontal direct effect and assessing the constitutional significance of this doctrine for the relationship between EU law and national legal systems.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Direct Effect of Treaty Articles and Regulations
questions.text: Regulations are 'directly applicable' under Article 288 TFEU. Critically examine whether 'direct applicability' and 'direct effect' are legally distinct concepts, explaining why a directly applicable Regulation may still require implementation by member states in certain circumstances and assessing the implications for national courts when a Regulation confers discretion on member states in its application.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Direct Effect of Treaty Articles and Regulations
questions.text: SolarEnergy GmbH is a German company that has been denied a grid connection by the Irish energy regulator, contrary to what it argues is a directly effective obligation under an EU Regulation on renewable energy network access. Advise on: (i) the conditions for the direct effect of Regulation provisions; (ii) whether the relevant provision is sufficiently clear, precise and unconditional; (iii) the national court's obligations when confronted with a conflicting national administrative decision; and (iv) whether SolarEnergy may also have a claim under the principle of effective judicial protection.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: In Van Duyn v Home Office (1974) and Ratti (1979), the Court of Justice established that Directives can have direct effect, notwithstanding Article 288 TFEU's characterisation of Directives as binding only as to the result. Critically examine the doctrinal basis for the direct effect of Directives, explaining the 'state liability' rationale from Ratti, the Marshall conditions, and why the Court has consistently refused to extend horizontal direct effect to Directives in Marshall v Southampton and Faccini Dori.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The refusal of the Court of Justice to recognise horizontal direct effect of Directives has been widely criticised as producing arbitrary distinctions between state and private employers. Critically assess this criticism with reference to the case law on the extended concept of 'emanation of the state', the incidental horizontal effect recognised in CIA Security and Unilever, and the role of indirect effect under the Von Colson principle as a partial remedy for the limitation on horizontal direct effect.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Aoife is employed by CityBus Ltd, a private bus company operating under a public service contract granted by the Irish government. She claims that her employer has violated rights conferred on her by an unimplemented EU Working Time Directive. The transposition deadline has passed. Advise on: (i) whether the Directive is directly effective against CityBus Ltd; (ii) the test for 'emanation of the state' under Foster v British Gas; (iii) whether CityBus is an emanation of the state on the facts; and (iv) any alternative remedies available to Aoife, including indirect effect and Francovich liability.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Indirect Effect and Limits in Criminal Proceedings
questions.text: The principle of indirect effect — established in Von Colson and Kauffmann and developed in Marleasing and Pfeiffer — requires national courts to interpret national law 'as far as possible' in conformity with unimplemented or incorrectly implemented Directives. Critically examine the scope of this obligation, identifying the limits recognised by the Court of Justice in Adeneler and Kücükdeveci, and assessing whether indirect effect adequately compensates for the absence of horizontal direct effect.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Indirect Effect and Limits in Criminal Proceedings
questions.text: The Court of Justice has held that a Directive cannot have the effect, directly or through indirect effect, of imposing or aggravating criminal liability under national law, as confirmed in Kolpinghuis Nijmegen and Arcaro. Critically analyse this limitation, explaining the constitutional rationale for it, assessing whether it is coherently applied in the case law, and examining whether it could ever be invoked to prevent the indirect effect of a Directive operating in favour of an accused person.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Indirect Effect and Limits in Criminal Proceedings
questions.text: Cian is prosecuted under Irish criminal law for the unlicensed sale of dietary supplements. The Irish law implementing an EU Directive on food supplements is stricter than what the Directive requires. Cian argues that he should be acquitted because the stricter domestic implementation is inconsistent with the Directive. Advise on: (i) whether Cian can rely on the Directive in criminal proceedings; (ii) the prohibition against adverse criminal reliance on Directives from Kolpinghuis Nijmegen; (iii) whether the Irish court is obliged to refer the question to the Court of Justice under Article 267 TFEU; and (iv) whether Cian has a Francovich damages claim against Ireland.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: In Francovich and Bonifaci v Italy (1991), the Court of Justice established that member states are liable in damages for loss caused by their failure to implement EU Directives. Critically examine the constitutional basis claimed by the Court for this doctrine, analyse the three conditions for Francovich liability as stated in the judgment, and assess the significance of this doctrine for the effective enforcement of EU law.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Francovich liability has been extended beyond non-implementation of Directives to any sufficiently serious breach of EU law by any branch of the state — including the judiciary, as confirmed in Köbler v Austria. Critically assess the development of state liability through Brasserie du Pêcheur, Factortame III, and Köbler, identifying which of the Brasserie conditions replaced the narrower Francovich conditions and examining whether judicial liability for breach of EU law is constitutionally sound.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Maria is an Italian worker who lost income because Italy failed to implement the Insolvency Protection Directive within the required period. She brings a Francovich claim in the Italian courts. Advise on: (i) the three conditions for Francovich liability; (ii) whether the failure to implement within the transposition deadline automatically constitutes a Francovich breach; (iii) the relationship between Francovich liability and the adequacy of compensatory remedies under national law; and (iv) whether Maria can alternatively claim against the Commission for failing to commence infringement proceedings against Italy sooner.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Brasserie, Factortame and the Sufficiently Serious Breach
questions.text: Brasserie du Pêcheur and Factortame III (joined cases C-46/93 and C-48/93) established the principle of state liability for breach of EU law beyond the specific context of Directive non-implementation. Critically examine the three Brasserie conditions — a rule conferring rights on individuals, a sufficiently serious breach, and a direct causal link — and assess whether these conditions are applied consistently in subsequent case law, including Dillenkofer and British Telecommunications.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Brasserie, Factortame and the Sufficiently Serious Breach
questions.text: The concept of a 'sufficiently serious breach' has been the most litigated of the Brasserie conditions. Critically examine the factors identified by the Court of Justice for determining whether a breach is sufficiently serious, with particular reference to the distinction between wrongful legislative choices and administrative misapplication, and assess whether the threshold of 'manifest and grave disregard' provides adequate clarity for national courts.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Brasserie, Factortame and the Sufficiently Serious Breach
questions.text: Ireland enacted a legislative measure transposing an EU Directive on financial services. The transposition was technically within the literal terms of the Directive but failed to achieve the Directive's intended result, causing significant loss to investors. Advise on: (i) whether an incorrect transposition that falls short of achieving the Directive's result can constitute a Brasserie/Francovich breach; (ii) the relevant factors for assessing whether the breach is sufficiently serious, with reference to the legislative discretion available to Ireland; (iii) the requirement of a direct causal link; and (iv) the relationship between the EU law damages claim and any available domestic tort claim.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: National Procedural Autonomy and its Limits
questions.text: The principle of national procedural autonomy provides that, in the absence of EU harmonisation, the procedural conditions for enforcing EU law rights are a matter for national law. However, this autonomy is subject to the principles of equivalence and effectiveness. Critically examine these two principles as limits on national procedural autonomy, with reference to the case law of the Court of Justice including Rewe-Zentralfinanz, Peterbroeck, and Courage v Crehan.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: National Procedural Autonomy and its Limits
questions.text: The principle of effectiveness has been used by the Court of Justice to override national rules on standing, limitation periods, res judicata, and national public policy rules, where those rules render the exercise of EU rights practically impossible or excessively difficult. Critically assess whether the Court of Justice's application of the effectiveness principle has gone too far in eroding national procedural sovereignty, with reference to at least four cases from different procedural contexts.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: National Procedural Autonomy and its Limits
questions.text: Brigid, an Irish consumer, was overcharged by her energy supplier under a pricing regime that the Court of Justice subsequently declared incompatible with EU law. Irish law imposes a two-year limitation period on claims for recovery of overpaid charges. The EU Directive establishing the consumer's right to accurate billing contains no specific limitation period. Advise on: (i) whether the two-year limitation period is consistent with the principle of effectiveness; (ii) the equivalence requirement; (iii) any relevant Irish authorities on limitation periods in EU law claims; and (iv) whether a Francovich damages claim against Ireland is available as an alternative.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Grounds of Review and Reviewable Acts
questions.text: Article 263 TFEU provides for direct actions for annulment of EU acts before the Court of Justice and General Court. Critically examine each of the four grounds of review listed in Article 263(2) — lack of competence, infringement of an essential procedural requirement, infringement of the Treaties, and misuse of powers — illustrating your answer with reference to leading cases from the Court of Justice.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Grounds of Review and Reviewable Acts
questions.text: Not all acts adopted by EU institutions are reviewable under Article 263 TFEU. The Court of Justice has drawn a distinction between acts that produce binding legal effects and preparatory or advisory acts that do not. Critically examine this distinction with reference to the ERTA case and the subsequent case law, assessing whether the test is sufficiently clear and whether it adequately protects the right to effective judicial protection under Article 47 of the Charter.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Grounds of Review and Reviewable Acts
questions.text: The Commission adopted a non-binding Recommendation advising member states on new reporting obligations for cryptocurrency exchanges. CryptoEU Ltd, an Irish company, argues the Recommendation is in substance a binding act that should have been adopted as a Regulation under the ordinary legislative procedure. Advise on: (i) the reviewability of non-binding acts under Article 263 TFEU following ERTA and the soft law jurisprudence; (ii) the substance over form test for reviewability; (iii) whether CryptoEU has standing under Article 263(4) TFEU; and (iv) the significance of Article 288 TFEU in distinguishing between binding and non-binding acts.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Standing: Privileged, Quasi-Privileged and Non-Privileged Applicants
questions.text: Article 263(4) TFEU provides that any natural or legal person may challenge acts addressed to them, acts of direct and individual concern, and regulatory acts of direct concern not entailing implementing measures. Critically trace the development of the standing rules for non-privileged applicants from Plaumann through UPA to the Lisbon Treaty amendments, assessing whether the post-Lisbon position adequately satisfies the right to effective judicial protection.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Standing: Privileged, Quasi-Privileged and Non-Privileged Applicants
questions.text: The Plaumann formula — that individual concern requires the applicant to be distinguished by attributes peculiar to them by reference to a closed class — has been consistently applied despite widespread academic criticism. Critically assess the Plaumann test, examining whether it is compatible with Article 47 of the Charter's guarantee of effective judicial protection, and evaluate the alternative of using the preliminary reference procedure as a substitute access route to the Court of Justice.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Standing: Privileged, Quasi-Privileged and Non-Privileged Applicants
questions.text: GreenTech Ireland, an Irish environmental technology company, wishes to challenge a Commission Decision approving state aid granted by France to a competitor. The Decision is not addressed to GreenTech but it has a demonstrable effect on its market position. Advise on: (i) whether GreenTech is directly concerned; (ii) whether GreenTech is individually concerned applying the Plaumann test; (iii) whether the Decision constitutes a regulatory act not entailing implementing measures under the third limb of Article 263(4); and (iv) any alternative procedural routes available.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Plea of Illegality: Article 277 and Indirect Challenges
questions.text: Article 277 TFEU provides that any party to proceedings before the Court of Justice may plead the inapplicability of a Regulation, even where the direct challenge period has expired. Critically examine the scope and purpose of the plea of illegality, explaining the relationship between Article 277 and the standing restrictions under Article 263(4), and assessing the extent to which Article 277 compensates for the narrow Plaumann standing test.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Plea of Illegality: Article 277 and Indirect Challenges
questions.text: An individual who was unable to bring a direct challenge under Article 263 TFEU within the two-month time limit now seeks to use Article 277 in ancillary proceedings to challenge the Regulation forming the legal basis for a Commission Decision addressed to her. Critically examine: (i) whether the plea of illegality under Article 277 is available to her; (ii) the conditions for admissibility of an Article 277 plea; (iii) what grounds of illegality may be invoked; and (iv) the legal consequences if the Court upholds the plea.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Plea of Illegality: Article 277 and Indirect Challenges
questions.text: Examine the extent to which the preliminary reference procedure under Article 267 TFEU operates as a functional substitute for direct actions that are inadmissible under Article 263 TFEU. In your answer, address: (i) the invalidity reference procedure; (ii) Foto-Frost and the obligation of national courts not to declare EU acts invalid; (iii) the role of the principle of effective judicial protection in bridging the gap between Article 263 standing restrictions and the reference procedure; and (iv) the practical consequences for litigants of reliance on this indirect route.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Article 265 TFEU creates a mechanism for challenging the failure of EU institutions to act where they are under a legal obligation to do so. Critically compare the action for failure to act under Article 265 with the action for annulment under Article 263, examining the procedural requirements specific to Article 265, the significance of the prior calling-upon requirement, and the limited circumstances in which a finding of failure to act does not compel the institution to take the precise action sought.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: The European Parliament formally called on the Commission to submit a legislative proposal on artificial intelligence liability, pursuant to Article 225 TFEU, and the Commission failed to respond within a reasonable time. The Parliament seeks a remedy under Article 265 TFEU. Advise on: (i) whether the Parliament has standing to bring an Article 265 action; (ii) the scope of the obligation to act and whether it encompasses the Commission's legislative initiative function; (iii) the procedural requirements; and (iv) the Court's jurisdiction to specify the content of the measure the institution must adopt.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: LogiSupply Ltd, an Irish logistics company, made a formal request to the Commission to investigate a state aid measure granted by the Lithuanian government that was damaging its business. The Commission failed to reach any decision within eighteen months. Advise on: (i) whether LogiSupply can bring an action under Article 265 TFEU; (ii) the standing of private applicants in Article 265 actions; (iii) the relationship between Article 265 and the Commission's discretion in state aid investigations; and (iv) whether an action under Article 340 TFEU for damages for the Commission's failure to act could succeed.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Preliminary Reference Mechanism and Foto-Frost
questions.text: The preliminary reference procedure under Article 267 TFEU is the principal instrument through which the Court of Justice maintains the uniform application of EU law across member states. Critically examine the purpose and constitutional function of Article 267, assessing how the Court has used it to develop foundational EU law principles and whether the procedure adequately balances judicial dialogue with judicial hierarchy between national courts and the Court of Justice.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Preliminary Reference Mechanism and Foto-Frost
questions.text: In Foto-Frost v Hauptzollamt Lübeck-Ost (1987), the Court of Justice held that national courts have no power to declare EU acts invalid and must refer any invalidity question to the Court. Critically examine the constitutional basis for this ruling, assessing whether it is consistent with the principle of effective judicial protection and analysing how it interacts with the direct challenge procedure under Article 263 TFEU, particularly for applicants who lack standing under Plaumann.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Preliminary Reference Mechanism and Foto-Frost
questions.text: An Irish High Court judge is hearing a case in which a company, FinanceIreland Ltd, claims that an EU Regulation applying a new financial services levy is invalid. The Commission has intervened to argue that the Court has no power to declare the Regulation invalid. Advise on: (i) the obligation under Foto-Frost (C-314/85) for national courts to refer invalidity questions; (ii) whether the High Court should make an expedited reference; (iii) the procedure for interim relief pending a reference, as established in Zuckerfabrik Süderdithmarschen and Atlanta; and (iv) the binding effect of the Court of Justice's ruling on the referring court.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Mandatory Duty to Refer and Acte Clair
questions.text: Article 267(3) TFEU imposes a mandatory obligation to refer on courts against whose decisions there is no judicial remedy under national law. Critically examine the scope of this obligation, explaining the exceptions established in Da Costa and CILFIT, including the acte clair doctrine, and assessing whether these exceptions have been applied with appropriate caution in light of the need for uniform interpretation of EU law.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Mandatory Duty to Refer and Acte Clair
questions.text: The CILFIT criteria for acte clair — that the correct interpretation is so obvious as to leave no scope for any reasonable doubt — have been widely criticised as unworkable and as encouraging national courts of last resort to avoid referrals. Critically assess this criticism, with reference to any recent clarification of the CILFIT test and examining the consequences under Köbler state liability where a court of last resort incorrectly declines to refer.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Mandatory Duty to Refer and Acte Clair
questions.text: The Irish Supreme Court is hearing an appeal in a case involving the interpretation of an EU Regulation on cross-border succession. The Supreme Court considers the answer to be clear, having regard to prior CJEU rulings on analogous provisions, and proposes not to refer. The appellant objects. Advise on: (i) the Supreme Court's obligation to refer under Article 267(3) TFEU; (ii) the conditions for the acte clair exception under CILFIT; (iii) whether the existence of prior CJEU jurisprudence automatically satisfies acte clair; and (iv) the consequences of a wrongful refusal to refer in terms of Köbler liability and infringement proceedings.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Effect of Preliminary Rulings and Temporal Limitations
questions.text: Preliminary rulings of the Court of Justice are authoritative interpretations of EU law binding on the referring court and, by virtue of the principle of uniform application, on all national courts. Critically examine the temporal effect of preliminary rulings, explaining why the Court holds that its rulings are declaratory and retroactive in principle, and examining the exceptional circumstances in which the Court has limited the temporal effect of a ruling, as in Defrenne v Sabena and Barber v Guardian Royal Exchange.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Effect of Preliminary Rulings and Temporal Limitations
questions.text: The Court of Justice has the power, in exceptional circumstances, to limit the temporal effect of its ruling — prospectively only, or applying only to the parties to the reference and those who brought proceedings before the date of the ruling. Critically assess the criteria the Court has developed for exercising this power, examining whether these criteria are applied consistently and whether they give adequate weight to the interests of individuals who relied on national law before the ruling.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Effect of Preliminary Rulings and Temporal Limitations
questions.text: The Court of Justice has just delivered a preliminary ruling that an Irish tax provision, used by thousands of companies since 2010, is incompatible with an EU Directive on VAT. The Irish government estimates that full retroactive application of the ruling would cost the exchequer €4 billion. Advise on: (i) the general retroactive effect of CJEU preliminary rulings; (ii) the criteria under Defrenne and Barber for a temporal limitation; (iii) who can apply for temporal limitation and at what stage; and (iv) the relationship between a temporal limitation granted by the CJEU and any domestic proceedings already underway.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Articles 28 to 30 TFEU prohibit customs duties and charges having equivalent effect (CEEs) between member states. Critically examine the concept of a charge having equivalent effect as developed in Commission v Italy (Statistical Levy) and Sociaal Fonds voor de Diamantarbeiders, and assess whether the Court of Justice's broad interpretation of CEE has served the internal market effectively or created excessive regulatory burdens for member states.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: A member state imposes an administrative fee at the border for veterinary inspections of livestock imported from other EU member states. The fee is identical to that imposed on imports from third countries. The importing company argues this is a CEE prohibited by Article 30 TFEU. The member state argues it is a fee for a service rendered. Critically examine the 'specific service' exception to the CEE prohibition and assess whether the fee could survive Article 30 scrutiny.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Ireland introduces a levy on all imported pharmaceuticals that are screened at Irish ports for compliance with Irish packaging regulations — a requirement that applies equally to Irish-made products. Advise on: (i) whether the levy constitutes a CEE under Articles 28–30 TFEU or an internal tax potentially reviewable under Article 110 TFEU; (ii) the criteria for distinguishing CEEs from internal taxes (Commission v France; Steinike); (iii) whether any mandatory inspection fees could be justified; and (iv) how the Customs Union's structural integrity would be affected if member states could freely impose inspection levies.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Article 110 TFEU: Internal Taxation, Discrimination and Protection
questions.text: Article 110 TFEU prohibits discriminatory internal taxation of imported goods and internal taxation that affords indirect protection to domestic products. Critically compare the two limbs of Article 110, examining how the Court of Justice distinguishes direct from indirect discrimination in the taxation of imported products, and assess the significance of the Humblot and Commission v France cases on differential vehicle and spirit taxation.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Article 110 TFEU: Internal Taxation, Discrimination and Protection
questions.text: A member state imposes a higher rate of excise duty on imported wine than on domestically produced beer, arguing the two products are not 'similar' under Article 110(1). Critically analyse how the Court determines whether products are 'similar' within Article 110(1) and 'in competition' under Article 110(2), with reference to Commission v UK (wine and beer) and Commission v France (spirits), and examine whether the protective effect test under Article 110(2) gives adequate weight to member state tax autonomy.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Article 110 TFEU: Internal Taxation, Discrimination and Protection
questions.text: Ireland imposes a 'sugar tax' on soft drinks, which applies to both domestic and imported products. However, the tax is calculated by reference to sugar content, and a traditional domestic Irish drink with moderate sugar content falls into a lower band than imported drinks with the same sugar content due to a volumetric discount available only to Irish producers. Advise on: (i) whether the Irish tax violates Article 110(1) on direct discrimination grounds; (ii) whether it violates Article 110(2) as having a protective effect; (iii) the applicable objective justification analysis; and (iv) whether the Humblot proportionality standard applies.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Articles 34–35: MEQRs, Dassonville, Cassis and Keck
questions.text: Article 34 TFEU prohibits quantitative restrictions and measures having equivalent effect (MEQRs) on imports. Critically trace the development of the MEQR concept from Dassonville through Cassis de Dijon to Keck, assessing how each landmark judgment restructured the scope of Article 34 and whether the Keck distinction between product requirements and selling arrangements provides a coherent and workable framework for national courts.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Articles 34–35: MEQRs, Dassonville, Cassis and Keck
questions.text: In Cassis de Dijon (1979), the Court of Justice established the principle of mutual recognition and identified the rule of reason — a set of mandatory requirements that could justify national product standards. Critically examine the relationship between the rule of reason and proportionality review, assess the mandatory requirements identified in the case law, and evaluate whether the Cassis framework has proven adequate for regulating national barriers to trade in a Single Market of 27 member states.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Articles 34–35: MEQRs, Dassonville, Cassis and Keck
questions.text: GreenBrew Ltd, an Irish microbrewery, is exporting speciality beer to France. French law requires that all beer labels display the alcohol content in a specific format mandated by French law, which differs from the Irish labelling standard used by GreenBrew. GreenBrew argues this is a barrier to trade. Advise on: (i) whether the French labelling requirement is an MEQR under the Dassonville formula; (ii) whether it constitutes a product requirement subject to Article 34 TFEU even after Keck; (iii) whether it is justified under the rule of reason or Article 36 TFEU; and (iv) the proportionality analysis.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Article 36 TFEU provides an exhaustive list of grounds on which member states may justify restrictions on imports, including public morality, public policy, public security, the protection of health and life, and the protection of industrial and commercial property. Critically examine the Court of Justice's approach to Article 36 justifications, explaining why the list is exhaustive and why the mandatory requirements doctrine from Cassis must be kept distinct from Article 36.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: A member state bans the import of a category of pesticides, relying on the public health justification in Article 36 TFEU. The scientific evidence on the health risk from the pesticide is contested. Critically examine how the Court of Justice applies the precautionary principle in the context of Article 36 TFEU health justifications, and assess whether a member state can rely on contested scientific evidence to justify a total ban rather than a less restrictive measure such as labelling requirements.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.text: Ireland enacts a law prohibiting the importation of fireworks from other EU member states, relying on the public safety ground in Article 36 TFEU. Professional fireworks companies in Germany, which hold EU-wide operator licences, challenge the Irish prohibition. Advise on: (i) whether the Irish ban is a quantitative restriction or MEQR under Article 34; (ii) whether it can be justified under the public security or public safety ground of Article 36; (iii) the proportionality requirement — specifically whether the ban is necessary and whether less restrictive measures could achieve the safety objective; and (iv) the significance of the EU's own harmonising legislation on fireworks safety.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Free Movement of Workers: Article 45 — Scope, Rights and Limitations
questions.text: Article 45 TFEU guarantees freedom of movement for workers within the EU. Critically examine the personal and material scope of Article 45, explaining how the Court of Justice has defined 'worker' in Lawrie-Blum and Levin, assessed the rights to equal treatment, access to employment, and residence conferred by Article 45 and Regulation 492/2011, and analyse the constitutional significance of the personal scope being defined autonomously by EU law.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Free Movement of Workers: Article 45 — Scope, Rights and Limitations
questions.text: The Court of Justice has held that Article 45 TFEU applies not only to restrictions imposed by the host member state but also to obstacles created by the home member state — the principle established in Singh and later extended in D'Hoop. Critically assess the development of the 'reverse discrimination' doctrine and examine whether extending Article 45 to home state restrictions is constitutionally coherent or whether it goes beyond the market access rationale underpinning free movement.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Free Movement of Workers: Article 45 — Scope, Rights and Limitations
questions.text: Vasile, a Romanian national, has been working in Ireland for eighteen months as a part-time shop assistant while studying for a college diploma. His employer decides not to renew his contract. The Irish Department of Social Protection refuses to grant him Jobseeker's Allowance, arguing he is not a 'worker' within Article 45 TFEU. Advise on: (i) whether Vasile satisfies the Lawrie-Blum test for worker status; (ii) whether his part-time hours affect his worker classification under Levin; (iii) whether he retains worker status after becoming unemployed under Article 7(3) of Directive 2004/38; and (iv) his right to equal treatment in access to social advantages under Article 7(2) of Regulation 492/2011.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Workers, Family Members, Jobseekers and Rights After Employment
questions.text: Directive 2004/38 on the right of EU citizens and their family members to move and reside freely within the member states consolidated and extended the residence rights previously contained in separate secondary legislation. Critically examine the rights conferred by Directive 2004/38 on EU workers' family members — including third-country national family members — and assess the conditions under which these rights are derived from the primary beneficiary's status rather than autonomous.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Workers, Family Members, Jobseekers and Rights After Employment
questions.text: The Court of Justice has held that jobseekers who have entered a member state with a genuine prospect of employment are entitled to a period of residence under Article 45 TFEU, but their access to social assistance benefits may be limited under Article 24(2) of Directive 2004/38. Critically examine the rights of jobseekers under EU free movement law, assessing the tension between equal treatment and member state concerns about welfare tourism, with reference to Vatsouras, Dano and Alimanovic.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Workers, Family Members, Jobseekers and Rights After Employment
questions.text: Nikolaj, a Danish national, worked as an engineer in Ireland for three years before being made redundant. He has been looking for work for six months. His Irish-born wife, Siobhán, and their two children (Danish and Irish nationality respectively) live in Ireland. Advise on: (i) whether Nikolaj retains worker status under Article 7(3)(b) of Directive 2004/38; (ii) his right to Jobseeker's Allowance having regard to Article 24(2) and Alimanovic; (iii) the residence rights of Siobhán and the children; and (iv) whether Nikolaj will qualify for permanent residence under Article 16 of the Directive given his three years of prior employment.
