questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Derogations: Public Policy, Security, Health and Expulsion Safeguards
questions.text: Articles 45(3) and 27–33 of Directive 2004/38 allow member states to restrict free movement on grounds of public policy, public security and public health. Critically examine the conditions under which these derogations may be invoked, explaining the 'genuine and sufficiently serious threat affecting one of the fundamental interests of society' test from Van Duyn and Bonsignore, and the procedural safeguards against expulsion established in Directive 2004/38.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Derogations: Public Policy, Security, Health and Expulsion Safeguards
questions.text: The Court of Justice has consistently narrowed the public policy and public security derogations, requiring member states to demonstrate that their own nationals are treated equivalently and that their measures are proportionate. Critically assess whether the current framework under Directive 2004/38 and the case law adequately balances member state sovereignty in immigration and public order matters against the principle of free movement as a fundamental right of EU citizens.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Derogations: Public Policy, Security, Health and Expulsion Safeguards
questions.text: Tomás, a Spanish national, has been living in Ireland for twelve years with permanent residence status under Article 16 of Directive 2004/38. He was convicted of a single drugs offence and sentenced to two years' imprisonment. The Minister for Justice has issued a deportation order. Advise on: (i) the enhanced protection against expulsion available to permanent residents under Article 28(2) of the Directive, requiring 'imperative grounds of public security'; (ii) whether a single conviction for a drugs offence satisfies this threshold; (iii) the procedural safeguards under Articles 30–31; (iv) whether the deportation order must take into account Tomás's length of residence and personal integration in Ireland.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: EU Citizenship: Origins, Nature and the Maastricht Innovation
questions.text: EU citizenship was introduced by the Maastricht Treaty in 1992 as Articles 17–22 TEC (now Articles 20–25 TFEU). Critically examine the nature and constitutional significance of EU citizenship, assessing whether it constitutes a genuinely autonomous legal status independent of national citizenship, or whether it remains parasitic on member state nationality, as reflected in Articles 20(1) TFEU and the Micheletti judgment.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: EU Citizenship: Origins, Nature and the Maastricht Innovation
questions.text: The Court of Justice stated in Grzelczyk that EU citizenship is 'destined to be the fundamental status of nationals of the Member States'. Critically assess how the Court has sought to realise this aspiration through its case law, examining the rights attached to EU citizenship under Articles 21–25 TFEU, and assess whether the CJEU's expansive approach to citizenship rights has gone beyond the constitutional limits of the Treaties.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: EU Citizenship: Origins, Nature and the Maastricht Innovation
questions.text: Zara, a dual Irish–Moldovan national, has her Irish nationality challenged by the Moldovan government on the basis that she was Irish by descent rather than by birth in Ireland, and that under a bilateral agreement Moldova treats nationality acquired by descent as secondary. The Irish government confirms her Irish citizenship is valid. Advise on: (i) the exclusively national control over nationality as confirmed in Micheletti; (ii) whether EU law can place limits on member state nationality decisions, as suggested in Rottmann v Freistaat Bayern; (iii) the rights of EU citizenship that Zara has as an Irish national; and (iv) whether the withdrawal of Irish nationality (hypothetically) could be challenged under EU law.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Right of Residence Under Directive 2004/38: Conditions and Welfare
questions.text: Directive 2004/38 establishes a graduated framework of residence rights for EU citizens, moving from short-term residence (under three months), to conditional residence (between three months and five years), to permanent residence (after five years). Critically examine each stage of this framework, explaining the conditions attached to each tier, the significance of 'sufficient resources' and 'comprehensive sickness insurance', and assessing whether the conditions for conditional residence impermissibly qualify what Article 21 TFEU describes as an unconditional right.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Right of Residence Under Directive 2004/38: Conditions and Welfare
questions.text: The right of non-economically active EU citizens to access social assistance in a host member state has been the subject of intense judicial and political controversy. Critically examine the Court's approach in Dano, Alimanovic and García-Nieto, assessing whether the Court has struck an appropriate balance between the principle of equal treatment under Article 24 of Directive 2004/38 and the legitimate interest of member states in not becoming welfare tourism destinations.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Right of Residence Under Directive 2004/38: Conditions and Welfare
questions.text: Agnés, a French national, has been living in Dublin for four years. She works part-time as a freelance translator and earns €700 per month. She applies for the back-to-education allowance under Irish social welfare law. The Department of Social Protection refuses, arguing she does not have sufficient resources and therefore her residence is not lawful. Advise on: (i) whether Agnés qualifies as a worker or self-employed person under Directive 2004/38; (ii) whether her part-time income satisfies the 'sufficient resources' condition; (iii) her right to equal treatment in social advantages under Article 7(2) of Regulation 492/2011 if she is a worker; and (iv) whether refusal of the allowance violates Article 24 of the Directive.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Third-Country National Family Members: Zambrano, McCarthy and Dereci
questions.text: In Ruiz Zambrano (C-34/09), the Court of Justice held that Article 20 TFEU precluded national measures that deprived EU citizen children of the genuine enjoyment of the substance of the rights conferred by EU citizenship. Critically examine the Zambrano principle, assess how it was subsequently limited in McCarthy, Dereci, and O v Minister for Justice, and evaluate whether the 'substance of rights' test provides an adequate framework for protecting EU citizens in purely internal situations.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Third-Country National Family Members: Zambrano, McCarthy and Dereci
questions.text: The judgment in McCarthy (C-434/09) distinguished Zambrano by holding that Article 20 TFEU does not apply where an EU citizen has never exercised free movement rights and is not dependent on a non-EU family member for the enjoyment of citizenship rights. Critically assess this distinction, examining whether it creates an arbitrary two-tier system of protection between EU citizens who have exercised free movement and those in purely internal situations, and consider whether the ECHR's Article 8 right to family life provides an alternative route.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Third-Country National Family Members: Zambrano, McCarthy and Dereci
questions.text: Renzo, an Italian national, has lived in Ireland since birth. He has never lived or worked in another EU member state. His wife Fatima is a Moroccan national who has lived with him in Dublin for three years without Irish immigration permission. They have a five-year-old Irish-born daughter, Giulia. The Minister for Justice issues a deportation order against Fatima. Advise on: (i) whether the Zambrano principle applies so as to prohibit Fatima's deportation; (ii) whether Giulia's EU citizenship rights would be frustrated by the deportation of her primary carer; (iii) the relevance of Dereci and the 'solely dependent' test; and (iv) whether Irish domestic law or the ECHR provides any further protection.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Political Rights, Diplomatic Protection and Constitutional Significance
questions.text: Articles 22–25 TFEU attach specific political and legal rights to EU citizenship, including the right to vote and stand in local and European Parliament elections in the member state of residence, the right to petition the European Parliament, and the right to consular protection in third countries where the citizen's member state is not represented. Critically assess the constitutional significance of these rights for the development of a genuinely European political community.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Political Rights, Diplomatic Protection and Constitutional Significance
questions.text: The citizens' initiative under Article 11(4) TEU, introduced by the Lisbon Treaty, allows one million EU citizens from at least seven member states to invite the Commission to submit a legislative proposal. Critically examine this mechanism as an expression of EU citizenship rights, assessing whether it constitutes a genuine form of participatory democracy or whether the Commission's discretion in responding to successful initiatives renders it constitutionally inadequate.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Political Rights, Diplomatic Protection and Constitutional Significance
questions.text: Aoife, an Irish national, is visiting the Philippines on holiday. A civil unrest emergency requires an emergency evacuation, but Ireland has no embassy in the Philippines. The French embassy is present. Advise on: (i) the right to consular protection under Article 23 TFEU and the conditions for exercising it; (ii) the obligation of other member states to treat EU citizens as their own nationals for consular purposes; (iii) whether the French authorities are legally obliged under EU law to assist Aoife; and (iv) the current state of EU diplomatic protection legislation and its limitations.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Equal Pay Principle: Article 157 TFEU and Defrenne
questions.text: Article 157 TFEU imposes a direct obligation on member states and employers to ensure equal pay for equal work or work of equal value between men and women. Critically trace the development of this principle from Defrenne v Sabena (No. 2) through Macarthys, Bilka-Kaufhaus and Enderby, examining in particular how the Court has defined 'pay', extended the comparator requirement, and placed the burden of proof in indirect pay discrimination cases.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Equal Pay Principle: Article 157 TFEU and Defrenne
questions.text: Indirect pay discrimination occurs where a facially neutral measure disproportionately disadvantages workers of one sex without objective justification. Critically examine the framework for establishing indirect pay discrimination under Article 157 TFEU and the Equal Pay Directive (now Directive 2006/54), with reference to the case law on part-time worker pay scales, seniority increments, and market forces as objective justification.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Equal Pay Principle: Article 157 TFEU and Defrenne
questions.text: Máiréad is a female senior nurse employed by a hospital trust. She discovers that male colleagues performing substantially the same work are paid 15% more, justified by her employer on the basis of a 'market supplement' paid to attract scarce candidates. She also discovers that a historical bonus scheme, inherited from a previous pay agreement, is only available to staff who were employed prior to 2005, a period when the workforce was predominantly male. Advise on: (i) whether she has an equal pay claim under Article 157 TFEU and Directive 2006/54; (ii) whether the 'market supplement' can constitute objective justification; (iii) the impact of the historical bonus scheme under the indirect discrimination framework in Enderby and Barber; and (iv) the applicable limitation period under Irish law.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Equal Treatment Directives and the Broadened Equality Framework
questions.text: The EU's equality framework has expanded substantially beyond sex discrimination to encompass discrimination on grounds of racial or ethnic origin, religion or belief, disability, age, and sexual orientation. Critically compare Directive 2000/43 (race), Directive 2000/78 (framework equality), and Directive 2006/54 (sex equality in employment), examining differences in scope, the grounds covered, and the availability of justification for direct discrimination.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Equal Treatment Directives and the Broadened Equality Framework
questions.text: The Age Equality Directive 2000/78 has generated an extensive jurisprudence from the Court of Justice, including Mangold, Kücükdeveci, and Palacios de la Villa. Critically examine how the Court has addressed the relationship between the prohibition on age discrimination and member states' legitimate social policy objectives in the fields of employment and retirement, assessing whether the Court's approach in Mangold — treating the ban on age discrimination as a general principle of EU law — is constitutionally sound.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Equal Treatment Directives and the Broadened Equality Framework
questions.text: Fionn, aged 62, is a pilot employed by AirLink Ltd, an Irish airline. He is compulsorily retired under a collective agreement that sets 62 as the mandatory retirement age for pilots, justified on grounds of flight safety. He also claims that the airline's 'accelerated progression' scheme, under which younger pilots receive higher pay increases to attract them to the profession, constitutes age discrimination in pay. Advise on: (i) whether the mandatory retirement age can be justified under Article 6 of Directive 2000/78 as a legitimate employment policy objective; (ii) the relevance of the public safety objective for pilots in particular; (iii) whether the pay progression scheme constitutes direct age discrimination; and (iv) whether Fionn can rely on the Directive directly against AirLink.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Freedom of Establishment: Article 49 TFEU and the Right to Set Up in Business
questions.text: Article 49 TFEU prohibits restrictions on freedom of establishment for EU nationals and companies. Critically examine the personal and material scope of Article 49, explaining how the Court of Justice has defined establishment, distinguished it from services, addressed discrimination and non-discriminatory obstacles to establishment, and analyse the significance of the Daily Mail, Centros, and Überseering cases for company law and the right of corporations to establish freely across the EU.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Freedom of Establishment: Article 49 TFEU and the Right to Set Up in Business
questions.text: In Gebhard (C-55/94), the Court of Justice identified a four-part test for assessing whether national restrictions on establishment are compatible with Article 49 TFEU: the restriction must be applied in a non-discriminatory manner, must be justified by imperative requirements in the general interest, must be suitable for securing attainment of the objective, and must not go beyond what is necessary. Critically examine the application of this test, assessing whether national professional qualification requirements and authorisation regimes for lawyers, doctors and architects can survive proportionality review under Article 49.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Freedom of Establishment: Article 49 TFEU and the Right to Set Up in Business
questions.text: FinancePlus AG, a German financial services company, wishes to establish a wholly owned subsidiary in Ireland to offer retail banking services. The Central Bank of Ireland imposes additional capital requirements and governance obligations on subsidiaries of non-Irish parent companies not imposed on indigenous Irish banks. FinancePlus argues this is a restriction on establishment. Advise on: (i) whether Article 49 TFEU protects secondary establishment by corporate bodies; (ii) whether the additional requirements constitute a restriction on establishment; (iii) whether the requirements can be justified under imperative requirements of public interest (financial stability, consumer protection); and (iv) the proportionality analysis.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Free Movement of Services: Article 56 TFEU and the Services Directive
questions.text: Article 56 TFEU prohibits restrictions on the free movement of services between member states. Critically examine the relationship between freedom of services and freedom of establishment, explaining the criteria by which the Court distinguishes temporary service provision from permanent establishment, and assess how the Services Directive (2006/123) has sought to create a general framework for cross-border services by abolishing authorisation requirements except where justified under strict conditions.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Free Movement of Services: Article 56 TFEU and the Services Directive
questions.text: The Services Directive 2006/123 adopted a country-of-origin approach to service regulation, requiring member states to accept services provided by operators lawfully established in other member states. Critically assess the constitutional significance of this approach, examining the sectors excluded from the Directive, the permitted grounds for refusing entry to service providers from other member states, and the extent to which the Directive achieves a genuine liberalisation of services markets.

questions.subject: EU Law
questions.lessonTitle: Free Movement of Services: Article 56 TFEU and the Services Directive
questions.text: BuildRight Ltd, an Irish construction company, is engaged to carry out building work in Paris under a short-term contract. The French authorities require BuildRight to comply with French rules on posted worker minimum wages, working hours, and mandatory social insurance contributions, in addition to Irish law requirements which already cover BuildRight's workers. Advise on: (i) whether BuildRight's activities constitute temporary service provision under Article 56 TFEU; (ii) the obligations imposed by the Posted Workers Directive (96/71) as amended by Directive 2018/957; (iii) whether double social insurance contributions are compatible with EU law; and (iv) the relationship between the freedom to provide services and the social protection justification relied on by France.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the principle of relativity of title in Irish property law. In your answer, consider the distinction between absolute and relative title, explain why Irish land law does not protect absolute ownership but rather the best relative title, and illustrate the principle with reference to decided cases.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the maxim 'nemo dat quod non habet' (no one can give what they do not have) in the context of Irish property law. In your answer, consider the exceptions to this principle — including those arising under the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 — and assess the extent to which these exceptions undermine the nemo dat rule.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the interaction between the principle of relativity of title and the system of registration of title under the Registration of Title Act 1964. In your answer, consider how the mirror, curtain, and insurance principles of registered land modify the operation of relativity of title.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the rules governing title to found objects in Irish law. In your answer, consider the relative rights of the finder, the owner or occupier of the land on which the object was found, and the original owner where identifiable, and illustrate your answer with relevant case law.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the decision in Elwes v Brigg Gas Co [1886] and Bridge v Hawkesworth [1851] and assess what principles these cases establish regarding the relative title to objects found on or in land. In your answer, consider how these principles have been applied in subsequent Irish and English decisions.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the legal position of objects found on the foreshore or in tidal waters in Ireland. In your answer, consider whether the State or the landowner has superior title to such objects and assess the impact of the National Monuments Act 1930 and subsequent legislation on the rights of finders.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Joint Tenancy and Tenancy in Common
questions.text: Explain the distinction between joint tenancy and tenancy in common in Irish property law. In your answer, consider the four unities required for a joint tenancy, the concept of the right of survivorship (jus accrescendi), and the consequences of this distinction for the transmission of co-owned property on death.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Joint Tenancy and Tenancy in Common
questions.text: Critically examine the rules for distinguishing between joint tenancy and tenancy in common. In your answer, consider the use of words of severance, the equitable presumptions that arise in commercial transactions and in purchases of property by cohabitants, and how the courts determine the nature of co-ownership in cases of ambiguity.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Joint Tenancy and Tenancy in Common
questions.text: Discuss the right of survivorship (jus accrescendi) in joint tenancy. In your answer, consider how the right operates, the circumstances in which equity will displace the right of survivorship, and assess whether the right of survivorship is an appropriate default rule for co-owners.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Creation of Co-Ownership under the LCLRA 2009
questions.text: Explain how the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 has reformed the law governing the creation of co-ownership in Ireland. In your answer, consider the statutory presumption introduced by section 30 of the 2009 Act and assess the extent to which it changes the pre-2009 position at common law and in equity.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Creation of Co-Ownership under the LCLRA 2009
questions.text: Critically examine the statutory framework for the creation of joint tenancy and tenancy in common under the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009. In your answer, consider the role of express declaration of trust, the default rules applicable in the absence of such declaration, and the courts' approach to disputed beneficial ownership.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Creation of Co-Ownership under the LCLRA 2009
questions.text: Discuss the equitable presumptions relevant to the creation of co-ownership where property is purchased in the names of two or more persons. In your answer, consider the presumption of resulting trust, the presumption of advancement, and the extent to which these presumptions survive the enactment of the 2009 Act.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the rules governing the severance of a joint tenancy in Irish property law. In your answer, consider the methods of severance recognised at common law and in equity, including alienation, mutual agreement, and mutual conduct, and explain how severance affects the title of the remaining joint tenants.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the method of severance by written notice under section 30(2) of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009. In your answer, consider what constitutes an effective written notice, whether the notice must be communicated, and assess how this statutory method has simplified the law of severance.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the equitable doctrines under which a course of conduct or mutual agreement may sever a joint tenancy. In your answer, consider the decisions in Williams v Hensman (1861) and Burgess v Rawnsley [1975] and assess the circumstances in which a course of dealings will be sufficient to effect severance in equity.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the legal rights and obligations of co-owners inter se in Irish property law. In your answer, consider the right to occupation, the right to a share of income and profits, the liability for repairs and improvements, and the rights of a co-owner to seek a court order for partition or sale under section 31 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the court's jurisdiction under section 31 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 to order the partition or sale of co-owned property. In your answer, consider the factors the court takes into account, the interests of co-owners and third parties, and assess whether the 2009 Act has struck an appropriate balance between the right to sell and the right to remain in occupation.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the position of a co-owner who has contributed to the cost of improvements to co-owned property. In your answer, consider the extent to which a co-owner may claim an enhanced beneficial share in the property by reason of improvements and assess the rules applicable where improvements are carried out without the other co-owner's consent.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of adverse possession in Irish law. In your answer, consider the historical basis of the doctrine, the statutory framework under the Statute of Limitations 1957 and the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009, and assess why adverse possession remains a controversial feature of Irish land law.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the effect of adverse possession on the title of the registered owner. In your answer, consider the difference between the position under unregistered land and registered land in Ireland following the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 and assess the implications of these differences for registered proprietors.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the policy justifications for and against the doctrine of adverse possession. In your answer, consider the interests of long-term possessors, the interests of registered owners, the public interest in productive use of land, and the competing considerations of legal certainty and the protection of property rights.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the requirements that must be satisfied for a squatter to establish a claim to title by adverse possession. In your answer, consider the elements of factual possession and the intention to possess (animus possidendi), the requirement that possession must be inconsistent with the owner's title, and the relevant limitation periods.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the requirement of 'animus possidendi' (intention to possess) in adverse possession claims. In your answer, consider how Irish and English courts have interpreted this requirement and assess the significance of the decisions in Pye v Graham [2003] and subsequent case law for Irish property law.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the acts that will and will not constitute sufficient 'factual possession' for the purposes of an adverse possession claim. In your answer, illustrate with case law the kinds of acts the courts regard as establishing possession adverse to the owner's title and assess whether the current requirement of 'factual possession' is appropriately calibrated.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Special Cases and Advanced Issues in Adverse Possession
questions.text: Explain the rules governing adverse possession of registered land in Ireland under the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 and the Registration of Title Act 1964. In your answer, consider the notification requirements introduced by the 2009 Act and assess whether these provisions adequately protect registered owners.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Special Cases and Advanced Issues in Adverse Possession
questions.text: Critically examine the position of a squatter who is a tenant or licensee of the paper title owner. In your answer, consider whether a tenant can adversely possess against a landlord, the circumstances in which a licensee's possession may become adverse, and the implications for claims by family members in possession of land.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Special Cases and Advanced Issues in Adverse Possession
questions.text: Discuss the effect of acknowledgement of title on an adverse possession claim. In your answer, consider when an acknowledgement will restart the limitation period, the formalities required for an effective acknowledgement under the Statute of Limitations 1957, and assess the practical implications of this rule for squatters who have entered into negotiations with the owner.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the nature of an easement in Irish property law and set out the four characteristics identified in Re Ellenborough Park [1956] that must be present for a right to qualify as an easement. In your answer, illustrate each characteristic with relevant case law.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the requirement that an easement must accommodate and serve the dominant tenement. In your answer, consider how the courts have interpreted this requirement — particularly in cases involving commercial benefits — and assess whether the current approach is sufficiently flexible to accommodate modern land use.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the distinction between an easement and a profit à prendre. In your answer, consider the legal differences between these two rights, how each is created and extinguished, and assess the practical implications of the distinction for landowners.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the methods by which an easement may be expressly or impliedly created in Irish property law. In your answer, consider express grant, express reservation, implied grant (including the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows (1879) and the general words in a conveyance), and the doctrine of easements of necessity.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows (1879) as a method of implied grant of easements. In your answer, consider the three conditions that must be satisfied for the rule to apply, its interaction with section 6 of the Conveyancing Act 1881 (now section 71 of the LCLRA 2009), and assess whether the rule adequately protects the expectations of purchasers of part of a seller's land.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the doctrine of easements of necessity and the doctrine of implied reservation. In your answer, consider the narrow circumstances in which an easement of necessity will be implied, the courts' reluctance to imply reservations in favour of a seller, and assess whether the current rules strike an appropriate balance between the parties to a conveyance.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the methods by which an easement may be acquired by prescription at common law, under the doctrine of lost modern grant, and under the Prescription Act 1832. In your answer, consider the requirements for each method and assess which method is most likely to succeed in practice in Ireland.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the doctrine of lost modern grant as a basis for prescriptive acquisition of easements. In your answer, consider the legal fiction involved, the period of use required, the circumstances in which the fictional grant will be defeated, and assess whether the doctrine is an adequate substitute for direct statutory prescription.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the reform of prescription law in Ireland effected by Part 8 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009. In your answer, consider how the 2009 Act changed the basis for prescriptive claims, the transitional provisions applicable to rights acquired before the Act, and assess whether the 2009 Act has achieved a satisfactory simplification of the law.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Extinguishment of Easements and Profits à Prendre
questions.text: Explain the circumstances in which an easement may be extinguished in Irish property law. In your answer, consider extinguishment by express release, by implied release (including the doctrine of abandonment), by merger of the dominant and servient tenements, and by statute.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Extinguishment of Easements and Profits à Prendre
questions.text: Critically examine the doctrine of abandonment as a method of extinguishing an easement. In your answer, consider the standard of intention required, the significance of non-user, and assess the decisions in Benn v Hardinge (1992) and Gotobed v Pridmore (1971) in establishing what constitutes sufficient evidence of an intention to abandon.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Extinguishment of Easements and Profits à Prendre
questions.text: Explain the nature of a profit à prendre and distinguish it from an easement. In your answer, consider the types of profits recognised in Irish law, the methods by which a profit may be created, the rights it confers on its holder, and how it may be extinguished.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the rules governing the enforceability of freehold covenants at common law. In your answer, consider the requirement of privity of contract, the rules for the passing of the benefit and burden of a covenant at common law, and assess why the common law rules were considered inadequate.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the rule that the burden of a freehold covenant does not run at common law. In your answer, consider the decision in Austerberry v Oldham Corporation (1885) and the qualification in Halsall v Brizell [1957], and assess whether the inability to enforce covenants against successors in title at common law creates significant practical problems in modern land transactions.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the conditions that must be satisfied for the benefit of a freehold covenant to pass to successors in title of the original covenantee at common law. In your answer, consider the requirements established by the courts — including that the covenant touches and concerns the dominant land — and assess whether these conditions are adequately defined.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Freehold Covenants in Equity and under the LCLRA 2009
questions.text: Explain the rules in equity for the passing of the benefit and burden of freehold restrictive covenants. In your answer, consider the rule in Tulk v Moxhay (1848), the conditions for the burden to pass in equity, and the conditions for the benefit to pass by annexation, assignment, or scheme of development.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Freehold Covenants in Equity and under the LCLRA 2009
questions.text: Critically examine the doctrine of a building scheme (scheme of development) in Irish property law. In your answer, consider the conditions established in Elliston v Reacher [1908] for a valid building scheme to arise, the consequences of a valid scheme for covenants between purchasers, and assess whether the doctrine remains appropriate for modern housing developments.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Freehold Covenants in Equity and under the LCLRA 2009
questions.text: Discuss the reforms to the law of freehold covenants introduced by the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009. In your answer, consider whether the 2009 Act has adequately modernised the rules for the enforceability of positive and restrictive covenants against successors in title, and assess the extent to which problems that existed prior to the Act persist.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the protection conferred by the Family Home Protection Act 1976 on the non-owning spouse in relation to the family home. In your answer, consider the requirement for prior written consent to any conveyance of the family home and assess the consequences of a purported conveyance without such consent.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the definition of 'family home' under the Family Home Protection Act 1976. In your answer, consider the case law that has developed around this definition, including the requirement that the home be the 'ordinary residence' of the spouse who is not the legal owner, and assess the scope of the protection in practice.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the rights of a purchaser of a family home in the context of the Family Home Protection Act 1976. In your answer, consider the protection available to a bona fide purchaser for value without notice of the non-owning spouse's rights and assess how the courts have balanced the interests of the purchaser against those of the non-owning spouse.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the property rights conferred on civil partners by the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 in relation to the shared home. In your answer, compare the protection provided to civil partners with that provided to married spouses under the Family Home Protection Act 1976.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the property rights available to cohabitants under Part 15 of the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010. In your answer, consider the criteria for qualifying as a 'qualified cohabitant', the redress orders available to a cohabitant on relationship breakdown, and assess whether these provisions adequately protect cohabitants' property interests.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss how the courts determine the beneficial interests of cohabitants in jointly occupied property outside the statutory regime. In your answer, consider the constructive trust and resulting trust principles applicable to cohabiting couples and assess whether the law provides adequate and certain protection for cohabitants' property interests.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Family Law Acts and Property Adjustment Orders
questions.text: Explain the property adjustment order jurisdiction available to the courts on marital breakdown under the Family Law Act 1995 and the Family Law (Divorce) Act 1996. In your answer, consider the types of property adjustment orders that may be made, the factors the court takes into account, and assess the constitutional basis for this jurisdiction.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Family Law Acts and Property Adjustment Orders
questions.text: Critically examine the concept of 'proper provision' for spouses and children in the context of property adjustment orders in Ireland. In your answer, consider the factors that the court must have regard to under the Family Law Act 1995 and assess whether the legislative framework provides sufficient certainty for parties dealing with family property.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Family Law Acts and Property Adjustment Orders
questions.text: Discuss the interaction between property adjustment orders made under family law legislation and the property rights of third parties (including mortgagees and other creditors) in relation to the family home. In your answer, consider how the courts balance the interests of the family against those of secured creditors.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the effect of a spouse's or civil partner's bankruptcy on title to the family home in Irish law. In your answer, consider the rights of the official assignee in bankruptcy, the protection conferred by the Family Home Protection Act 1976 and the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010, and assess how the courts balance the interests of creditors with those of the non-bankrupt spouse.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the provisions of the Personal Insolvency Act 2012 relevant to the family home. In your answer, consider the personal insolvency arrangements available to debtors in financial difficulty and assess whether these arrangements provide adequate protection for the family home whilst fairly addressing the interests of secured creditors.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the position of the non-owning spouse or civil partner where the legal owner is adjudicated a bankrupt. In your answer, consider the extent to which the non-owning partner can resist the official assignee's claim to possession and sale of the family home and assess whether Irish insolvency law strikes an appropriate balance between the competing interests involved.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the historical methods by which mortgages were created over freehold and leasehold property before the enactment of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009. In your answer, consider the mortgage by conveyance and reconveyance, the mortgage by demise, and the equitable mortgage, and assess the practical difficulties each created.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the method of creating a mortgage under the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009. In your answer, consider how the 2009 Act simplified the creation of mortgages, the concept of the 'charge by deed expressed to be by way of legal mortgage', and assess whether the 2009 Act has achieved a satisfactory reform of mortgage creation.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the distinction between a legal mortgage and an equitable mortgage. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which an equitable mortgage arises, the methods of creating an equitable mortgage (including deposit of title deeds and agreement to create a legal mortgage), and assess the practical significance of this distinction for lenders.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the remedies available to a mortgagee where the mortgagor is in default under Irish property law. In your answer, consider the mortgagee's rights to sue on the personal covenant, to appoint a receiver, to take possession, to foreclose, and to exercise the power of sale, and assess which remedy is most commonly used in practice.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the mortgagee's power of sale under Irish law. In your answer, consider when the power of sale arises and becomes exercisable, the mortgagee's duty of care in exercising the power of sale with reference to Holohan v Friends Provident and Century Life [1966] and subsequent case law, and assess the extent to which the law adequately protects mortgagors.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the right of a mortgagee to take possession of mortgaged property. In your answer, consider when the right to possession arises, the restrictions on the exercise of this right introduced by the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009, and assess whether the court's jurisdiction to adjourn possession proceedings is an adequate safeguard for mortgagors in financial difficulty.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Equity of Redemption and Clogs on the Equity
questions.text: Explain the concept of the equity of redemption in Irish mortgage law. In your answer, consider the historic importance of the equity of redemption as a protection for mortgagors, the equitable rules preventing clogs and fetters on the equity, and assess how these rules continue to operate in modern mortgage practice.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Equity of Redemption and Clogs on the Equity
questions.text: Critically examine the equitable doctrine that prohibits 'clogs on the equity of redemption'. In your answer, consider the three main categories of clog — collateral advantages, options to purchase, and unconscionable terms — and assess how the courts have balanced the mortgagor's right to redeem against the freedom of contract principle.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Equity of Redemption and Clogs on the Equity
questions.text: Discuss the doctrine that 'once a mortgage, always a mortgage'. In your answer, consider how this doctrine has been applied by the courts to protect the mortgagor's right of redemption and assess whether the current approach strikes an appropriate balance between the protection of mortgagors and the commercial freedom of lending institutions.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the statutory protections available to consumer mortgagors in Ireland. In your answer, consider the provisions of the Consumer Credit Act 1995, the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009, and the Personal Insolvency Act 2012, and assess whether these protections provide adequate safeguards for residential mortgage holders.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the provisions of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 governing possession proceedings brought by mortgagees of residential property. In your answer, consider the court's power to adjourn proceedings, to impose conditions on the grant of a possession order, and assess whether these powers adequately protect family home owners.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the regulatory framework governing the conduct of mortgage lenders in Ireland, including the Central Bank's Code of Conduct on Mortgage Arrears. In your answer, consider the interaction between regulatory requirements and the legal rights of mortgagees, and assess whether the regulatory framework supplements the statutory protections for mortgagors effectively.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the nature of a licence in Irish land law and distinguish it from a proprietary interest such as an easement or a lease. In your answer, consider the four types of licence recognised by Irish law — bare licences, licences coupled with an interest, contractual licences, and licences by estoppel — and explain how each arises.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the distinction between a licence and a lease in Irish property law. In your answer, consider the factors the courts consider — particularly the grant of exclusive possession — and assess the significance of the decision in Street v Mountford [1985] for the Irish law of licences.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the revocability of licences in Irish law. In your answer, consider which types of licence are freely revocable at common law, the circumstances in which revocation may be restrained in equity or at law, and assess whether the rules governing revocability adequately protect the reasonable expectations of licensees.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the nature of a contractual licence and distinguish it from a bare licence. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which a contractual licence arises, the rights it confers on the licensee, and the extent to which a contractual licence can be enforced against third parties who acquire the licensor's land.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the question of whether a contractual licence can be enforced against a successor in title of the licensor. In your answer, consider the decisions in Clore v Theatrical Properties Ltd [1936] and Errington v Errington [1952] and assess the current Irish position on the bindingness of contractual licences on third parties.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the remedies available to a licensee where a contractual licence is wrongfully revoked. In your answer, consider whether the licensee may seek an injunction to restrain eviction or to enforce re-entry, and assess the circumstances in which equity will intervene to prevent the licensor from reneging on the contractual licence.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of proprietary estoppel in Irish property law. In your answer, consider the elements that must be established to found a successful claim — representation or assurance, reliance, and detriment — and illustrate each element with relevant case law.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the remedial discretion exercised by the courts in proprietary estoppel cases. In your answer, consider the range of remedies available — from a personal award of compensation to the transfer of a freehold interest — and assess the extent to which the court's approach satisfies the claimant's expectation, compensates for detriment, or simply prevents unconscionability.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between proprietary estoppel and constructive trust in Irish property law. In your answer, consider whether the two doctrines are truly distinct or whether they overlap significantly, and assess the circumstances in which a court is more likely to employ one doctrine rather than the other to protect an informal interest in land.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the nature of a right of residence in Irish property law. In your answer, consider how a right of residence is typically created, whether it constitutes a proprietary or a personal right, the consequences of this classification for its enforceability against successors in title, and the relevant provisions of the Registration of Title Act 1964.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the Irish case law on the enforceability of rights of residence against successors in title. In your answer, consider whether a right of residence can be a burden on registered land under the Registration of Title Act 1964 and assess the extent to which the courts have been willing to protect the holders of such rights against third-party purchasers.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the practical problems that arise in relation to rights of residence and assess the extent to which the current law provides adequate protection for elderly and vulnerable persons whose only security is such a right. In your answer, consider the interaction between rights of residence and the Family Home Protection Act 1976 and related legislation.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Nature of a Lease and Distinction from Licence
questions.text: Explain the essential characteristics of a lease (or tenancy) in Irish property law. In your answer, consider the requirement that a lease must grant exclusive possession for a certain term at a rent, and assess how the courts determine the proper characterisation of an agreement as either a lease or a licence.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Nature of a Lease and Distinction from Licence
questions.text: Critically examine the decision in Street v Mountford [1985] and its application in the Irish context. In your answer, consider the significance of exclusive possession as the hallmark of a tenancy, the circumstances in which a court will look behind the label given to an agreement, and assess how this decision has affected drafting practices.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Nature of a Lease and Distinction from Licence
questions.text: Discuss the requirement that a lease must be for a 'certain term' in Irish property law. In your answer, consider the rules governing leases of uncertain duration with reference to the decision in Prudential Assurance Co v London Residuary Body [1992] and assess the implications of uncertainty of term for the validity of a lease.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the rules governing the enforceability of leasehold covenants between landlord and tenant and their respective successors in title in Irish property law. In your answer, consider the common law rules of privity of contract and privity of estate and assess the extent to which these rules enable the enforcement of covenants by and against successors.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the covenant to repair and maintain in a lease. In your answer, consider the extent of a landlord's and tenant's respective repairing obligations, the standard of repair required, the remedies available for breach, and assess whether the law appropriately allocates repairing responsibilities between the parties.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the landlord's covenant for quiet enjoyment in a lease and the related covenant not to derogate from grant. In your answer, consider the scope of each covenant, the circumstances in which a breach arises, the remedies available to a tenant, and assess whether these implied covenants adequately protect the tenant's right to undisturbed occupation.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the principal rights and obligations conferred on landlords and tenants of residential dwelling houses by the Residential Tenancies Act 2004 (as amended). In your answer, consider the tenant's right to security of tenure, the landlord's rights of termination, and the role of the Residential Tenancies Board in regulating disputes.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the security of tenure provisions of the Residential Tenancies Act 2004 as amended by the Residential Tenancies (Amendment) Acts. In your answer, consider the conditions for the exercise of a landlord's right to terminate under each of the permitted grounds and assess whether the current framework provides adequate and meaningful security of tenure for residential tenants.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the rent review and rent certainty provisions of the Residential Tenancies Act 2004 as amended. In your answer, consider the restrictions on rent increases introduced in Rent Pressure Zones, assess whether these provisions are consistent with the constitutional protection of property rights under Article 43, and evaluate their effectiveness in addressing housing affordability.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the principal features of a commercial lease in Ireland. In your answer, consider the standard covenants typically found in commercial leases — including rent review, user, alienation, and repair obligations — and assess how the negotiation and drafting of such leases reflects the respective bargaining positions of landlord and tenant.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the Landlord and Tenant Acts as they apply to commercial tenants in Ireland. In your answer, consider the right to a new tenancy under the Landlord and Tenant (Amendment) Act 1980 and the grounds on which a landlord can oppose the grant of a new tenancy, and assess whether the legislative framework provides adequate protection for commercial tenants.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the nature of a ground rent in Irish property law. In your answer, consider the legal framework governing the acquisition of the fee simple by residential ground rent tenants under the Landlord and Tenant (Ground Rents) Acts, the conditions for exercising this right, and assess whether the ground rent legislation has achieved its social objective of enabling occupiers to become freeholders.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the requirements for testamentary capacity under the Succession Act 1965. In your answer, consider the age requirement, the statutory requirements for the formal validity of a will, the mental capacity requirements derived from Banks v Goodfellow (1870), and assess what the propounder of a will must establish to rebut a challenge to capacity.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the requirement that a testator must have testamentary capacity at the time of making the will. In your answer, consider the 'lucid interval' doctrine, the interaction between capacity and undue influence, and assess the difficulties of proving lack of capacity in probate litigation.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the formal requirements for a valid will under the Succession Act 1965. In your answer, consider the requirements of writing, signature, and attestation, the relaxation of strict formalities introduced by the Civil Law (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2008, and assess whether the current formal requirements adequately prevent fraud while accommodating the reasonable expectations of testators.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Revocation, Codicils and Alteration of Wills
questions.text: Explain the circumstances in which a will may be revoked under the Succession Act 1965. In your answer, consider revocation by subsequent will or codicil, by burning, tearing, or destruction, and by the testator's marriage, and assess the legal consequences of each method of revocation.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Revocation, Codicils and Alteration of Wills
questions.text: Critically examine the doctrine of dependent relative revocation in Irish succession law. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which a court will treat a purported revocation as conditional and inoperative, the policy reasons underlying the doctrine, and assess whether the current approach is consistent with the testator's probable intention.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Revocation, Codicils and Alteration of Wills
questions.text: Discuss the legal effect of an alteration to a will under the Succession Act 1965. In your answer, consider the distinction between alterations made before and after execution, the evidential presumption relating to undated alterations, and assess whether the current rules strike an appropriate balance between protecting testamentary freedom and preventing fraud.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the principles of construction applicable to wills under Irish law. In your answer, consider the 'armchair principle', the rule that the court gives effect to the testator's actual intention, the admissibility of extrinsic evidence under section 90 of the Succession Act 1965, and illustrate the principles with relevant case law.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the consequences of a gift in a will failing. In your answer, consider the circumstances giving rise to lapse, the anti-lapse provisions in the Succession Act 1965 applicable to gifts to children and issue of the testator, and assess whether the statutory provisions adequately reflect the likely intentions of testators.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the construction of gifts to a class in a will. In your answer, consider the rules for determining who qualifies as a member of the class, the 'class-closing' rules applicable where some members are ascertained at the testator's death and others are not, and assess whether these rules correspond to the reasonable expectations of testators.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Legal Right Share: Section 111 of the Succession Act 1965
questions.text: Explain the right of a surviving spouse to a legal right share under section 111 of the Succession Act 1965. In your answer, consider the proportion of the estate to which the surviving spouse is entitled (depending on whether children survive), the nature of the legal right share as a charge on the estate, and the procedure by which the spouse may elect to take the legal right share in lieu of any devise or bequest.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Legal Right Share: Section 111 of the Succession Act 1965
questions.text: Critically examine the relationship between the surviving spouse's legal right share and the testator's freedom of testation under section 111 of the Succession Act 1965. In your answer, consider the extent to which the testator can reduce the value of the legal right share by inter vivos transfers and assess whether the Act provides adequate protection for the surviving spouse's expectations.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Legal Right Share: Section 111 of the Succession Act 1965
questions.text: Discuss the consequences of a surviving spouse's renunciation of the legal right share. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which a valid renunciation may be made, the formalities required, and the implications of renunciation for the distribution of the estate, including the rights of any children.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Provision for Children: Section 117 of the Succession Act 1965
questions.text: Explain the application under section 117 of the Succession Act 1965 by which a child may seek provision from a parent's estate. In your answer, consider the test applied by the court (whether the testator 'failed in his moral duty to make proper provision' for the child), the factors the court takes into account, and the time limit for bringing such an application.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Provision for Children: Section 117 of the Succession Act 1965
questions.text: Critically examine the 'moral duty' test applied under section 117 of the Succession Act 1965. In your answer, consider the case law that has developed around this concept, including the factors that are relevant to the assessment of the testator's moral duty — such as the financial needs of the child, the size of the estate, and the relationship between the parties — and assess whether the test provides adequate certainty.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.lessonTitle: Provision for Children: Section 117 of the Succession Act 1965
questions.text: Discuss the impact of a successful section 117 application on the administration of an estate. In your answer, consider the priority of a section 117 order relative to the rights of a surviving spouse, the rights of other beneficiaries under the will, and the rights of creditors, and assess whether the current statutory framework fairly balances all competing claims on the estate.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Explain the rules governing the distribution of an intestate estate under Part VI of the Succession Act 1965. In your answer, consider the shares to which a surviving spouse, children, and more remote relatives are entitled under the Act, and assess the extent to which the statutory distribution scheme reflects the likely wishes of those who die without a valid will.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Critically examine the rights of the surviving spouse and children of a person who dies wholly intestate under the Succession Act 1965. In your answer, consider the provisions of sections 67 and 67A and assess whether the Act achieves a fair balance between the competing claims of the surviving spouse and the deceased's children from different relationships.

questions.subject: Property Law
questions.text: Discuss the position of a person who dies partially intestate — that is, where a will disposes of part but not all of the estate. In your answer, consider the interaction between the testate and intestate portions of the estate, the application of the hotchpot rule under section 114 of the Succession Act 1965, and assess whether the Act provides clear and practical rules for the administration of partially intestate estates.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Nature and Function of Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the nature and function of tort law. In your answer, consider how tort law differs from contract law and criminal law, the interests that tort law protects, and the principal heads of general damages available to a successful plaintiff in a personal injury action in Ireland.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Nature and Function of Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the goals of tort law — compensation, deterrence, vindication, and loss-spreading — and assess the extent to which the Irish civil liability system achieves each of these objectives in practice.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Nature and Function of Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the distinction between misfeasance (active wrongdoing) and nonfeasance (failure to act) in Irish tort law. In your answer, consider the general reluctance of the courts to impose liability for omissions and assess whether this distinction is defensible as a matter of legal principle.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: FE-1 Tort Paper Structure and Exam Strategy
questions.text: In an FE-1 Tort Law problem question involving a road traffic accident in which a driver, a cyclist and a pedestrian are all injured due to allegedly negligent road maintenance by a local authority, outline the legal issues you would need to address and explain how you would structure your answer. Identify the relevant cause(s) of action, the key elements to establish, and any significant defences or issues of apportionment that would arise.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: FE-1 Tort Paper Structure and Exam Strategy
questions.text: You are presented with the following scenario in an FE-1 Tort Law exam: a factory owner installs new machinery. A worker is injured when the machine malfunctions. A visiting client slips on an unmarked wet floor. A delivery driver suffers psychiatric injury after witnessing the accident. Identify the separate tort claims that arise on these facts, the relevant legal principles governing each, and the order in which you would address them in an exam answer.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: FE-1 Tort Paper Structure and Exam Strategy
questions.text: Critically evaluate the approach a candidate should take when answering an FE-1 Tort Law essay question that asks them to 'assess the development and current state of the duty of care in Irish negligence law'. In your answer, consider how to structure the essay, the key areas of doctrine to address, the case law that must be discussed, and how to demonstrate critical analysis rather than mere description.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Historical Development and the Glencar Test
questions.text: Trace the development of the duty of care in Irish negligence law from Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] through to the adoption of the Glencar test in Glencar Exploration plc v Mayo County Council [2002]. In your answer, explain why the Supreme Court in Glencar departed from the broad two-stage Anns/Ward approach and assess the significance of this departure for the scope of negligence liability in Ireland.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Historical Development and the Glencar Test
questions.text: Explain the three-part test for the existence of a duty of care established in Glencar Exploration plc v Mayo County Council [2002]. In your answer, consider each element — foreseeability, proximity, and the just and reasonable requirement — and assess how the courts have applied these elements in subsequent Irish case law.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Historical Development and the Glencar Test
questions.text: Critically assess the 'just and reasonable' stage of the Glencar duty of care test. In your answer, consider the policy considerations that the courts have used to deny or limit a duty of care and evaluate whether this stage of the test imports an inappropriate degree of judicial policy-making into the analysis.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Special Duty Situations: Omissions, Third Parties and Public Authorities
questions.text: Explain the circumstances in which a defendant may owe a duty of care to a plaintiff in respect of harm caused by the positive act of a third party. In your answer, consider the exceptions to the general rule of no liability for third-party acts — including special relationships, the assumption of responsibility, and the creation of danger — and illustrate with relevant case law.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Special Duty Situations: Omissions, Third Parties and Public Authorities
questions.text: Critically examine the liability of public authorities in negligence in Ireland. In your answer, consider the distinction between operational and policy decisions, the circumstances in which the courts have imposed a duty of care on public authorities, and assess whether the current framework strikes an appropriate balance between accountability and the freedom of public bodies to exercise statutory discretions.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Special Duty Situations: Omissions, Third Parties and Public Authorities
questions.text: Discuss the circumstances in which a person may owe a duty of care in respect of a failure to act (an omission). In your answer, consider the categories of special relationship that give rise to a duty to act and assess whether the Irish courts have been consistent in their approach to omissions liability.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Pure Economic Loss and Negligent Misstatement
questions.text: Explain why Irish tort law is generally reluctant to impose liability for pure economic loss caused by negligent acts (as opposed to physical damage). In your answer, consider the policy reasons for this limitation, the principal exceptions recognised by the courts, and assess whether the current law strikes an appropriate balance.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Pure Economic Loss and Negligent Misstatement
questions.text: Critically examine the liability in negligence for negligent misstatement causing pure economic loss. In your answer, consider the special relationship requirement established in Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] and the subsequent development of this principle in Irish law through the concept of voluntary assumption of responsibility.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Pure Economic Loss and Negligent Misstatement
questions.text: Discuss the liability of a professional adviser (such as a solicitor, accountant, or financial advisor) for negligent advice causing pure economic loss to a client. In your answer, consider the duty of care owed, the standard of care applicable to professionals, and assess whether the current law adequately protects clients who suffer financial loss as a result of relying on defective professional advice.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Objective Standard and the Reasonable Person
questions.text: Explain the objective standard of care in Irish negligence law. In your answer, consider what the law means by 'the reasonable person', the circumstances in which a defendant's personal characteristics (such as age, physical disability, or professional qualification) are taken into account, and assess whether the objective standard is appropriate as a general benchmark.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Objective Standard and the Reasonable Person
questions.text: Critically examine the standard of care applicable to persons who profess a special skill or expertise. In your answer, consider the Bolam test and its Irish application, the extent to which the courts defer to the views of a professional body on questions of standard of care, and assess whether expert testimony should be determinative of breach in professional negligence cases.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: The Objective Standard and the Reasonable Person
questions.text: Discuss the standard of care applicable to learner drivers and other persons who engage in activities for which they lack full qualification or experience. In your answer, consider whether the objective standard of the competent practitioner should apply to novices, refer to the relevant Irish and English case law, and assess the policy implications of the answer.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Breach of Duty and the Factors Courts Consider
questions.text: Explain the factors that a court will take into account when determining whether a defendant has breached a duty of care. In your answer, consider the probability of harm, the severity of the potential harm, the social utility of the defendant's conduct, the cost of precautions, and any applicable statutory standards, and illustrate each factor with relevant case law.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Breach of Duty and the Factors Courts Consider
questions.text: Critically examine the role of risk assessment in determining breach of duty in Irish negligence law. In your answer, consider the 'calculus of risk' approach, the relevance of industry custom and practice, and assess whether the current approach to breach adequately incentivises defendants to take reasonable precautions.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Breach of Duty and the Factors Courts Consider
questions.text: Discuss the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur ('the thing speaks for itself') in Irish negligence law. In your answer, consider the conditions for its application, its procedural effect, and assess whether the doctrine is best understood as creating a presumption of negligence or merely shifting the evidential burden to the defendant.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the 'but-for' test of factual causation in Irish negligence law. In your answer, consider what the plaintiff must establish, the difficulties that arise where the breach of duty is only one of several contributing causes of damage, and illustrate the operation of the test with reference to decided cases.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the decision in Fairchild v Glenhaven Funeral Services Ltd [2002] and similar cases dealing with evidential gaps in causation where the plaintiff cannot prove on the balance of probabilities which defendant's breach caused the damage. In your answer, consider how the material contribution to risk principle operates and assess its application in the Irish context.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the 'loss of a chance' doctrine in the context of medical negligence and other professional liability cases. In your answer, consider whether Irish law permits recovery for the loss of a less than 50% chance of a better medical outcome, with reference to relevant Irish and UK authority, and assess the policy arguments for and against such recovery.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of novus actus interveniens (a new intervening act) in Irish tort law. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which an intervening act — whether by a third party, the plaintiff, or a natural event — will break the chain of causation between the defendant's negligence and the plaintiff's damage.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the circumstances in which the negligent act of a third party will constitute a novus actus interveniens sufficient to break the chain of causation. In your answer, consider the foreseeability test, the distinction between acts that are 'a mere coincidence' and those that are 'directly caused' by the defendant's breach, and illustrate with decided cases.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the extent to which the plaintiff's own conduct following the defendant's breach of duty may constitute a novus actus interveniens. In your answer, consider the distinction between contributory negligence (which reduces damages) and a novus actus (which breaks the chain entirely), and assess the policy considerations that inform this distinction.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the 'material contribution to damage' principle as an alternative to the strict but-for test in cases of cumulative causation. In your answer, consider the decision in Bonnington Castings v Wardlaw [1956], the circumstances in which this principle may be applied in Irish negligence law, and assess its relationship with the but-for test.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the problems of causation in cases involving multiple defendants who each contributed to the plaintiff's injury. In your answer, consider the principles of concurrent and successive tortfeasors, the application of the Civil Liability Act 1961, and assess how the courts apportion liability in cases of indivisible injury caused by multiple defendants.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the application of causation principles in cases of industrial disease where a plaintiff's condition has been caused or contributed to by exposure to harmful substances from multiple employers over many years. In your answer, consider the material contribution and 'material increase in risk' doctrines and assess whether the Irish courts have adopted a coherent approach to causation in such cases.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Remoteness of Damage: The Wagon Mound Test
questions.text: Explain the test of remoteness of damage in Irish negligence law. In your answer, consider the shift from the direct consequence test in Re Polemis [1921] to the foreseeability test established in Overseas Tankship (UK) Ltd v Morts Dock & Engineering Co Ltd (The Wagon Mound No 1) [1961], and assess the significance of this change for the scope of a defendant's liability.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Remoteness of Damage: The Wagon Mound Test
questions.text: Critically examine the 'thin skull' (or 'egg-shell skull') rule in Irish tort law. In your answer, consider how this rule modifies the Wagon Mound foreseeability test, the distinction between types of harm that are foreseeable (even if the extent is not) and entirely unforeseeable harm, and assess whether the thin skull rule is consistent with the underlying rationale of remoteness of damage.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Remoteness of Damage: The Wagon Mound Test
questions.text: Discuss the distinction between the type of damage that must be foreseeable for the purposes of remoteness and the precise manner in which the damage occurs.
