In your answer, consider how the courts have applied the Wagon Mound test in practice — particularly in cases involving unusual or improbable sequences of events — and assess whether the current approach provides an intelligible boundary for tortious liability.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the rules governing recovery for psychiatric injury suffered by primary victims in Irish tort law. In your answer, consider who qualifies as a 'primary victim', the foreseeability requirements applicable to primary victims, and assess whether the distinction between primary and secondary victims is satisfactory as a matter of legal principle.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the decision in Page v Smith [1996] in the context of primary victim psychiatric injury claims. In your answer, consider the controversial proposition that it is sufficient for a primary victim to show that some personal injury was foreseeable (even if purely psychiatric injury was not) and assess the extent to which this approach has been adopted or modified in Irish case law.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the requirement that psychiatric injury must constitute a 'recognised psychiatric illness' before it is recoverable in negligence. In your answer, consider the rationale for this requirement, the types of conditions that have been recognised or rejected by the courts, and assess whether this threshold requirement is appropriately calibrated.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Psychiatric Injury: Secondary Victims and the Kelly v Hennessy Test
questions.text: Explain the test applicable to secondary victim psychiatric injury claims in Ireland as established in Kelly v Hennessy [1995]. In your answer, set out all five elements of the Kelly v Hennessy test and explain how each element must be satisfied by a plaintiff claiming psychiatric injury as a secondary victim.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Psychiatric Injury: Secondary Victims and the Kelly v Hennessy Test
questions.text: Critically examine the requirement of 'sudden shocking perception' in secondary victim psychiatric injury claims. In your answer, consider what satisfies this requirement, the difficulties faced by plaintiffs who witness a slow deterioration rather than a sudden shocking event, and assess how the Irish courts have interpreted and applied this element of the Kelly v Hennessy test.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Psychiatric Injury: Secondary Victims and the Kelly v Hennessy Test
questions.text: Discuss the proximity of relationship requirement in secondary victim psychiatric injury claims. In your answer, consider the range of relationships the courts have recognised as sufficiently proximate — from spouses to bystanders — and assess whether the control mechanisms applicable to secondary victim claims strike an appropriate balance between preventing unlimited liability and providing just compensation for genuine psychiatric harm.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the framework of liability for occupiers of premises under the Occupiers' Liability Act 1995. In your answer, consider the three categories of entrant recognised by the Act — visitors, recreational users, and trespassers — the duty of care owed to each category, and assess whether the 1995 Act has achieved a satisfactory reform of the pre-existing common law.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the duty of care owed by an occupier to a 'visitor' under the Occupiers' Liability Act 1995. In your answer, consider the definition of 'visitor', the common duty of care applicable to visitors under section 3 of the Act, the extent to which this duty may be modified or excluded by agreement, and assess the effectiveness of the visitor protection provided by the Act.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the duty owed by an occupier to a recreational user or trespasser under the Occupiers' Liability Act 1995. In your answer, consider the reduced duty of care imposed by section 4 of the Act, the circumstances in which this duty may be raised to a higher standard, and assess whether the protection afforded to trespassers under the 1995 Act is appropriate — in particular in relation to child trespassers.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Product Liability: Common Law and the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991
questions.text: Explain the common law liability of a manufacturer for defective products in Irish law. In your answer, consider the principles established in Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] and the subsequent development of the neighbour principle as a basis for product liability, and assess the scope and limitations of the common law remedy.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Product Liability: Common Law and the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991
questions.text: Critically examine the strict liability regime for defective products under the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 (which implemented the EU Products Liability Directive). In your answer, consider the definition of 'defect', the categories of producer who are strictly liable, the types of damage recoverable, and the defences available under the Act — in particular the development risk (state of the art) defence.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Product Liability: Common Law and the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991
questions.text: Discuss the practical advantages and limitations of claiming in strict liability under the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 compared with an action in negligence at common law. In your answer, consider the burden of proof, the recoverable damage, the available defences, and the limitation periods under each cause of action, and assess which approach is preferable from a plaintiff's perspective in a typical defective product case.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defamation: Elements of the Tort under the Defamation Act 2009
questions.text: Explain the elements that a plaintiff must establish to succeed in a defamation action under the Defamation Act 2009. In your answer, consider the definition of a defamatory statement under section 2 of the Act, the requirement of publication to a third party, the presumption as to falsity, and the abolition of the distinction between libel and slander.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defamation: Elements of the Tort under the Defamation Act 2009
questions.text: Critically examine the definition of a 'defamatory statement' under section 2 of the Defamation Act 2009. In your answer, consider the objective test for defamation — whether the statement would tend to injure the plaintiff's reputation in the eyes of reasonable members of society — and assess how the courts have applied this test in practice, with particular reference to statements in electronic and social media.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defamation: Elements of the Tort under the Defamation Act 2009
questions.text: Discuss the issue of identification in defamation proceedings. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which a statement that does not name the plaintiff may nonetheless be treated as referring to them — including cases of defamation by innuendo — and assess the extent to which group libel is actionable in Ireland.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defamation: Defences under the Defamation Act 2009
questions.text: Explain the defence of truth (justification) in a defamation action under section 16 of the Defamation Act 2009. In your answer, consider the standard of proof required to establish truth, the effect of partial justification where multiple defamatory meanings are alleged, and assess whether the burden of proof on the defendant to establish truth is appropriate.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defamation: Defences under the Defamation Act 2009
questions.text: Critically examine the defence of honest opinion (formerly 'fair comment') under section 20 of the Defamation Act 2009. In your answer, consider the conditions that must be satisfied for the defence to succeed — including that the statement must be an opinion, based on true facts, and honestly held — and assess whether the Act's reformulation of this defence represents an improvement on the pre-existing common law.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defamation: Defences under the Defamation Act 2009
questions.text: Discuss the defence of absolute and qualified privilege in Irish defamation law. In your answer, consider the categories of statement that attract absolute privilege under the Defamation Act 2009 and at common law, the conditions for qualified privilege, the circumstances in which qualified privilege may be defeated by malice, and assess whether the privilege defences strike an appropriate balance between the protection of reputation and the free flow of information.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defamation: Remedies and Practical Exam Application
questions.text: Explain the range of remedies available to a successful plaintiff in a defamation action under the Defamation Act 2009. In your answer, consider the new remedies introduced by the Act — including a declaratory order, a correction order, and a prohibition order — in addition to general and aggravated damages, and assess whether this expanded range of remedies better serves the interests of defamation plaintiffs.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defamation: Remedies and Practical Exam Application
questions.text: Critically examine the role of the jury in assessing damages in defamation proceedings in Ireland. In your answer, consider the powers of the trial judge to guide the jury on damages, the circumstances in which an appellate court may interfere with a jury's award of damages, and assess whether jury assessment of damages in defamation is consistent with predictability and proportionality.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defamation: Remedies and Practical Exam Application
questions.text: In the context of an FE-1 Tort Law problem question, a regional newspaper publishes an article incorrectly naming the plaintiff as a convicted fraudster. The plaintiff loses his job as a result. Applying the provisions of the Defamation Act 2009, identify all relevant legal issues, state the applicable rules, apply them to the facts, and advise the plaintiff as to his prospects of recovery and the remedies that may be available to him.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the tort of battery in Irish law. In your answer, consider the elements of the tort — including the requirement of a direct and intentional act, the concept of 'hostile' touching, and the absence of consent — and illustrate with relevant case law the kinds of contact that constitute a battery.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the requirement of intention in battery. In your answer, consider whether intention to cause the contact, rather than intention to cause harm, is sufficient, assess the decisions in Letang v Cooper [1965] and Collins v Wilcock [1984], and discuss the implications of the intent requirement for cases involving medical treatment administered without consent.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between the tort of battery and the law of medical consent in Ireland. In your answer, consider whether a medical procedure performed without the patient's informed consent constitutes a battery, the distinction between battery and negligence as causes of action in the medical context, and assess the Irish case law on this issue.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trespass to the Person: Assault and False Imprisonment
questions.text: Explain the torts of assault and false imprisonment in Irish law. In your answer, consider the elements that must be established for each tort, the distinction between civil assault (an apprehension of imminent contact) and battery (the contact itself), and assess the practical importance of each tort in contemporary litigation.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trespass to the Person: Assault and False Imprisonment
questions.text: Critically examine the tort of false imprisonment. In your answer, consider the requirement of total restraint of the plaintiff's liberty, the relevance of the plaintiff's awareness of the constraint, the question of whether imprisonment within an area (partial confinement) is actionable, and the defences available to a defendant.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trespass to the Person: Assault and False Imprisonment
questions.text: Discuss the circumstances in which a private citizen or a Garda officer may lawfully arrest or detain a person without being liable in tort for false imprisonment. In your answer, consider the common law and statutory powers of arrest, the conditions that must be satisfied, and assess the adequacy of the constitutional and common law safeguards against unlawful detention.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trespass to the Person: Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress
questions.text: Explain the tort of intentional infliction of emotional distress (or intentional infliction of nervous shock) in Irish law. In your answer, consider the elements of the tort as formulated in Wilkinson v Downton [1897], the requirement that the defendant's conduct be calculated or designed to cause emotional harm, and assess whether this tort fills a gap left by negligence law.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trespass to the Person: Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress
questions.text: Critically examine the relationship between the tort of intentional infliction of emotional distress and the tort of harassment. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which repeated conduct may constitute tortious harassment at common law or under the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997, and assess the remedies available to a plaintiff who has been subjected to a course of harassing conduct.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Trespass to the Person: Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress
questions.text: Discuss the role of the tort of intentional infliction of emotional distress in the context of workplace bullying in Ireland. In your answer, consider whether an employee who suffers a recognised psychiatric illness as a result of a course of deliberate bullying by colleagues or management may maintain an action in the tort, and assess the interaction between this tort and an employer's duty of care in negligence.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the tort of trespass to land in Irish law. In your answer, consider the elements of the tort — including the requirement of direct physical interference, the extent of a landowner's rights above and below the surface, the actionability of trespass per se (without proof of damage) — and assess the defences available to a defendant.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the remedies available to a plaintiff whose land has been trespassed upon. In your answer, consider the action for ejectment, mesne profits, an injunction to restrain repeated trespass, and the doctrine of self-help, and assess whether the remedies available in Irish law adequately protect the landowner's right to exclusive possession.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the circumstances in which a licensee who has overstayed their permission, or a person whose original entry was lawful but who subsequently refuses to leave, may be liable in trespass. In your answer, consider the effect of revocation of a licence, the notice requirements applicable to different types of licensee, and assess the circumstances in which a landowner may lawfully use reasonable force to eject a trespasser.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the tort of private nuisance in Irish law. In your answer, consider the distinction between physical encroachment, physical damage, and interference with enjoyment, the requirement that the interference be 'unreasonable', the factors relevant to this assessment, and the categories of defendant who may be liable in nuisance.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the 'locality' principle in private nuisance. In your answer, consider the role of the character of the neighbourhood in determining whether an interference with enjoyment constitutes actionable nuisance, the statement in St Helen's Smelting Co v Tipping (1865) that locality is irrelevant to physical damage nuisance claims, and assess whether the locality principle is consistent in its application.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the remedies available to the plaintiff in a private nuisance action. In your answer, consider damages (including whether damages in lieu of injunction are appropriate), the availability of an injunction as a remedy for nuisance, the principles applicable to the grant or refusal of injunctive relief (including the balance of convenience), and assess whether the current approach to nuisance remedies is satisfactory.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Public Nuisance and the Rule in Rylands v Fletcher
questions.text: Explain the distinction between private nuisance and public nuisance in Irish law. In your answer, consider the elements of public nuisance, the requirement that a private plaintiff suffer 'special damage' over and above that suffered by the public at large, and assess the circumstances in which a public nuisance gives rise to a civil cause of action.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Public Nuisance and the Rule in Rylands v Fletcher
questions.text: Explain the rule in Rylands v Fletcher (1868) and critically assess its application in Irish law. In your answer, consider the elements of the rule — the accumulation of a dangerous thing on land, an escape, and non-natural user — and the defences available, and assess whether the rule remains a useful and distinct head of liability in Irish tort law or whether it has been largely subsumed by negligence.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Public Nuisance and the Rule in Rylands v Fletcher
questions.text: Discuss whether the rule in Rylands v Fletcher should be recognised and applied as a distinct head of strict liability in Ireland. In your answer, consider the arguments for and against retaining the rule, its treatment in Cambridge Water Co v Eastern Counties Leather plc [1994], and assess whether Irish law has adopted a coherent position on the future of this form of strict liability.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Strict Liability: Animals and the Animals Act
questions.text: Explain the liability of the keeper of an animal for injury or damage caused by that animal under Irish law. In your answer, consider the distinction between dangerous (ferae naturae) and non-dangerous (mansuetae naturae) species, the 'scienter' action, the reform of animal liability under the Animals Act 1985, and assess whether the current law strikes an appropriate balance between the interests of animal keepers and the public.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Strict Liability: Animals and the Animals Act
questions.text: Critically examine the liability of the owner or keeper of a dog for injury caused by the dog in Ireland under the Control of Dogs Act 1986 and at common law. In your answer, consider the statutory strict liability imposed under the 1986 Act, the defences available under the Act, and assess whether strict liability is an appropriate basis for dog bite claims.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Strict Liability: Animals and the Animals Act
questions.text: Discuss the common law liability of a farmer whose livestock strays onto a public road and causes a road traffic accident. In your answer, consider the traditional rule that a landowner owed no duty to fence land adjacent to the public highway to prevent straying animals, the reform effected by the Animals Act 1985 (if applicable in Ireland), and assess the current position under Irish law.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defences: Contributory Negligence and the Civil Liability Act 1961
questions.text: Explain the defence of contributory negligence under the Civil Liability Act 1961. In your answer, consider the shift from the old common law rule (complete bar to recovery) to the modern apportionment regime, the test for contributory negligence, and the principles the courts apply when apportioning liability between the plaintiff and defendant.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defences: Contributory Negligence and the Civil Liability Act 1961
questions.text: Critically examine the courts' approach to apportioning fault between a negligent defendant and a contributorily negligent plaintiff. In your answer, consider the factors relevant to apportionment — including the causal potency and the degree of blameworthiness of each party's conduct — and assess whether the apportionment exercise is sufficiently principled and predictable.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defences: Contributory Negligence and the Civil Liability Act 1961
questions.text: Discuss the operation of the Civil Liability Act 1961 in cases involving multiple defendants. In your answer, consider the definition of 'concurrent wrongdoers' under the Act, the joint and several liability of concurrent wrongdoers for the full damage, the right of contribution between wrongdoers, and assess whether the Act strikes a fair balance between the interests of plaintiffs and defendants.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defences: Volenti Non Fit Injuria and Limitation of Actions
questions.text: Explain the defence of volenti non fit injuria (consent) in Irish tort law. In your answer, consider what must be established to make out the defence — including genuine consent to the risk of harm, knowledge of the risk, and the requirement that consent be freely given — and assess why this defence is rarely successful in Irish personal injury litigation.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defences: Volenti Non Fit Injuria and Limitation of Actions
questions.text: Critically examine the distinction between voluntary assumption of risk (volenti) and contributory negligence. In your answer, consider how the courts distinguish between a plaintiff who has accepted the risk of harm and one who has merely failed to take reasonable care for their own safety, and assess whether this distinction is satisfactorily drawn in practice.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Defences: Volenti Non Fit Injuria and Limitation of Actions
questions.text: Explain the limitation periods applicable to tort claims under the Statute of Limitations 1957 (as amended). In your answer, consider the general limitation period for personal injury actions, the 'date of knowledge' rule applicable to latent injury cases, the special rules for minors and persons under disability, and assess whether the current limitation framework adequately balances the interests of plaintiffs against the legitimate expectations of defendants.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the heads of general damages recoverable in a personal injury action in Ireland. In your answer, consider the assessment of damages for pain and suffering (past and future), loss of amenity, and the 'book of quantum' published by the Judicial Council under the Judicial Council Act 2019, and assess the impact of the Book of Quantum on the assessment of personal injury damages.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the rules governing the assessment of damages for loss of earnings in a personal injury action. In your answer, consider the multiplier/multiplicand approach, the discount rate applicable to future pecuniary losses, the deductions required under the Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 1964 for social welfare and collateral benefits, and assess whether the current method of assessment produces fair outcomes for seriously injured plaintiffs.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the principles governing aggravated and exemplary (punitive) damages in Irish tort law. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which such damages may be awarded, the basis for each type of enhanced damages, and assess whether the award of punitive damages in tort serves a legitimate function or whether it conflicts with the compensatory principle.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the principles governing the grant of an interlocutory injunction in Irish tort litigation. In your answer, consider the test established in Campus Oil Ltd v Minister for Industry and Energy [1983], the 'balance of convenience' assessment, and the role of the undertaking in damages, and assess whether the Campus Oil test is appropriate for all categories of interlocutory injunction application.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the circumstances in which a court will award damages in lieu of an injunction in a nuisance or trespass case. In your answer, consider the Shelfer v City of London Electric Lighting Co [1895] criteria as applied in Irish courts, and assess whether the preference for injunctive relief over damages is appropriate in cases involving ongoing interference with property rights.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the availability of quia timet injunctions and mandatory injunctions in Irish tort law. In your answer, consider the circumstances in which a court will grant a quia timet injunction to restrain a threatened wrong, the higher threshold for mandatory injunctions, and assess whether the remedial framework in Irish tort law is sufficiently flexible to provide effective relief in all circumstances.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fatal Injuries and Survival of Actions: The Civil Liability Act 1961
questions.text: Explain the survival of causes of action under Part III of the Civil Liability Act 1961. In your answer, consider which causes of action survive the death of the plaintiff (or defendant), what heads of damage are recoverable by or against the deceased's estate, and assess whether the survival regime under the 1961 Act achieves a satisfactory outcome for the dependants of deceased victims.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fatal Injuries and Survival of Actions: The Civil Liability Act 1961
questions.text: Critically examine the statutory cause of action for dependency loss under Part IV of the Civil Liability Act 1961. In your answer, consider who qualifies as a 'dependant', the types of loss recoverable under the statutory action, the assessment of damages for future dependency loss, and assess whether the statutory regime adequately compensates the dependants of a person who is killed by a tortfeasor.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Fatal Injuries and Survival of Actions: The Civil Liability Act 1961
questions.text: Discuss the interaction between the survival of action under Part III and the statutory dependency action under Part IV of the Civil Liability Act 1961. In your answer, consider the risk of double recovery, the deductions applicable under each head of claim, and assess whether the current framework for compensating the families of fatal accident victims is coherent and fair.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the concept of 'concurrent wrongdoers' under the Civil Liability Act 1961. In your answer, consider the definition of concurrent wrongdoers in section 11 of the Act, the principle of joint and several liability for the full damage, and the rules governing contribution between concurrent wrongdoers under Part III of the Act.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the principle of joint and several liability of concurrent wrongdoers under the Civil Liability Act 1961. In your answer, consider the arguments for and against imposing full liability on each concurrent wrongdoer, the reform of joint and several liability in other jurisdictions, and assess whether the current Irish position — which retains full joint and several liability — is appropriate in multi-defendant personal injury litigation.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the right of contribution between concurrent wrongdoers under section 21 of the Civil Liability Act 1961. In your answer, consider the principles governing the apportionment of contribution, the time limits applicable to contribution claims, and assess whether the contribution regime adequately protects a defendant who settles a claim and subsequently seeks contribution from another concurrent wrongdoer.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Vicarious Liability: Employers and Independent Contractors
questions.text: Explain the doctrine of vicarious liability in Irish tort law. In your answer, consider the two-stage test for vicarious liability — the relationship between the defendant and the tortfeasor, and the connection between the relationship and the tortfeasor's wrongdoing — and assess the rationale for imposing liability on a party who is not themselves at fault.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Vicarious Liability: Employers and Independent Contractors
questions.text: Critically examine the distinction between an employee and an independent contractor for the purposes of vicarious liability in Irish law. In your answer, consider the tests developed by the courts to determine the nature of the working relationship (including the control test, the integration test, and the multiple indicia test), and assess whether the traditional distinction remains appropriate given the increasing prevalence of non-standard working arrangements.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.lessonTitle: Vicarious Liability: Employers and Independent Contractors
questions.text: Discuss the 'close connection' test for establishing the necessary link between an employee's role and their tort. In your answer, consider the decision in Lister v Hesley Hall Ltd [2001] and its application in Ireland — particularly in cases involving abuse of positions of trust and authority — and assess whether the close connection test has unduly expanded the scope of vicarious liability.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Explain the principles governing the standard of care in medical negligence actions in Ireland as established in Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989]. In your answer, set out all six Dunne principles and assess the extent to which these principles have been applied and modified by subsequent Irish case law, particularly in light of the decision in Geoghegan v Harris [2000].

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Critically examine the principle that a medical practitioner is not negligent merely because they adopt a practice that a body of professional opinion would not adopt, provided a reasonable body of medical opinion supports the practice. In your answer, consider whether Irish law — following Dunne — applies a 'Bolam-type' test in medical negligence and assess whether such deference to medical opinion adequately protects the interests of patients.

questions.subject: Tort Law
questions.text: Discuss the duty of a medical practitioner to disclose material risks to a patient before obtaining consent to treatment. In your answer, consider the evolution of the Irish law of informed consent from Dunne to Fitzpatrick v White [2007] and subsequent Irish and UK authority, and assess whether the current Irish standard of disclosure (focused on what a reasonable patient would want to know) provides an appropriate level of protection for patient autonomy.
